> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NMOSFET Amplifiers: MOSFET Circuits at DC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOSFET DC Biasing (Q-Point Establishment):** The process of applying external DC voltages to the terminals of a MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor) so as to set up a stable **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** ŌĆö defined by the DC coordinates $(V_{DSQ}, I_{DQ})$ ŌĆö in the saturation region of the output characteristics. The Q-point ensures the device operates as a linear amplifier for small AC signals superimposed on the DC levels, without distortion or cutoff.

For an **NMOS Enhancement MOSFET**, the fundamental DC operating conditions are:

$$V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)} \quad \text{(to turn ON the channel)}$$

$$V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \quad \text{(to operate in saturation / pinch-off region)}$$

The drain current in saturation is governed by Shockley's square-law equation:

$$I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

where $K_n = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$ is the **conduction parameter** (in **A/V┬▓**), and $\lambda$ is the channel-length modulation parameter (in **VŌü╗┬╣**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Water-Tap & Pressure Regulator"

Imagine the **Gate (G)** of the MOSFET as the **handle of a water tap**, the **Source (S)** as the water inlet, and the **Drain (D)** as the outlet pipe.

| MOSFET Terminal | Water-Tap Analogy | Role |
|-----------------|-------------------|------|
| Gate (G) | Tap handle | Controls how much channel is "open" |
| Source (S) | Water tank | Provides charge carriers (electrons for NMOS) |
| Drain (D) | Discharge pipe | Where current flows out |
| $V_{GS}$ | How far you turn the handle | Controls drain current $I_D$ |
| $V_{DS}$ | Downstream back-pressure | Determines saturation/non-saturation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** The threshold voltage $V_{GS(th)}$ is like the **"stiffness"** of the tap handle ŌĆö you must apply at least that much force (voltage) before any water (current) starts flowing. Once flowing, the drain current grows as the **square** of how far you turn the handle beyond the threshold ŌĆö hence the "square-law" name.

---

## 1.3 Why DC Biasing is Indispensable

An unbiased MOSFET sits at $I_D = 0$. To use it as an **amplifier**:

1. The Q-point must be located in the **saturation region** (constant-current region).
2. The Q-point must provide enough **swing room** for the AC signal in both directions (positive and negative half-cycles) without entering cutoff or triode.
3. The Q-point should be **stable** against temperature variations, device parameter spread, and supply voltage fluctuations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Module 1 Core Outcome (CO1):** *Analyze MOSFET DC biasing circuits and determine the Q-point stability using graphical and analytical methods.*

---

## 1.4 Biasing Requirements ŌĆö A Quick Snapshot

| Requirement | Symbol | Typical Target |
|-------------|--------|----------------|
| Gate-to-Source voltage | $V_{GS}$ | $\approx \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ for max AC swing |
| Drain current | $I_{DQ}$ | $\approx \dfrac{I_{DSS,eq}}{2}$ |
| Drain-to-Source voltage | $V_{DSQ}$ | $\approx \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ |
| Stability against $V_{GS(th)}$ variation | ŌĆö | Independent / minimally dependent |

Where $I_{DSS,eq}$ denotes an "equivalent" maximum drain current parameter used in JFET-style modeling.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference)

- **Electron mobility (Si):** $\mu_n \approx 1350 \;\text{cm}^2/\text{V┬Ęs}$
- **Hole mobility (Si):** $\mu_p \approx 480 \;\text{cm}^2/\text{V┬Ęs}$
- **Oxide capacitance per unit area:** $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$, where $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ **F/cm**
- **Threshold voltage range:** $V_{GS(th)} \approx 0.5$ to **3 V** (typical for enhancement NMOS)
- **Process transconductance:** $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$ in **┬ĄA/V┬▓**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for a Stable Q-Point

The MOSFET is a **square-law device**, meaning a small change in $V_{GS}$ produces a large, nonlinear change in $I_D$. Without proper biasing:

- The device may operate in the **triode region** (acts like a variable resistor, not amplifier).
- It may enter **cutoff** during one half-cycle (signal clipping).
- It may be sensitive to **temperature** ŌĆö $V_{GS(th)}$ typically shifts by $\mathbf{-2\;mV/^{\circ}C}$.

A stable bias network ensures the Q-point remains in saturation regardless of:
1. Device-to-device parameter variations (manufacturing spread).
2. Temperature drift.
3. Power supply variations.

---

## 2.2 Four Standard NMOS DC Biasing Configurations

### (A) Fixed Bias (Gate Bias) Configuration

**Topology:** A single DC source $V_{GG}$ drives the gate directly; the source is grounded; the drain connects to $V_{DD}$ via resistor $R_D$.

**Why this exists:** Simplest possible biasing ŌĆö one resistor at drain, one DC source at gate.

**Operating equations:**

$$V_{GS} = V_{GG}$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n (V_{GG} - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Drawback:** $I_{DQ}$ is **highly sensitive** to $V_{GS(th)}$ variations because the square-law amplification magnifies small changes. This circuit has **poor bias stability** and is rarely used in practice for discrete amplifiers, but is common in IC design where matched devices are available.

---

### (B) Self-Bias (Source Bias) Configuration

**Topology:** Gate is tied to ground through $R_G$ (very high value, often **1ŌĆō10 M╬®**); source has resistor $R_S$; drain has $R_D$.

**Why this exists:** The gate draws **zero DC current** (MOSFET input is insulated), so no DC voltage drops across $R_G$. The source resistor $R_S$ develops a voltage that **automatically opposes** any change in $I_D$ ŌĆö providing **negative DC feedback**.

**Operating equations:**

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = 0 - I_{DQ} R_S = -I_{DQ} R_S$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2 = K_n (-I_{DQ} R_S - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

This is a **quadratic in $I_{DQ}$** ŌĆö solved either by substitution or by taking the square root:

$$\sqrt{I_{DQ}} = \sqrt{K_n} \, (I_{DQ} R_S + V_{GS(th)})$$

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{DQ}}{K_n}} - I_{DQ} R_S = V_{GS(th)}$$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is self-bias self-stabilizing?** If $I_D$ tries to increase (e.g., due to temperature rise reducing $V_{GS(th)}$), then $V_S = I_D R_S$ rises, making $V_{GS}$ more negative, which in turn reduces $I_D$ ŌĆö counteracting the original disturbance. This is **DC negative feedback**.

---

### (C) Voltage-Divider Bias (Potential-Divider Bias) Configuration

**Topology:** Two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a voltage divider at the gate; source has $R_S$; drain has $R_D$.

**Why this exists:** Provides a **fixed, predictable** gate DC voltage $V_G$ independent of drain current (assuming $I_G \approx 0$).

**Operating equations (assuming $I_G \approx 0$):**

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_S = I_{DQ} R_S$$

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_G - I_{DQ} R_S$$

$$\Rightarrow I_{DQ} = K_n (V_G - I_{DQ} R_S - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

This is the **most widely used discrete MOSFET biasing circuit** because it combines good stability with predictable operating point.

---

### (D) Source-Bias with Drain Feedback (Bias with $R_G$ to Drain)

**Topology:** $R_G$ connects gate to **drain** instead of ground. $V_{GS}$ becomes a function of $V_{DS}$.

**Operating equations:**

$$V_{GS} = V_{DS} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(if } R_G \text{ replaces } R_2 \text{ in divider notation)}$$

Or equivalently, for a generic feedback resistor $R_G$ from drain to gate with $R_1$ from gate to ground:

$$V_G = V_{DS} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_G}$$

$$V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S = V_{DS}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_G} - I_D R_S$$

Combined with the device equation $I_D = K_n(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ and $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$, a system of three equations in three unknowns results.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS ŌĆö MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_D = K_n(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | Drain current (saturation, with $\lambda$) | A |
| 2 | $I_D \approx K_n(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$ | Drain current (saturation, $\lambda = 0$) | A |
| 3 | $g_m = 2 K_n (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}) = 2\sqrt{K_n I_D}$ | Transconductance | S (A/V) |
| 4 | $g_{m0} = 2 K_n V_{GS(th)}$ | Transconductance at $V_{GS} = 2V_{GS(th)}$ | S |
| 5 | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Output resistance | ╬® |
| 6 | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$ | Saturation (overdrive) voltage | V |
| 7 | $V_G = V_{DD} \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Gate voltage (voltage divider) | V |
| 8 | $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$ | Gate-to-source voltage | V |
| 9 | $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D(R_D + R_S)$ | Drain-to-source voltage | V |
| 10 | $\mu = g_m r_o$ | Voltage amplification factor | dimensionless |
| 11 | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$ | Overdrive voltage (also $V_{DS,sat}$) | V |
| 12 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta I_D / I_D}{\Delta V_{GS(th)} / V_{GS(th)}}$ | Stability factor (lower = better) | ŌĆö |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS Inverters (Digital):** Q-point in digital CMOS is the switching threshold; DC analysis defines noise margins.
- **Audio Amplifiers:** Voltage-divider-biased MOSFET input stages provide high input impedance (Ōēź **10┬╣┬▓ ╬®**) ŌĆö essential for guitar pickups, condenser microphones.
- **RF Front-Ends:** Stable DC bias of LNAs (Low-Noise Amplifiers) determines gain, NF (noise figure), and linearity (IIP3).
- **Operational Transconductance Amplifiers (OTAs):** $g_m$ is set by a DC bias current and is the core "transconductance" knob of the OTA.
- **Sensor Interface Circuits:** MOSFET source-follower biasing gives ultra-high input impedance for pH, ion-selective, and biosensor readout.

---

## 2.5 Bias Stability ŌĆö Sensitivity to $V_{GS(th)}$ Variation

The **stability factor** $S$ measures percentage change in $I_D$ for a percentage change in $V_{GS(th)}$:

$$S_{V_{th}} = \dfrac{\partial I_D / I_D}{\partial V_{GS(th)} / V_{GS(th)}}$$

For self-bias and voltage-divider bias, $S$ is significantly reduced compared to fixed bias because of the **DC negative feedback** through $R_S$.

| Configuration | $S_{V_{th}}$ (qualitative) | Practical Use |
|---------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Fixed bias | **Highest** (worst) | Rare |
| Self-bias | Moderate | Common in JFET circuits |
| Voltage-divider bias | **Lowest** (best) | Industry standard |
| Drain-feedback | Low | Compact designs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Self-Bias Q-Point Equation

**Given Circuit:** NMOS in self-bias. Gate connected to ground via $R_G$. Source resistor $R_S$. Drain resistor $R_D$. Supply $V_{DD}$.

**Goal:** Derive the explicit quadratic equation for $I_{DQ}$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the DC voltages

Because the MOSFET gate draws **zero DC current** (insulated gate), there is **no voltage drop across $R_G$**:

$$V_G = 0 \;\text{V}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply KVL on the gate-to-source loop

The source is at a higher potential than the gate (since $I_D$ flows from D to S, the source resistor drops a positive voltage with respect to ground):

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = 0 - I_D R_S = -I_D R_S$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into Shockley's equation

For an enhancement NMOS in saturation (ignoring $\lambda$):

$$I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

$$I_D = K_n (-I_D R_S - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Take the square root of both sides

Since $I_D \geq 0$, we use the positive root:

$$\sqrt{I_D} = \sqrt{K_n} \, (I_D R_S + V_{GS(th)})$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange into a quadratic in $\sqrt{I_D}$

Let $x = \sqrt{I_D}$. Then $I_D = x^2$:

$$x = \sqrt{K_n}(x^2 R_S + V_{GS(th)})$$

$$\sqrt{K_n} R_S \, x^2 - x + \sqrt{K_n} V_{GS(th)} = 0$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Solve the quadratic using the standard formula

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4 K_n R_S^2 V_{GS(th)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_S}}}{2 \sqrt{K_n} R_S} \\
  &= \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4 K_n R_S V_{GS(th)}}}{2 \sqrt{K_n} R_S}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Final closed-form for $I_{DQ}$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{DQ} = x^2 = \left[ \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4 K_n R_S V_{GS(th)}}}{2 \sqrt{K_n} R_S} \right]^2
\end{aligned}$$

The **physical (positive)** root is selected such that $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ lies between $V_{GS(th)}$ and $0$ (or below $V_{GS(th)}$ ŌĆö for depletion NMOS).

### Step 8 ŌĆö Verify by sanity check (numerical example)

Take $K_n = 0.5\;\text{mA/V}^2$, $V_{GS(th)} = 2\;\text{V}$, $R_S = 1\;\text{k}\Omega$.

Compute discriminant:

$$1 - 4 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^3 \times 2 = 1 - 4 = -3 \;\;< 0$$

**No real solution!** This means with these values the MOSFET cannot turn on with self-bias alone. We would need $4 K_n R_S V_{GS(th)} < 1$, i.e., $R_S < 1/(4 K_n V_{GS(th)}) = 250\;\Omega$.

For $R_S = 200\;\Omega$:

$$1 - 4 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \times 2 = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$$

$$\sqrt{0.2} \approx 0.447$$

$$x = \dfrac{1 - 0.447}{2 \times \sqrt{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 200} = \dfrac{0.553}{0.2828} \approx 1.955\;\text{mA}^{1/2}$$

$$I_{DQ} = x^2 \approx 3.82\;\text{mA}$$

$$V_{GS} = -I_{DQ} R_S = -3.82 \times 0.2 = -0.764\;\text{V}$$

**Check via Shockley:** $K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = 0.5 \times (-0.764 - 2)^2 = 0.5 \times 7.636 = 3.82\;\text{mA}$ Ō£ō

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Voltage-Divider Bias Q-Point Equation

**Given:** Standard voltage-divider biased NMOS.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the gate voltage (Thevenin equivalent)

Thevenin voltage seen at the gate:

$$V_{TH} = V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Thevenin resistance at the gate:

$$R_{TH} = R_1 \Vert R_2 = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Since gate current is **zero**, $R_{TH}$ does **not** enter the DC equations (but matters for AC input impedance).

### Step 2 ŌĆö KVL on the gate-to-source loop

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_{TH} - I_D R_S$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into Shockley

$$I_D = K_n (V_{TH} - I_D R_S - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Take square root

$$\sqrt{I_D} = \sqrt{K_n}(V_{TH} - I_D R_S - V_{GS(th)})$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange to quadratic

$$\sqrt{K_n} R_S \, I_D + \sqrt{I_D} - \sqrt{K_n}(V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)}) = 0$$

Let $u = \sqrt{I_D}$, then $I_D = u^2$:

$$\sqrt{K_n} R_S \, u^2 + u - \sqrt{K_n}(V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)}) = 0$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Solve quadratic in $u$

$$\begin{aligned}
u &= \dfrac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 K_n R_S^2 \cdot (V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)}) / R_S}}{2 \sqrt{K_n} R_S} \\
  &= \dfrac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 K_n R_S (V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)})}}{2 \sqrt{K_n} R_S}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The '+' root is taken (since $u > 0$).

### Step 7 ŌĆö Compute $V_{DSQ}$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Numerical example for verification

Given: $V_{DD} = 12\;\text{V}$, $R_1 = 1\;\text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 500\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 2\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\;\text{k}\Omega$, $K_n = 0.5\;\text{mA/V}^2$, $V_{GS(th)} = 2\;\text{V}$.

$$V_{TH} = 12 \times \dfrac{500}{1500} = 4\;\text{V}$$

$$V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)} = 2\;\text{V}$$

Discriminant:

$$1 + 4 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^3 \times 2 = 1 + 4 = 5$$

$$\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$u = \dfrac{-1 + 2.236}{2 \times \sqrt{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 10^3} = \dfrac{1.236}{1.414} \approx 0.874\;\text{mA}^{1/2}$$

$$I_{DQ} = u^2 \approx 0.764\;\text{mA}$$

$$V_{GS} = 4 - 0.764 \times 1 = 3.236\;\text{V}$$

**Verification:** $K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = 0.5 \times (3.236 - 2)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.527 = 0.764\;\text{mA}$ Ō£ō

$$V_{DSQ} = 12 - 0.764 \times (2 + 1) = 12 - 2.292 = 9.708\;\text{V}$$

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} = 1.236\;\text{V}$$

Since $V_{DSQ} = 9.708 > 1.236$, the device is in **saturation** Ō£ō

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Bias Stability Factor $S_{V_{th}}$

**Goal:** Show that adding source resistance $R_S$ reduces sensitivity of $I_D$ to $V_{GS(th)}$ variations.

**Starting from:** $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{GS(th)}$ while treating $V_{GS}$ as the independent variable and assuming $V_G$ is fixed:

$$\dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS(th)}} = 2 K_n (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}) \cdot \left(-1 - \dfrac{\partial V_S}{\partial V_{GS(th)}}\right) / ?$$

Let us use a cleaner approach. Take natural log:

$$\ln I_D = \ln K_n + 2 \ln(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})$$

Differentiate with respect to $V_{GS(th)}$, holding $V_{GS}$ fixed momentarily:

$$\dfrac{1}{I_D}\dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS(th)}} = -\dfrac{2}{V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}}$$

But $V_{GS}$ itself depends on $I_D$ via $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$ (in self/divider bias), so we must chain-rule through $I_D$:

$$\dfrac{d I_D}{d V_{GS(th)}} = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS(th)}} + \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \cdot \dfrac{d V_{GS}}{d I_D} \cdot \dfrac{d I_D}{d V_{GS(th)}}$$

Solving the resulting implicit equation gives the stability factor:

$$S_{V_{th}} = \dfrac{\partial \ln I_D}{\partial \ln V_{GS(th)}} = \dfrac{V_{GS(th)}}{V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + 2 K_n R_S (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})}$$

### Key Insight:

For **fixed bias** ($R_S = 0$):

$$S_{V_{th}}^{\text{fixed}} = \dfrac{V_{GS(th)}}{V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}}$$

For **voltage-divider bias** with non-zero $R_S$:

$$S_{V_{th}}^{\text{divider}} = \dfrac{S_{V_{th}}^{\text{fixed}}}{1 + 2 K_n R_S (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})} = \dfrac{S_{V_{th}}^{\text{fixed}}}{1 + 2 \sqrt{K_n I_D} \cdot R_S}$$

The denominator $(1 + g_m R_S)$ is the **DC negative feedback gain** ŌĆö making $S$ smaller by this factor.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Pros and Cons of Each Biasing Method

| Configuration | Components Count | Bias Stability | Q-Point Predictability | AC Input Impedance | Typical Use |
|---------------|------------------|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| Fixed bias | 2 ($R_D$, $V_{GG}$) | **Poor** | Moderate | Infinite (no $R_G$) | IC design |
| Self-bias | 3 ($R_D$, $R_S$, $R_G$) | Good | Depends on device | $R_G$ (M╬®) | JFET, depletion MOSFET |
| Voltage-divider | 4 ($R_D$, $R_S$, $R_1$, $R_2$) | **Excellent** | Highly predictable | $R_1 \Vert R_2$ | Discrete amplifiers |
| Drain-feedback | 3 ($R_D$, $R_S$, $R_G$) | Good | Depends on $V_{DS}$ | $R_G$ | Compact designs |
| Constant-current (active bias) | 4+ | **Best** | Set by reference | Depends | IC, high-performance |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Complete MOSFET Amplifier Stage

The following diagram maps the functional interactions of a generic common-source NMOS amplifier biased by a voltage-divider network. Note that, as per KTU 2024 visualization guidelines, the diagram represents the **functional architecture flow** rather than physical schematic placement, because true schematic drawing of physical circuits (with ground symbols, decoupling capacitors, etc.) cannot be rendered in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC1["V_DD Supply"] --> RD1["R_D Resistor"]
    DC1 --> R1top["R_1 Resistor"]
    DC1 --> R2bot["R_2 Resistor"]
    R1top --> GATE1["GATE Node"]
    R2bot --> GNDnode["GROUND"]
    RD1 --> DRAINnode["DRAIN Node"]
    GNDnode --> RS1["R_S Resistor"]
    RS1 --> SRCnode["SOURCE Node"]
    DRAINnode --> NFET1["NMOS Device"]
    GATE1 --> NFET1
    SRCnode --> NFET1
    ACin["AC Input Signal v_in"] --> CIN1["Coupling Capacitor C_in"]
    CIN1 --> GATE1
    DRAINnode --> COUT1["Coupling Capacitor C_out"]
    COUT1 --> ACout["AC Output v_out"]
    NFET1 --> GDSC["g_m v_gs Current Source"]
    GDSC --> RO1["r_o Output Resistance"]
    RO1 --> DRAINnode
    NFET1 --> RS1
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
- The **left branch** (R_1, R_2) sets the gate DC voltage $V_G$.
- The **right branch** (R_D) sets the drain DC voltage via $V_{DD} - I_D R_D$.
- The **bottom branch** (R_S) provides DC negative feedback for stability.
- The **AC signal path** is via the coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ (which block DC but pass AC).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] Define the Q-point of a MOSFET and state the two conditions for the device to operate in the saturation region as an amplifier.

**Model Answer:**

The **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** of a MOSFET is the DC operating point ŌĆö the pair $(I_{DQ}, V_{DSQ})$ ŌĆö at which the device rests when no AC signal is applied. For the device to function as a **linear amplifier** in the **saturation region**, two conditions must hold:

1. **Device must be ON (channel induced):**
$$V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$$

2. **Pinch-off (saturation) condition:**
$$V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$$

Under these conditions, the drain current follows Shockley's square-law: $I_D = K_n(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$, and the device behaves as a **voltage-controlled current source** for small AC signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Many students write the saturation condition with a strict ">" instead of "Ōēź". The "Ōēź" is preferred because $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$ is the **boundary** (edge of saturation / triode).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] Why is the fixed-bias configuration rarely used in discrete MOSFET amplifier design?

**Model Answer:**

In the **fixed-bias** configuration, the gate is connected directly to a fixed DC voltage source $V_{GG}$, and $V_{GS}$ is therefore **independent of $I_D$.** This makes the Q-point highly sensitive to variations in the threshold voltage $V_{GS(th)}$.

Because the drain current follows $I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GG} - V_{GS(th)})^2$, even a small change in $V_{GS(th)}$ (due to **manufacturing tolerance**, **temperature drift** of $\mathbf{-2\;mV/^{\circ}C}$, or **aging**) produces a **large, squared change** in $I_{DQ}$. The resulting bias stability factor is:

$$S_{V_{th}} = \dfrac{V_{GS(th)}}{V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}}$$

which can be very large (e.g., $S > 5$ in typical designs). Hence, fixed bias is **rarely used** in discrete designs. It is acceptable in **integrated circuits** where matched devices share the same $V_{GS(th)}$ variations and the bias is generated by current mirrors.

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö Voltage-Divider Bias Q-Point Design

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the drain current $I_{DQ}$ of an enhancement NMOS transistor biased by a voltage-divider network, in terms of $V_{TH}$, $V_{GS(th)}$, $R_S$, and $K_n$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the circuit shown, determine $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, and $V_{DSQ}$, and verify that the transistor operates in saturation.

**Given data:**
- $V_{DD} = 15\;\text{V}$
- $R_1 = 2\;\text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\;\text{M}\Omega$
- $R_D = 4\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 2\;\text{k}\Omega$
- $V_{GS(th)} = 2\;\text{V}$, $K_n = 0.25\;\text{mA/V}^2$
- Assume $\lambda = 0$ **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation

**Step 1:** Gate voltage from unloaded voltage divider (since $I_G = 0$):

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \equiv V_{TH}$$

**Step 2:** KVL on the gate-source-source loop:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_{TH} - I_D R_S$$

**Step 3:** Shockley's saturation equation (with $\lambda = 0$):

$$I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2 = K_n (V_{TH} - I_D R_S - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

**Step 4:** Take the positive square root (since $I_D \geq 0$):

$$\sqrt{I_D} = \sqrt{K_n}\,(V_{TH} - I_D R_S - V_{GS(th)})$$

**Step 5:** Let $u = \sqrt{I_D}$, so $I_D = u^2$. Rearranging:

$$\sqrt{K_n}\,R_S\,u^2 + u - \sqrt{K_n}\,(V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)}) = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve the quadratic using the formula $u = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = \sqrt{K_n} R_S$, $b = 1$, $c = -\sqrt{K_n}(V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)})$:

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} = \left[ \dfrac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 K_n R_S (V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)})}}{2 \sqrt{K_n}\,R_S} \right]^2}$$

This is the final closed-form result. **(7 marks ŌĆö derivation complete)**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation

**Step 1:** Compute the Thevenin gate voltage:

$$V_{TH} = 15 \times \dfrac{1\;\text{M}\Omega}{2\;\text{M}\Omega + 1\;\text{M}\Omega} = 15 \times \dfrac{1}{3} = 5\;\text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)}$:

$$V_{TH} - V_{GS(th)} = 5 - 2 = 3\;\text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the quadratic:

$$a = \sqrt{0.25 \times 10^{-3}} \times 2000 = 0.5 \times 10^{-1.5} \times 2 \times 10^3$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$\sqrt{K_n} = \sqrt{0.25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-1.5} = 0.01581\;\text{(mA)}^{1/2}/\text{V}$

$\sqrt{K_n} \cdot R_S = 0.01581 \times 2000 = 31.62\;\text{V}^{-1}$

Discriminant:

$$1 + 4 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 2000 \times 3 = 1 + 6 = 7$$

$$\sqrt{7} \approx 2.6458$$

**Step 4:** Compute $u$:

$$u = \dfrac{-1 + 2.6458}{2 \times 31.62} = \dfrac{1.6458}{63.24} = 0.02602\;\text{mA}^{1/2}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $I_{DQ}$:

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} = u^2 = (0.02602)^2 \approx 0.677\;\text{mA}}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $V_{GSQ}$:

$$V_{GSQ} = V_{TH} - I_{DQ} R_S = 5 - 0.677 \times 2 = 5 - 1.354 = \boxed{3.646\;\text{V}}$$

**Step 7:** Compute $V_{DSQ}$:

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) = 15 - 0.677 \times (4 + 2) = 15 - 4.062 = \boxed{10.94\;\text{V}}$$

**Step 8:** Verify saturation:

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GSQ} - V_{GS(th)} = 3.646 - 2 = 1.646\;\text{V}$$

Since $V_{DSQ} = 10.94\;\text{V} > V_{DS,sat} = 1.646\;\text{V}$, the device is in **saturation** Ō£ō **(7 marks ŌĆö numerical solution complete)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) ŌĆö Self-Bias and Stability Analysis

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** For a self-biased enhancement NMOS (with $R_G$ from gate to ground), derive the equation for $I_{DQ}$ in closed form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given: $V_{DD} = 20\;\text{V}$, $R_D = 3\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1.5\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1\;\text{M}\Omega$, $K_n = 0.4\;\text{mA/V}^2$, $V_{GS(th)} = 1.5\;\text{V}$. Determine $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, and $V_{DSQ}$. Calculate the stability factor $S_{V_{th}}$ and compare with fixed bias. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Self-Bias Derivation

**Step 1:** With gate connected to ground via $R_G$ and $I_G = 0$:

$$V_G = 0$$

**Step 2:** Source voltage:

$$V_S = I_D R_S$$

**Step 3:** Gate-source voltage:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = -I_D R_S$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into Shockley's equation:

$$I_D = K_n(-I_D R_S - V_{GS(th)})^2 = K_n(I_D R_S + V_{GS(th)})^2$$

**Step 5:** Take square root:

$$\sqrt{I_D} = \sqrt{K_n}(I_D R_S + V_{GS(th)})$$

**Step 6:** Let $u = \sqrt{I_D}$, $I_D = u^2$:

$$\sqrt{K_n} R_S u^2 - u + \sqrt{K_n} V_{GS(th)} = 0$$

**Step 7:** Quadratic formula:

$$u = \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4 K_n R_S V_{GS(th)}}}{2\sqrt{K_n} R_S}$$

**Step 8:** Final closed form:

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} = \left[ \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4 K_n R_S V_{GS(th)}}}{2\sqrt{K_n} R_S} \right]^2}$$

Note: For an **enhancement NMOS** in self-bias, we typically need $R_S < \dfrac{1}{4 K_n V_{GS(th)}}$ for a real solution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution

**Step 1:** Compute discriminant:

$$\Delta = 1 - 4 K_n R_S V_{GS(th)} = 1 - 4 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 \times 1.5$$

$$\Delta = 1 - 4 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 2250 = 1 - 3.6 = -2.6$$

**Discriminant is negative ŌĆö no real solution!**

> [!WARNING]
> **This means the circuit as given cannot bias the transistor in saturation.** The enhancement NMOS with self-bias alone cannot turn on because $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S \leq 0 < V_{GS(th)}$. We need either (i) a smaller $R_S$ such that $4 K_n R_S V_{GS(th)} < 1$, i.e., $R_S < 1/(4 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5) = 416.67\;\Omega$, OR (ii) a positive gate bias (e.g., through $R_G$ connected to $V_{DD}$ or via a divider).

**Corrected problem:** Let us redo with $R_S = 400\;\Omega$:

$$\Delta = 1 - 4 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 400 \times 1.5 = 1 - 0.96 = 0.04$$

$$\sqrt{\Delta} = 0.2$$

$\sqrt{K_n} = \sqrt{0.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0200\;\text{mA}^{1/2}/\text{V}$

$\sqrt{K_n} R_S = 0.0200 \times 400 = 8.0\;\text{V}^{-1}$

$$u = \dfrac{1 - 0.2}{2 \times 8.0} = \dfrac{0.8}{16} = 0.05\;\text{mA}^{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} = (0.05)^2 = 2.5\;\text{mA}}$$

$$V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S = -2.5 \times 0.4 = -1.0\;\text{V}$$

**Verify:** $K_n(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2 = 0.4 \times (-1 - 1.5)^2 = 0.4 \times 6.25 = 2.5\;\text{mA}$ Ō£ō

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) = 20 - 2.5 \times (3 + 0.4) = 20 - 8.5 = \boxed{11.5\;\text{V}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute stability factor for self-bias:

$$g_m = 2\sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}} = 2\sqrt{0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2\sqrt{10^{-6}} = 2\;\text{mA/V}$$

$$S_{V_{th}}^{\text{self}} = \dfrac{V_{GS(th)}/V_{OV}}{1 + g_m R_S} = \dfrac{1.5/(-1 - 1.5)}{1 + 0.002 \times 400}$$

Hmm ŌĆö for enhancement MOSFET in self-bias, $V_{GS} < V_{GS(th)}$ initially is required to turn on. The stability factor formula derivation must be applied carefully.

Alternative form:

$$S_{V_{th}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + g_m R_S} \cdot \dfrac{V_{GS(th)}}{V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}}$$

For depletion MOSFET in self-bias with $V_{GS} < 0$ and $|V_{GS}| > V_{GS(th)}^{\text{(dep)}}$:

$V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}^{\text{(dep)}} = -1 - 1.5 = -2.5\;\text{V}$ (magnitude 2.5)

$$S_{V_{th}}^{\text{self}} = \dfrac{1.5/2.5}{1 + 0.002 \times 400} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + 0.8} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.8} \approx 0.333$$

**For fixed bias with the same parameters** ($V_{GG}$ chosen so that $V_{GSQ} = -1\;\text{V}$):

$$S_{V_{th}}^{\text{fixed}} = \dfrac{V_{GS(th)}}{V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}} = \dfrac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.6$$

**Comparison:** Self-bias reduces $S$ by a factor of $1 + g_m R_S = 1.8$, confirming that **self-bias is more stable than fixed bias** by the factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$. **(7 marks ŌĆö solution complete)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (KTU ESE Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $V_{GS}$:** Many students write $V_{GS} = +I_D R_S$ instead of $-I_D R_S$ for self-bias. Remember: source is at *higher* potential than gate, so $V_S > V_G$, hence $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S < 0$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Saturation Check:** Computing $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, $V_{DSQ}$ is only **half** the problem. You **must** verify $V_{DSQ} \geq V_{GSQ} - V_{GS(th)}$. If the Q-point falls in triode, the bias design is **invalid** for amplifier operation.
>
> 3. **Wrong Root Selection:** The quadratic in $\sqrt{I_D}$ has two roots. Always choose the one that gives **physical** $V_{GS}$ (i.e., $V_{GS}$ between $V_{GS(th)}$ and $V_G$ for the given topology). Reject roots where $V_{GS} < V_{GS(th)}$.
>
> 4. **$\lambda$ Omission (or Misuse):** When $\lambda \neq 0$, the saturation current includes $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$. For exact KTU answers, include it. For initial design estimates, $\lambda = 0$ is acceptable.
>
> 5. **Units in Final Answer:** KTU valuation **deducts** marks for missing units. Always specify $I_D$ in mA or A, $V_{GS}$ in V, $R$ in ╬® or k╬®.
>
> 6. **Stability Factor Definition:** When asked for $S$, do **not** just state the formula ŌĆö also compute the numerical value for the given circuit and **compare** with the corresponding fixed-bias value. Showing the comparison earns full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision ŌĆö Bullet Card for Last-Minute KTU Prep**

- Ō£ģ **Q-point = $(I_{DQ}, V_{DSQ})$** ŌĆö set by DC bias network; must lie in **saturation region**.
- Ō£ģ **Saturation conditions:** $V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$ **AND** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} = V_{OV}$.
- Ō£ģ **Shockley Equation (Saturation):** $I_D = K_n(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$ (with $\lambda = 0$); multiply by $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ for $\lambda \neq 0$.
- Ō£ģ **$K_n = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}$** ŌĆö geometry-dependent conduction parameter.
- Ō£ģ **Transconductance:** $g_m = 2\sqrt{K_n I_D} = 2K_n(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})$.
- Ō£ģ **Voltage-Divider Bias** is the **most stable** discrete configuration ŌĆö uses $R_1$, $R_2$ to set $V_G$ and $R_S$ for DC negative feedback.
- Ō£ģ **Self-Bias** uses only $R_G$, $R_S$, $R_D$ ŌĆö no divider; works for depletion NMOS/JFET, may fail for enhancement NMOS.
- Ō£ģ **Fixed Bias** is the **worst** for stability ŌĆö used mainly in IC current-mirror designs.
- Ō£ģ **Stability Factor** $S_{V_{th}} = \dfrac{V_{GS(th)}}{V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + g_m R_S}$.
- Ō£ģ **Always check saturation** after computing Q-point ŌĆö invalid bias = full marks deducted in design problems.
- Ō£ģ **Quadratic in $\sqrt{I_D}$** ŌĆö solve using $a u^2 + b u + c = 0$ formula; choose physically meaningful root.
- Ō£ģ **Thevenin equivalent** at gate: $V_{TH} = V_{DD}\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$; $R_{TH} = R_1 \Vert R_2$.
- Ō£ģ **Overdrive voltage** $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$ determines $V_{DS,sat}$ ŌĆö a key design parameter.
- Ō£ģ **Temperature sensitivity:** $V_{GS(th)}$ shifts $\approx \mathbf{-2\;mV/^{\circ}C}$ ŌĆö self/divider bias counters this via $R_S$.
- Ō£ģ **Gate current is zero** in DC analysis ŌĆö MOSFET input is insulated.
- Ō£ģ **AC vs DC separation:** DC sets the Q-point; AC analysis (covered in next module) uses small-signal $g_m$ and $r_o$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET as an Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MOSFET as an Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOSFET as an Amplifier** is a small-signal analog circuit configuration in which a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET), biased in the **saturation (pinch-off) region**, uses the transconductance property (\(g_m\)) of the device to convert a small time-varying input voltage (\(v_{gs}\)) at the gate into a proportional time-varying output current (\(i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs}\)) at the drain, which is then converted into an amplified output voltage (\(v_{ds}\)) across a load resistance (\(R_D\)).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the MOSFET is operated as a **Voltage-Controlled Current Source (VCCS)** in the small-signal domain, and the amplifier is characterized by three principal parameters:
- **Transconductance (\(g_m\))** ŌĆö measured in **Amperes per Volt (A/V) or Siemens (S)**,
- **Voltage Gain (\(A_v\))** ŌĆö dimensionless ratio,
- **Input and Output Resistances (\(R_{in}, R_{out}\))** ŌĆö measured in **Ohms (\(\Omega\))**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy ŌĆö "The Faucet and the Water Pressure Model"**
>
> Imagine a **pressurized water tap** connected to a flexible pipe. The tap's handle acts as the **gate** of the MOSFET, the water pressure at the inlet is the **drain supply (\(V_{DD}\))**, and the flow rate of water coming out is the **drain current (\(I_D\))**.
>
> - A **tiny rotation** of the handle (small input signal \(v_{gs}\)) produces a **large change** in the water flow rate.
> - This changed flow is then routed through a **narrow nozzle** (the load resistor \(R_D\)), which converts the flow change into a **large pressure drop** (output voltage \(v_{ds}\)).
> - The MOSFET, therefore, behaves like a **"voltage-to-flow transducer"** ŌĆö a small voltage effort at the gate produces a large voltage swing at the drain.

The amplification arises because the **gate is electrically isolated** (oxide layer, ~10Ōü╗Ōü╣ m thick), meaning the input draws **virtually zero current**, while the output is sourced through a low-impedance current generator. This **impedance transformation** is the true source of power/voltage gain.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics Highlighted in Bold:**
> - **Transconductance \(g_m\)**: typically **0.1 mA/V to 30 mA/V** for small-signal MOSFETs.
> - **Threshold Voltage \(V_{th}\) (or \(V_{TH}\))**: typically **0.5 V to 3 V** for modern CMOS processes.
> - **Process Transconductance Parameter \(k'_n\)**: typically **25 ╬╝A/V┬▓ to 250 ╬╝A/V┬▓** depending on oxide thickness.
> - **Output Resistance \(r_o\)** (due to channel-length modulation): typically **10 k\(\Omega\) to 100 k\(\Omega\)**.
> - **Capacitance \(C_{ox}\)**: oxide capacitance per unit area, typically **\(10^{-8}\) to \(10^{-7}\) F/cm┬▓**.

---

## 1.4 Why MOSFET as an Amplifier? (Engineering Motivation)

- **Infinite DC input impedance** ŌåÆ ideal for high-impedance signal sources (e.g., sensors, antennas).
- **Low power consumption** ŌåÆ suitable for battery-operated and portable analog front-ends.
- **High packing density** ŌåÆ millions of amplifiers on a single CMOS IC.
- **Low noise** at moderate frequencies ŌåÆ preferred in RF and audio pre-amplifiers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Prerequisites ŌĆö Biasing the MOSFET for Amplification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Operation (KTU Board-Exam Favourite):**
> For the MOSFET to function as a **linear amplifier**, it must be biased in the **saturation (active) region**. This is the single most-tested fact in Module 1 of PBRAT304.

The two mandatory conditions for saturation are:

1. **Gate-Source condition:** \(V_{GS} > V_{TH}\)  *(to turn the channel ON)*
2. **Drain-Source condition:** \(V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}\)  *(to keep the channel pinched off at the drain end)*

The quantity \((V_{GS} - V_{TH})\) is called the **overdrive voltage**, often denoted \(V_{OV}\). The DC operating point is called the **Q-point** or **quiescent point**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Amplification Mechanism

- **Step 1 ŌĆö DC Biasing:** A DC gate voltage \(V_{GS,Q}\) and drain voltage \(V_{DS,Q}\) are established such that the Q-point lies in the middle of the saturation region on the \(I_D\)ŌĆō\(V_{DS}\) characteristic curve.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Small-Signal Superposition:** A small time-varying input signal \(v_{gs}(t)\) is superimposed on the DC bias. The total instantaneous gate-source voltage becomes \(v_{GS}(t) = V_{GS,Q} + v_{gs}(t)\).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Nonlinear-to-Linear Conversion (Taylor Series):** The drain current is a nonlinear function of \(V_{GS}\). Taylor expansion around the Q-point gives a linearized expression, and the **partial derivative \(\partial I_D / \partial V_{GS}\)** evaluated at the Q-point is defined as the **transconductance \(g_m\)**.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Output Current Generation:** The MOSFET delivers an AC drain current \(i_d(t) = g_m \cdot v_{gs}(t)\) to the load.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Voltage Conversion:** The AC drain current flows through \(R_D\) (or \(R_D \parallel r_o\)), producing an amplified AC drain voltage \(v_{ds}(t) = -i_d(t) \cdot R_D = -g_m R_D \cdot v_{gs}(t)\).
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Power/Voltage Gain:** Because the gate draws no current, input power is near zero, and the voltage ratio \(A_v = v_{ds}/v_{gs}\) can be very large (e.g., ŌłÆ10 to ŌłÆ100 for a single stage).

---

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Parameter

- **Why is \(g_m\) called 'transconductance'?** Because it has units of **conductance (A/V)**, but unlike a resistor's conductance, it relates an **input voltage** to an **output current** ŌĆö hence *trans* (across) *conductance*.
- **How is \(g_m\) physically realized?** It is the **slope of the \(\sqrt{I_D}\) vs. \(V_{GS}\) curve** at the Q-point. A steeper slope ŌćÆ higher \(g_m\) ŌćÆ more amplification.
- **Why negative voltage gain in common-source?** Because an **increase in \(V_{GS}\)** causes \(I_D\) to **rise**, which causes a **larger drop** across \(R_D\), and thus \(V_{DS}\) **falls**. This 180┬░ phase inversion is a hallmark of the common-source amplifier.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready** and aligns with the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus outcomes.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Derivation Source** | **Units** | **Validity** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drain Current (Saturation) | \(I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} k'_n \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2\) | Square-law I-V relation | **A (Amperes)** | Saturation region only |
| Overdrive Voltage | \(V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}\) | Definition | **V (Volts)** | \(V_{OV} > 0\) |
| Transconductance (Method 1) | \(g_m = k'_n \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})\) | \(\partial I_D / \partial V_{GS}\) | **A/V (Siemens)** | Saturation region |
| Transconductance (Method 2) | \(g_m = 2 \dfrac{I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}\) | Substitute \(I_D\) | **A/V** | Saturation region |
| Transconductance (Method 3) | \(g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}\) | Eliminate \(V_{GS}\) | **A/V** | Saturation region |
| Output Resistance | \(r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}\) | Channel-length modulation | **\(\Omega\)** | \(\lambda\) = CLM parameter (\(V^{-1}\)) |
| Voltage Gain (Basic) | \(A_v = -g_m R_D\) | Ideal current-source model | **dimensionless** | Neglecting \(r_o\) |
| Voltage Gain (Accurate) | \(A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)\) | Including channel-length modulation | **dimensionless** | For long-channel devices |
| Input Resistance | \(R_{in} = \infty\) (ideal) | Gate oxide isolation | **\(\Omega\)** | DC and low frequency |
| Output Resistance | \(R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o\) | Looking into drain | **\(\Omega\)** | Without source degeneration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** The negative sign in \(A_v = -g_m R_D\) signifies the **180┬░ phase inversion** between input and output. Examiners award full marks only if this sign is **explicitly shown** in the answer.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Preamplifiers** in smartphones (CMOS op-amp first stage).
- **RF Low-Noise Amplifiers (LNAs)** in wireless receivers.
- **Biomedical Signal Amplifiers** (ECG, EEG front-ends) due to high input impedance.
- **Charge-sensitive amplifiers** in radiation detectors and image sensors.
- **Transimpedance Amplifiers (TIAs)** in optical communication photoreceivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Transconductance \(g_m\) from the Square-Law I-V Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **foundation** of MOSFET small-signal amplifier analysis. The KTU 2024 Board pattern **routinely** awards 5ŌĆō7 marks for this derivation.

### Starting Equation (Saturation Region Drain Current)

The DC drain current in saturation, neglecting channel-length modulation, is given by the **square-law equation**:

$$
I_D = \frac{1}{2} \, k'_n \, \frac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
$$

> Here, \(k'_n\) is the process transconductance parameter (A/V┬▓), \(W\) and \(L\) are the channel width and length (micrometers), and \(V_{TH}\) is the threshold voltage.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the Definition of Transconductance

Transconductance is the rate of change of drain current with respect to gate-source voltage at the Q-point. Mathematically:

$$
g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS} = \text{const}}
$$

> The condition \(V_{DS} = \text{const}\) holds because we are operating in the saturation region where \(I_D\) is independent of \(V_{DS}\) (to first order).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate the Square-Law Expression

Apply the chain rule. The derivative of \((V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2\) with respect to \(V_{GS}\) is \(2(V_{GS} - V_{TH})\):

$$
g_m = \frac{1}{2} \, k'_n \, \frac{W}{L} \, \cdot \, 2 \, (V_{GS} - V_{TH})
$$

> The factor of ┬Į in the original expression and the factor of 2 from differentiation cancel out.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify to Obtain the Final Form (Method 1)

$$
g_m = k'_n \, \frac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{TH})
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** \(g_m\) is directly proportional to the aspect ratio \((W/L)\) and the overdrive voltage \((V_{GS} - V_{TH})\). Designers tune \(W/L\) to set \(g_m\).

### Step 4 ŌĆö Express in Terms of \(I_D\) (Method 2)

Starting from the same square-law equation, solve for \((V_{GS} - V_{TH})\):

$$
(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n \, (W/L)}}
$$

Substitute this into the expression for \(g_m\):

$$
g_m = k'_n \, \frac{W}{L} \, \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n \, (W/L)}}
$$

Simplify by extracting the square root:

$$
g_m = \sqrt{2 \, k'_n \, \frac{W}{L} \, I_D}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** To double \(g_m\), the designer must increase the bias current \(I_D\) by a factor of **four** (square-root relationship).

### Step 5 ŌĆö Alternative Form Using Overdrive Voltage (Method 3)

From Method 1, use the identity \(I_D = \frac{1}{2} g_m (V_{GS} - V_{TH})\), which gives:

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}
$$

> This form is the most commonly **used in KTU numerical problems** because it directly relates \(g_m\) to the bias current.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Voltage Gain of the Common-Source MOSFET Amplifier

### Circuit Topology

A **common-source (CS) amplifier** has the input applied to the **gate**, the output taken from the **drain**, and the **source** connected to ground (AC-wise, with a bypass capacitor).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Small-Signal Model

Replace the MOSFET with its small-signal equivalent: a voltage-controlled current source \(g_m v_{gs}\) at the drain, with the gate as an open circuit.

> The gate-source small-signal voltage is \(v_{gs} = v_{in}\), since the source is at AC ground.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Express the Output Current

The AC drain current generated by the dependent source is:

$$
i_d = g_m \, v_{gs}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply KCL at the Output Node

The AC drain current flows through the load resistor \(R_D\), producing a voltage drop. By KCL (output node has no other branches in the basic model):

$$
v_{out} = -i_d \cdot R_D
$$

> The negative sign arises because conventional current flowing **into** the drain from \(V_{DD}\) (top of \(R_D\)) creates a voltage drop such that the **drain voltage decreases** when \(i_d\) increases.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute \(i_d = g_m v_{gs}\) and Recognize \(v_{gs} = v_{in}\)

$$
v_{out} = -g_m \, v_{gs} \cdot R_D = -g_m \, v_{in} \cdot R_D
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Form the Voltage Gain Ratio

$$
A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m R_D
$$

> **Sign Convention:** The negative sign confirms a **180┬░ phase reversal** between input and output ŌĆö a signature of the common-source topology.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Refined Gain Including \(r_o\)

For an accurate model, the dependent source has a parallel resistance \(r_o\) (due to channel-length modulation). The total load at the drain becomes \(R_D \parallel r_o\):

$$
A_v = -g_m \, (R_D \parallel r_o)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Input and Output Resistance of the CS Amplifier

### Input Resistance

The gate of a MOSFET is **capacitively coupled** through the oxide, meaning at DC and low frequencies, **no current flows** into the gate. Therefore:

$$
R_{in} = \frac{v_{gs}}{i_{gate}} \rightarrow \infty
$$

> For practical circuits, \(R_{in}\) is set by the biasing resistors (e.g., voltage-divider network), typically **100 k\(\Omega\) to 10 M\(\Omega\)**.

### Output Resistance

Looking into the drain of the small-signal model, the test-voltage method gives:

$$
R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o
$$

> For \(r_o \gg R_D\) (a common case), \(R_{out} \approx R_D\).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Data (typical KTU problem):** \(k'_n (W/L) = 1 \text{ mA/V}^2\), \(V_{TH} = 1 \text{ V}\), \(I_D = 0.5 \text{ mA}\), \(R_D = 10 \text{ k}\Omega\), \(\lambda = 0.02 \text{ V}^{-1}\).
> **Find:** \(g_m\), \(r_o\), and \(A_v\).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute \(g_m\)

Using the formula \(g_m = \sqrt{2 \, k'_n \, (W/L) \, I_D}\):

$$
g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
g_m = \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/V} = 1 \text{ mA/V}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute \(r_o\)

$$
r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{10^{-5}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Voltage Gain (Accurate)

$$
A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -1 \times 10^{-3} \times (10\text{k} \parallel 100\text{k})
$$

$$
R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{10 \times 100}{10 + 100} = \frac{1000}{110} \approx 9.09 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

$$
A_v = -1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.09 \times 10^{3} = -9.09 \text{ V/V}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A small-signal input of 1 mV produces an **inverted output of 9.09 mV**, confirming voltage amplification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MOSFET Small-Signal Equivalent Model (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hybrid-ŽĆ small-signal model** of the MOSFET, which is the cornerstone of all amplifier analyses. It is drawn in functional block form to avoid the limitations of physical schematics in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Voltage Source v_gs] -->|gate terminal| B[GATE NODE]
    B --> C[GATE-TO-SOURCE OPEN CIRCUIT\ninfinite input impedance]
    B --> D[Voltage Controlled Current Source\nid = gm times v_gs]
    D -->|current flow| E[DRAIN NODE]
    E --> F[Output Resistance ro\nrepresenting channel length modulation]
    E --> G[Load Resistor RD to VDD]
    E --> H[Output Voltage v_ds terminal]
    I[SOURCE NODE] -->|AC ground reference| C
    I -->|AC ground reference| F
    I -->|AC ground reference| G
    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The voltage source \(v_{gs}\) appears across the gate-source terminals. The dependent current source \(g_m v_{gs}\) drives the parallel combination of \(r_o\) and \(R_D\) at the drain, generating the output voltage \(v_{ds}\).

---

## 4.2 Common-Source Amplifier Block Diagram (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sigBlock[Small Signal Input v_in] -->|coupled via Cin| gateBiasBlock[DC Bias Network VGS]
    gateBiasBlock --> mosfetBlock[NMOS in Saturation Region\nID flows from Drain to Source]
    mosfetBlock --> drainNode[Drain Node with AC Voltage v_ds]
    drainNode --> rdLoadBlock[Load Resistor RD connected to VDD]
    drainNode --> routBlock[Output Resistance ro to AC Ground]
    drainNode -->|coupled via Cout| outBlock[Amplified Output v_out with 180 degree phase inversion]
    style sigBlock fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style gateBiasBlock fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style mosfetBlock fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style drainNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outBlock fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 I-V Characteristic Curve (Saturation Region Operating Point)

> [!NOTE]
> The following flowchart conceptually represents the placement of the Q-point on the MOSFET's \(I_D\)ŌĆō\(V_{DS}\) characteristic curve, which is the geometric foundation of amplifier operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisOrigin[VDS = 0 ID = 0 Origin Point] --> prePinch[VDS less than VOV Triode Region Linear Rise]
    prePinch --> pinchOff[VDS equals VOV Pinch Off Boundary Vth line]
    pinchOff --> saturationFlat[Saturation Region Nearly Constant ID Independent of VDS]
    saturationFlat --> qPointLocation[Q Point Selected at Midpoint of Saturation Region for Maximum Symmetric Swing]
    qPointLocation --> qPointEffect[Q Point determines gm and Av for small signal amplification]
    style axisOrigin fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style prePinch fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style pinchOff fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style saturationFlat fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style qPointLocation fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style qPointEffect fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Signal Flow Block Diagram (DC + AC Superposition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dcSource[DC Bias Voltage VGS Q Point] --> superpositionBlock[Superposition at Gate]
    acSource[Small Signal Input v_gs time varying] --> superpositionBlock
    superpositionBlock --> totalVGS[Total Gate Source Voltage VGS Q plus v_gs]
    totalVGS --> mosfetTrans[NMOS Transconductance Stage]
    mosfetTrans --> drainCurrent[AC Drain Current id equals gm v_gs]
    drainCurrent --> loadConversion[Conversion to Voltage across RD]
    loadConversion --> outputVoltage[Output v_ds equals minus gm RD v_gs]
    style dcSource fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style acSource fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    superpositionBlock -->|sum| totalVGS
    style mosfetTrans fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outputVoltage fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are **direct-concept** questions modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme Part A pattern (2-mark conceptual + 1-mark explanation).

### **Q1. Why must a MOSFET amplifier be biased in the saturation region?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A MOSFET must be biased in the saturation (pinch-off) region to function as a linear amplifier for the following reasons:
1. In saturation, the drain current \(I_D\) is **independent of \(V_{DS}\)** (to first order), making the device behave as an **ideal voltage-controlled current source**. This linearity is essential for distortion-free amplification. **(1.5 marks)**
2. In the triode region, \(I_D\) varies strongly with \(V_{DS}\), and the device acts like a **voltage-controlled resistor** rather than an amplifier, producing non-linear and uncontrolled output. **(1 mark)**
3. The small-signal model parameters \(g_m\) and \(r_o\) are well-defined only in the saturation region. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Q2. Define transconductance \(g_m\) of a MOSFET. State its units.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Transconductance \(g_m\) is defined as the **rate of change of drain current with respect to gate-source voltage**, evaluated at the DC operating (Q-) point, while keeping \(V_{DS}\) constant.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS} = \text{const}}
$$

**Units:** Amperes per Volt (A/V) or equivalently **Siemens (S)** or **mhos**. **(0.5 mark for units)**
For typical small-signal MOSFETs, \(g_m\) ranges from **0.1 mA/V to 30 mA/V**. **(0.5 mark for value range)**
It is called "trans" conductance because it relates an input **voltage** to an output **current**, crossing the device's input-output boundary. **(0.5 mark for name origin)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions follow the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern** ŌĆö two 7-mark sub-parts each. Internal choice is provided via **Question A and Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**
**Derive the expression for the transconductance \(g_m\) of a MOSFET biased in the saturation region. Hence, obtain three alternative forms of \(g_m\).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start with the square-law equation for drain current in saturation:

$$
I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the definition of transconductance:

$$
g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{Q\text{-point}}
$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate:

$$
g_m = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})
$$

**This is Form 1.** **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** From the square-law, \((V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \sqrt{2 I_D / (k'_n W/L)}\). Substitute:

$$
g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n (W/L)}} = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D}
$$

**This is Form 2.** **(2 marks)**

**Step 5:** Rearrange Form 1 as \(g_m / (k'_n W/L) = (V_{GS} - V_{TH})\). From the square-law, \(I_D = \frac{1}{2} g_m (V_{GS} - V_{TH})\), so:

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}
$$

**This is Form 3.** **(2 marks)**

**Step 6:** State the engineering significance: **all three forms are equivalent; choice depends on available data.** **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**
**For an NMOS common-source amplifier with \(R_D = 5 \text{ k}\Omega\) and \(g_m = 2 \text{ mA/V}\), calculate the voltage gain. If the channel-length modulation parameter is \(\lambda = 0.01 \text{ V}^{-1}\) and \(I_D = 0.5 \text{ mA}\), find the refined gain including \(r_o\).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the basic voltage gain (ignoring \(r_o\)):

$$
A_v = -g_m R_D = -(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{3}) = -10 \text{ V/V}
$$

**= ŌłÆ10 V/V (or 20 dB)** **(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Compute \(r_o\) using the channel-length modulation formula:

$$
r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.01 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 200 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Compute the parallel combination \(R_D \parallel r_o\):

$$
R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{5 \times 200}{5 + 200} = \frac{1000}{205} \approx 4.878 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4:** Compute the refined voltage gain:

$$
A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (4.878 \times 10^{3}) \approx -9.76 \text{ V/V}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Conclusion:** The presence of \(r_o\) reduces the gain magnitude slightly (from 10 to 9.76), illustrating that the **finite output resistance degrades the ideal gain**. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Sample Paper]`

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**
**Explain with a neat small-signal equivalent circuit how a MOSFET acts as a common-source amplifier. Derive the expression for voltage gain.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Draw / describe the **small-signal hybrid-ŽĆ model** consisting of:
- Gate as an open circuit (infinite input impedance),
- A voltage-controlled current source \(g_m v_{gs}\) between drain and source,
- Output resistance \(r_o\) in parallel with the controlled source,
- Load resistor \(R_D\) from drain to \(V_{DD}\) (AC ground). **(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Identify input and output nodes:
- Input \(v_{gs}\) applied at the gate (with source at AC ground).
- Output \(v_{ds}\) taken at the drain. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Express the AC drain current:

$$
i_d = g_m v_{gs}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Apply KCL at the output node. Since the dependent source current flows from drain to source, and the current through \(R_D\) flows from \(V_{DD}\) to drain, the output voltage is:

$$
v_{out} = -i_d R_D = -g_m v_{gs} R_D
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5:** Form the gain ratio:

$$
A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m R_D
$$

**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 6:** With \(r_o\) included, the load becomes \(R_D \parallel r_o\), giving the refined gain:

$$
A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)
$$

**(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**
**A MOSFET amplifier is biased at \(I_D = 1 \text{ mA}\) with \(k'_n (W/L) = 0.5 \text{ mA/V}^2\) and \(V_{TH} = 1 \text{ V}\). Find: (i) \(V_{GS}\), (ii) \(g_m\), (iii) voltage gain if \(R_D = 8 \text{ k}\Omega\).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the overdrive voltage using the square-law equation:

$$
I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2
$$

$$
V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n (W/L)}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{4} = 2 \text{ V}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Find \(V_{GS}\):

$$
V_{GS} = V_{OV} + V_{TH} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ V}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Find \(g_m\):

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1 \text{ mA/V}
$$

Alternatively, \(g_m = k'_n (W/L) V_{OV} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 1 \text{ mA/V}\). **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Find the voltage gain:

$$
A_v = -g_m R_D = -(1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (8 \times 10^{3}) = -8 \text{ V/V}
$$

**(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "MOSFET as an Amplifier" Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in \(A_v\):** The negative sign indicating 180┬░ phase inversion is a **1-mark item**. Many students write \(A_v = g_m R_D\) and lose that mark. *Always explicitly show the sign.*
>
> 2. **Using the triode-region equation by mistake:** The square-law \(I_D\) equation is valid **only in saturation**. If \(V_{DS} < V_{OV}\), the wrong formula is applied, and the entire numerical answer becomes incorrect.
>
> 3. **Mixing up \(g_m\) formulas:** Each of the three forms of \(g_m\) requires different given data. Students often plug values into the wrong formula. *Match the formula to the given parameters.*
>
> 4. **Omitting units in the final answer:** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (e.g., A/V for \(g_m\), V/V for \(A_v\)).
>
> 5. **Ignoring the parallel combination \(R_D \parallel r_o\):** When \(\lambda\) is given, **always** include \(r_o\) in the gain calculation. Marks are specifically allocated for this refinement.
>
> 6. **Confusing \(g_m\) with the small-signal output conductance \(g_{ds}\):** \(g_m = \partial I_D / \partial V_{GS}\) (input-controlled), while \(g_{ds} = 1/r_o = \partial I_D / \partial V_{DS}\) (output-related). Examiners often test this distinction.
>
> 7. **Not drawing/mentioning the small-signal model:** For 7-mark derivations, even a **textual description** of the model is mandatory. Skipping it costs 2ŌĆō3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision ŌĆö Print-Save Memorize:**
>
> - **MOSFET amplifier works only in SATURATION region** ŌĆö both conditions \(V_{GS} > V_{TH}\) and \(V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}\) must hold.
> - **Q-point** is the DC operating point; it must be centrally placed in saturation for maximum symmetric output swing.
> - **Transconductance \(g_m\) has three forms:**
>   - \(g_m = k'_n (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})\)
>   - \(g_m = 2 I_D / (V_{GS} - V_{TH})\)
>   - \(g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D}\)
> - **Voltage gain of common-source amplifier:** \(A_v = -g_m R_D\) (basic) or \(A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)\) (refined).
> - **Negative sign = 180┬░ phase inversion** ŌĆö never omit it.
> - **Input resistance of MOSFET amplifier = ideally infinite** (gate oxide isolation).
> - **Output resistance** = \(R_D \parallel r_o\).
> - **Channel-length modulation** is modeled by \(r_o = 1 / (\lambda I_D)\).
> - **Overdrive voltage** \(V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}\) ŌĆö central to all biasing and \(g_m\) calculations.
> - **Square-law I-V** applies **only in saturation**: \(I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2\).
> - **Aspect ratio \((W/L)\)** is the designer's primary tool to set \(g_m\) and hence amplifier gain.
> - **Doubling \(g_m\) requires quadrupling \(I_D\)** (square-root relationship) ŌĆö a frequent MCQ/trap question.
> - **MOSFET as amplifier** = Voltage-Controlled Current Source (VCCS) in the small-signal domain.
> - **Units to remember:** \(g_m\) ŌåÆ A/V or S; \(A_v\) ŌåÆ V/V (or dB = \(20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert\)); \(r_o, R_D\) ŌåÆ \(\Omega\).

topics.title: Biasing of Discrete MOSFET Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biasing of Discrete MOSFET Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **MOSFET Biasing** is the process of establishing a fixed **DC operating point (Q-point)** in the saturation region of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor by applying appropriate DC voltages to its three terminals ŌĆö **Gate (G), Drain (D), and Source (S)** ŌĆö so that the device operates as a linear amplifier when an AC signal is superimposed on the DC bias.

The DC operating point of a MOSFET amplifier is characterized by four quiescent quantities:

| Parameter | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Drain Current | $I_{DQ}$ | DC current flowing into the drain terminal |
| Gate-to-Source Voltage | $V_{GSQ}$ | DC voltage between gate and source |
| Drain-to-Source Voltage | $V_{DSQ}$ | DC voltage between drain and source |
| Transconductance | $g_{mQ}$ | Small-signal parameter at the Q-point |

The Q-point is selected such that the MOSFET remains firmly in the **saturation (active) region** for the entire AC signal swing, which requires:

$$V_{DSQ} \geq V_{GSQ} - V_t \quad \text{and} \quad I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2$$

where $V_t$ is the **threshold voltage** and $K_n$ is the conduction parameter (units: A/V┬▓).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Valve and Pressure Tank" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture (The Garden Hose Analogy):**
> Imagine a **MOSFET** as a **variable water nozzle** attached to a garden hose:
> - The **gate voltage $V_{GS}$** acts like the **squeezing force on the trigger** of the nozzle ŌĆö the harder you press, the more current (water) flows.
> - The **threshold voltage $V_t$** is the **minimum force** you must apply before *any* water flows ŌĆö below it, the nozzle is shut.
> - The **drain supply $V_{DD}$** is the **municipal water pressure** pushing water through the pipe.
> - The **source resistor $R_S$** is a **partial flow-restrictor** placed *back at the hose base* ŌĆö it creates a small pressure drop that "talks back" to the trigger mechanism, stabilizing the flow.
>
> **Biasing = pre-setting the trigger squeeze** so that the nozzle operates in a sweet spot ŌĆö neither fully closed nor fully open ŌĆö so that tiny variations in squeeze pressure (your AC signal) produce proportional, undistorted changes in water flow (output current).

This is why biasing is called "**setting the stage**" for amplification.

### 1.3 Why Biasing is Indispensable

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Reality Check:** A *common B.Tech mistake* is to assume an amplifier works without bias. A MOSFET with $V_{GS} = 0$ is OFF (for enhancement type), and if biased beyond saturation, it distorts badly. Proper biasing ensures the AC signal rides symmetrically on a stable DC level ŌĆö this is the **#1 reason MOSFET amplifiers are biased**.

The three golden goals of any biasing network are:
1. **Establish a stable Q-point** in the saturation region.
2. **Make $I_{DQ}$ insensitive to variations** in device parameters (like $V_t$ and $K_n$) and temperature.
3. **Couple the AC signal efficiently** in and out without disturbing the bias.

### 1.4 Standard Parameters in KTU 2024 Syllabus

- **Threshold voltage $V_t$** of a typical enhancement NMOS: **0.5 V to 1.5 V**
- **Conduction parameter $K_n$** (often written $\frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}$): in the range **0.1 mA/V┬▓ to 5 mA/V┬▓**
- **Transconductance $g_m = 2\sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}}$** ŌĆö fundamental small-signal parameter (units: **A/V or Siemens/Siemens**)
- **Channel-length modulation parameter $\lambda$**: typically **0.01 VŌü╗┬╣ to 0.05 VŌü╗┬╣**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Four Standard MOSFET Biasing Circuits

A discrete MOSFET amplifier (using no ICs) must be biased using external resistors and a DC supply. The four canonical configurations tested in KTU are:

1. **Fixed Bias (Gate-to-Ground Bias)**
2. **Self-Bias (Source Resistor Bias)**
3. **Voltage-Divider Bias**
4. **Drain-Feedback Bias**

### 2.2 Biasing Configuration 1: Fixed Bias

The gate is connected to a fixed DC voltage $V_{GG}$ (often from a separate supply, or via a divider from $V_{DD}$). Source is grounded.

**Operating Equations:**

$$V_{GSQ} = V_{GG}$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2 = K_n(V_{GG} - V_t)^2$$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D$$

**Stability Factor:**

$$S = \frac{\partial I_{DQ}}{\partial I_{DQ}} \cdot \frac{\partial I_{DQ}}{\partial K_n} \cdot \dots \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Fixed bias is INDEPENDENT of } V_t \text{ but DEPENDENT on } K_n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Fixed bias has **poor stability** because $V_{GS}$ does not adjust if $I_D$ tries to drift. For example, if $K_n$ increases (e.g., due to temperature), $I_D$ increases uncontrollably, possibly destroying the device.

### 2.3 Biasing Configuration 2: Self-Bias (Source Resistor Bias)

A resistor $R_S$ is placed at the source, and the gate is grounded through a large resistor $R_G$ (so that $I_G \approx 0$ for a MOSFET).

**Operating Equations:**

$$V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2 = K_n(-I_{DQ} R_S - V_t)^2$$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

**Stability Factor Derivation (Concept):**

$$S_I = \frac{\partial I_{DQ}}{\partial I_{D0}} = \frac{1}{1 + g_{mQ} R_S}$$

where $I_{D0}$ is the "zero-bias" current ($V_{GS} = 0$). The negative feedback action of $R_S$ stabilizes the bias.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why self-bias works:** If $I_D$ tends to increase (say, due to temperature), the voltage across $R_S$ ($= I_D R_S$) also increases. This makes $V_{GS}$ more negative, which *reduces* $I_D$, opposing the original change. This is **negative feedback at DC**.

### 2.4 Biasing Configuration 3: Voltage-Divider Bias (Most Common)

A resistor divider $R_1$ŌĆō$R_2$ sets the gate voltage, while $R_S$ provides feedback. This is the **workhorse of discrete MOSFET amplifier design**.

**Operating Equations:**

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{GSQ} = V_G - I_{DQ} R_S$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2$$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

**Stability Factor:**

$$S_I = \frac{1 + R_S/R_{th}}{1 + g_{mQ} R_S + R_S/R_{th}}$$

where $R_{th} = R_1 \parallel R_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Voltage-divider bias offers the best compromise between **bias stability** and **insensitivity to device parameter variations**. This is what you will analyze in 80% of numerical problems.

### 2.5 Biasing Configuration 4: Drain-Feedback Bias

A large resistor $R_G$ is connected from **drain to gate**, providing negative feedback that stabilizes $I_{DQ}$. The DC gate current is zero, so no current flows through $R_G$ at DC; the gate sits at the drain DC voltage.

**Operating Equations:**

$$V_{GSQ} = V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{DSQ} - V_t)^2 = K_n(V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D - V_t)^2$$

This is a single equation in $I_{DQ}$ that can be solved by substituting the quadratic.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU ESE and internal assessments.** Memorize this table.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drain current (saturation) | $I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2$ | **A** (Amperes) |
| 2 | Drain current (with channel modulation) | $I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2(1 + \lambda V_{DSQ})$ | Used in advanced analysis |
| 3 | Drain-to-source voltage (generic) | $V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D$ | For grounded-source configurations |
| 4 | Drain-to-source voltage (with $R_S$) | $V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$ | For self-bias and divider bias |
| 5 | Q-point stability factor | $S_I = \frac{1 + R_S/R_{th}}{1 + g_{mQ} R_S + R_S/R_{th}}$ | Voltage-divider bias |
| 6 | Simplified stability (no divider) | $S_I = \frac{1}{1 + g_{mQ} R_S}$ | Self-bias |
| 7 | Transconductance (at Q-point) | $g_{mQ} = 2 K_n (V_{GSQ} - V_t) = 2\sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}}$ | **A/V or S (Siemens)** |
| 8 | Small-signal output resistance | $r_{ds} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}}$ | **╬® (Ohms)** |
| 9 | Saturation boundary | $V_{DSQ} \geq V_{GSQ} - V_t$ (overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$) | Ensures active region |
| 10 | Overdrive voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GSQ} - V_t$ | **V (Volts)** |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MOSFET biasing is foundational to **virtually every analog and mixed-signal IC**:
> - **Audio amplifiers** in smartphones and home theaters
> - **RF front-end low-noise amplifiers (LNAs)** in 5G/Wi-Fi receivers
> - **Operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs)** in filters and data converters
> - **Source followers and level shifters** in mixed-voltage digital/analog SoCs
> - **Power management ICs** (LDOs, DC-DC converters)
>
> Without proper biasing, **none** of these would function. Biasing is to analog design what foundations are to a skyscraper.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Q-Point of Voltage-Divider Bias

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 12\text{ V}$, $R_1 = 1\text{ M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\text{ M}\Omega$, $R_D = 4\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_t = 1\text{ V}$, $K_n = 0.5\text{ mA/V}^2$.

**Find:** $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, $V_{DSQ}$, and verify saturation.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Thevenin equivalent at the gate:**

The gate draws no DC current (MOSFET gate is insulated), so $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a simple unloaded voltage divider.

$$V_{G} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{G} = 12 \cdot \frac{1\text{ M}\Omega}{1\text{ M}\Omega + 1\text{ M}\Omega} = 12 \cdot 0.5 = 6 \text{ V}$$

Thevenin resistance:

$$R_{th} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1 \cdot 1}{1 + 1} = 0.5 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write KVL around the gate-source loop:**

Starting from gate, going through $R_S$ to source (which is grounded through $R_S$):

$$V_{GSQ} = V_{G} - I_{DQ} R_S$$

Substituting:

$$V_{GSQ} = 6 - I_{DQ} \cdot (2\text{ k}\Omega)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the MOSFET saturation equation:**

$$I_{DQ} = K_n (V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2$$

Substituting $V_{GSQ} = 6 - 2 I_{DQ}$ (with $I_{DQ}$ in mA so that $2 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot I_{DQ} \text{ mA}$ gives volts):

$$I_{DQ} = 0.5 \cdot \left[(6 - 2 I_{DQ}) - 1\right]^2$$

$$I_{DQ} = 0.5 \cdot (5 - 2 I_{DQ})^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the quadratic:**

$$I_{DQ} = 0.5 \cdot (25 - 20 I_{DQ} + 4 I_{DQ}^2)$$

$$I_{DQ} = 12.5 - 10 I_{DQ} + 2 I_{DQ}^2$$

Rearranging to standard form ($a x^2 + b x + c = 0$):

$$2 I_{DQ}^2 - 11 I_{DQ} + 12.5 = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the quadratic using the formula:**

$$I_{DQ} = \frac{11 \pm \sqrt{(-11)^2 - 4(2)(12.5)}}{2 \cdot 2} = \frac{11 \pm \sqrt{121 - 100}}{4} = \frac{11 \pm \sqrt{21}}{4}$$

$$\sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$$

$$I_{DQ1} = \frac{11 + 4.583}{4} \approx 3.896 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_{DQ2} = \frac{11 - 4.583}{4} \approx 1.604 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Discard the unphysical root:**

For $I_{DQ1} = 3.896$ mA: $V_{GSQ} = 6 - 2(3.896) = -1.79$ V. This is **below $V_t = 1$ V**, which means the MOSFET is OFF ŌĆö invalid root.

For $I_{DQ2} = 1.604$ mA: $V_{GSQ} = 6 - 2(1.604) = 2.79$ V. Since $2.79 > 1$, the MOSFET is ON ŌĆö **valid root**.

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} = 1.60 \text{ mA}, \quad V_{GSQ} = 2.79 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute $V_{DSQ}$:**

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

$$V_{DSQ} = 12 - 1.604 \cdot (4 + 2) = 12 - 9.625 = 2.38 \text{ V}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Verify saturation region operation:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GSQ} - V_t = 2.79 - 1 = 1.79 \text{ V}$$

Check: $V_{DSQ} = 2.38$ V $\geq V_{OV} = 1.79$ V Ō£ō

**The Q-point is in the saturation region. The bias design is valid.**

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Stability Factor of Self-Bias

**Given:** Self-bias with $R_S$ and zero-bias current $I_{D0}$ (where $I_{D0} = K_n V_t^2$, the saturation current at $V_{GS} = 0$).

**Find:** $S_I = \partial I_{DQ} / \partial I_{D0}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the self-bias equation:**

$$V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S$$

The drain current with $V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S$:

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2 = K_n(-I_{DQ} R_S - V_t)^2$$

Define $I_{D0} = K_n V_t^2$ for algebraic convenience.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express $I_{DQ}$ in terms of $I_{D0}$:**

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(I_{DQ} R_S + V_t)^2 = K_n \left[I_{DQ}^2 R_S^2 + 2 I_{DQ} R_S V_t + V_t^2\right]$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n I_{DQ}^2 R_S^2 + 2 K_n I_{DQ} R_S V_t + K_n V_t^2$$

$$I_{DQ} = I_{D0} \frac{I_{DQ}^2 R_S^2}{V_t^2} + 2 I_{D0} \frac{I_{DQ} R_S}{V_t} + I_{D0}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to $I_{D0}$:**

Let $y = I_{DQ}$, $x = I_{D0}$. Differentiate the implicit equation:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{R_S^2}{V_t^2} \cdot 2 y \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} \cdot x + \frac{y^2 R_S^2}{V_t^2} + 2 \frac{R_S}{V_t} y \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} \cdot x + 2 \frac{R_S y}{V_t} + 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify using $g_{mQ} = 2 \sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}}$ and $V_t$:**

The result, after algebraic simplification (omitting intermediate algebra for clarity, as it is the standard result):

$$S_I = \frac{1}{1 + g_{mQ} R_S}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Interpretation:**

A larger $R_S$ gives a smaller $S_I$ (more stable). But $R_S$ also reduces $V_{DSQ}$, limiting signal swing. The design trade-off is:
- **Stability Ōåæ** with larger $R_S$
- **Signal headroom Ōåō** with larger $R_S$

A practical rule of thumb: choose $R_S$ such that $V_{GSQ}$ is approximately $\frac{1}{3}$ to $\frac{1}{2}$ of $V_{DD}$.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Effect of $\lambda$ on Q-Point (Advanced)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Modified drain current equation:**

With channel-length modulation:

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2(1 + \lambda V_{DSQ})$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D$ (grounded source):**

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2 \left[1 + \lambda(V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D)\right]$$

This is no longer purely quadratic in $I_{DQ}$ ŌĆö it now has a linear-in-$I_{DQ}$ term, giving a cubic equation in $I_{DQ}$. For typical $\lambda \approx 0.02$ VŌü╗┬╣, the effect on Q-point is small ($< 5\%$) and often neglected in hand calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Unless the problem explicitly gives $\lambda$ and asks for its effect, set $\lambda = 0$ in Q-point calculations. The effect of $\lambda$ is primarily seen in **small-signal output resistance** $r_{ds} = 1/(\lambda I_{DQ})$, not in DC biasing.

### 3.4 Numerical Comparison Matrix: Which Bias is "Best"?

| Property | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias | Voltage Divider | Drain Feedback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bias Stability** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good |
| **Insensitive to $K_n$ variation** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Insensitive to $V_t$ variation** | Yes (via $V_{GG}$) | No | Partially | Yes |
| **Independent DC supplies needed** | Yes ($V_{GG}$) | No | No | No |
| **Number of resistors** | 2 | 3 | 4ŌĆō5 | 2 |
| **Signal swing headroom** | Maximum | Reduced | Reduced | Medium |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ŌśģŌśģ | ŌśģŌśģŌśģ | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ | ŌśģŌśģ |
