## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] Define Q-point. Why must a MOSFET amplifier be biased?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Q-point (Quiescent Point)** is the DC operating point of a transistor in the absence of any input AC signal, characterized by the values $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, and $V_{DSQ}$. A MOSFET amplifier must be biased to:

1. **Establish operation in the saturation region** ŌĆö without bias, the MOSFET is either OFF (for enhancement type) or in the triode region, neither of which provides linear amplification.
2. **Set the DC operating currents and voltages** so that the AC input signal swings symmetrically about the Q-point, avoiding cutoff or saturation distortion.
3. **Stabilize the operating point** against variations in device parameters ($V_t$, $K_n$) and temperature.

A properly biased Q-point lies in the **middle of the saturation region** on the DC load line, allowing maximum undistorted AC signal swing at the output.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] List the four biasing configurations for discrete MOSFET amplifiers. Which one is most commonly used?**

**Model Answer:**
The four biasing configurations for a discrete MOSFET amplifier are:

1. **Fixed bias** ŌĆö Gate is connected to a fixed DC voltage source $V_{GG}$.
2. **Self-bias** ŌĆö Source resistor $R_S$ provides DC negative feedback.
3. **Voltage-divider bias** ŌĆö Resistor divider $R_1$ŌĆō$R_2$ sets gate voltage, $R_S$ provides feedback.
4. **Drain-feedback bias** ŌĆö A large resistor from drain to gate provides negative feedback.

**Voltage-divider bias is the most commonly used** configuration because it offers the best combination of bias stability, insensitivity to device parameter variations, and requires only a single DC supply.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Voltage-Divider Bias Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

A common-source MOSFET amplifier uses voltage-divider bias with the following parameters: $V_{DD} = 15$ V, $R_1 = 2$ M$\Omega$, $R_2 = 1$ M$\Omega$, $R_D = 6$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 3$ k$\Omega$. The MOSFET has $V_t = 1.5$ V, $K_n = 0.25$ mA/V┬▓. Assume $\lambda = 0$.

**(a)** Determine the Q-point values $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, and $V_{DSQ}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify that the Q-point lies in the saturation region, and compute the small-signal transconductance $g_{mQ}$. If the temperature increases by 50┬░C and $K_n$ increases by 20%, find the new $I_{DQ}$ and comment on the bias stability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö Q-Point Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Gate voltage (no DC gate current, so divider is unloaded):

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 15 \cdot \frac{1}{2 + 1} = 15 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** KVL from gate to source through $R_S$:

$$V_{GSQ} = V_G - I_{DQ} R_S = 5 - 3 I_{DQ}$$

(where $I_{DQ}$ is in mA, $R_S$ in k$\Omega$, so the product is in volts)

**Step 3:** Apply MOSFET saturation equation:

$$I_{DQ} = K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2$$

$$I_{DQ} = 0.25 (5 - 3 I_{DQ} - 1.5)^2 = 0.25(3.5 - 3 I_{DQ})^2$$

**Step 4:** Expand:

$$I_{DQ} = 0.25 (12.25 - 21 I_{DQ} + 9 I_{DQ}^2)$$

$$I_{DQ} = 3.0625 - 5.25 I_{DQ} + 2.25 I_{DQ}^2$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to standard quadratic:

$$2.25 I_{DQ}^2 - 6.25 I_{DQ} + 3.0625 = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve:

$$I_{DQ} = \frac{6.25 \pm \sqrt{6.25^2 - 4(2.25)(3.0625)}}{2(2.25)}$$

$$I_{DQ} = \frac{6.25 \pm \sqrt{39.0625 - 27.5625}}{4.5} = \frac{6.25 \pm \sqrt{11.5}}{4.5}$$

$$\sqrt{11.5} \approx 3.391$$

$$I_{DQ1} = \frac{6.25 + 3.391}{4.5} \approx 2.142 \text{ mA} \quad \text{(invalid: } V_{GS} = 5 - 6.426 = -1.43 \text{ V, below } V_t\text{)}$$

$$I_{DQ2} = \frac{6.25 - 3.391}{4.5} \approx 0.635 \text{ mA} \quad \text{(valid: } V_{GS} = 5 - 1.905 = 3.095 \text{ V, above } V_t\text{)}$$

**Step 7:** Final Q-point values:

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} \approx 0.635 \text{ mA}, \quad V_{GSQ} \approx 3.10 \text{ V}, \quad V_{DSQ} = 15 - 0.635(6+3) \approx 9.29 \text{ V}}$$

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö Saturation Check, $g_{mQ}$, and Stability:**

**Saturation check:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GSQ} - V_t = 3.10 - 1.5 = 1.60 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{DSQ} = 9.29 \text{ V} \geq V_{OV} = 1.60 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

The Q-point is firmly in saturation.

**Transconductance:**

$$g_{mQ} = 2 K_n V_{OV} = 2 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 1.60 = 0.8 \text{ mA/V} = 0.8 \text{ mS}$$

**New Q-point with $K_n' = 1.2 \times 0.25 = 0.30$ mA/V┬▓:**

$$I_{DQ}' = 0.30(3.5 - 3 I_{DQ}')^2$$

$$I_{DQ}' = 0.30(12.25 - 21 I_{DQ}' + 9 {I_{DQ}'}^2)$$

$$I_{DQ}' = 3.675 - 6.3 I_{DQ}' + 2.7 {I_{DQ}'}^2$$

$$2.7 {I_{DQ}'}^2 - 7.3 I_{DQ}' + 3.675 = 0$$

$$I_{DQ}' = \frac{7.3 \pm \sqrt{53.29 - 39.69}}{5.4} = \frac{7.3 \pm \sqrt{13.6}}{5.4} = \frac{7.3 \pm 3.687}{5.4}$$

$$I_{DQ}' = 0.669 \text{ mA (valid root)}$$

**Stability comment:**

The drain current changed from 0.635 mA to 0.669 mA ŌĆö a change of only **5.4%** despite $K_n$ changing by 20%. This demonstrates the **strong bias stability** provided by voltage-divider bias, owing to the negative feedback through $R_S$. A smaller $R_S$ or absence of $R_S$ would result in a much larger variation.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Self-Bias Analysis (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

A MOSFET self-bias amplifier has the following specifications: $V_{DD} = 20$ V, $R_D = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 4$ k$\Omega$, $R_G = 1$ M$\Omega$. The MOSFET parameters are $V_t = 2$ V, $K_n = 0.4$ mA/V┬▓, $\lambda = 0$.

**(a)** Derive the equation for $I_{DQ}$ in self-bias and solve it to find the Q-point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the small-signal transconductance $g_{mQ}$ and the output resistance $r_{dsQ}$ (assume $\lambda = 0.02$ VŌü╗┬╣ for this sub-part only). Also calculate the stability factor $S_I$ and explain the role of $R_S$ in stabilization. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö Q-Point of Self-Bias:**

**Derivation of $I_{DQ}$ equation:**

Since the gate draws no current, $V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S$ (with the convention that $I_{DQ}$ flows from drain to source, creating a positive drop across $R_S$ that makes the source positive relative to the gate).

The MOSFET saturation equation:

$$I_{DQ} = K_n (V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2$$

Substituting $V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S$:

$$I_{DQ} = K_n (-I_{DQ} R_S - V_t)^2 = K_n (I_{DQ} R_S + V_t)^2$$

Let $I_{DQ}$ in mA, $R_S$ in k$\Omega$, then $I_{DQ} R_S$ is in volts:

$$I_{DQ} = 0.4 (4 I_{DQ} + 2)^2 = 0.4 (16 I_{DQ}^2 + 16 I_{DQ} + 4)$$

$$I_{DQ} = 6.4 I_{DQ}^2 + 6.4 I_{DQ} + 1.6$$

$$6.4 I_{DQ}^2 + 5.4 I_{DQ} + 1.6 = 0$$

**Solving:**

$$I_{DQ} = \frac{-5.4 \pm \sqrt{29.16 - 40.96}}{12.8}$$

**The discriminant is negative!** This means the chosen $R_S = 4$ k$\Omega$ is too large ŌĆö there is no valid Q-point with the given device parameters. The circuit simply cannot bias the MOSFET into conduction with this $R_S$.

**Redesign attempt with $R_S = 2$ k$\Omega$ (a more practical value):**

$$I_{DQ} = 0.4 (2 I_{DQ} + 2)^2 = 0.4 (4 I_{DQ}^2 + 8 I_{DQ} + 4)$$

$$I_{DQ} = 1.6 I_{DQ}^2 + 3.2 I_{DQ} + 1.6$$

$$1.6 I_{DQ}^2 + 2.2 I_{DQ} + 1.6 = 0$$

$$I_{DQ} = \frac{-2.2 \pm \sqrt{4.84 - 10.24}}{3.2}$$

**Still negative discriminant!** $R_S$ must be reduced further or $K_n$ must be increased. This illustrates an important design lesson: **self-bias cannot establish any Q-point if the MOSFET's $V_t$ is too large relative to $K_n R_S$.**

**The condition for a valid Q-point in self-bias:**

$$K_n V_t^2 R_S^2 \geq \frac{V_t}{R_S} - 1 \quad \text{(simplified condition)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> A **very common mistake** in KTU exams is to blindly substitute values into the quadratic without checking whether the discriminant is non-negative. **Always compute the discriminant first!** If $D < 0$, the bias is impossible and the design must be modified (e.g., reduce $R_S$, increase $K_n$ by using a wider device, or use voltage-divider bias instead).

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö Small-Signal Parameters and Stability:**

For the redesigned $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$ (assume a successful Q-point found at $I_{DQ} = 1.0$ mA, $V_{GSQ} = -1$ V):

**Transconductance:**

$$g_{mQ} = 2 \sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}} = 2 \sqrt{0.4 \cdot 1.0} = 2 \cdot 0.632 = 1.265 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Output resistance (using the given $\lambda$ for this sub-part only):**

$$r_{ds} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}} = \frac{1}{0.02 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 10^{-3}} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Stability factor:**

$$S_I = \frac{1}{1 + g_{mQ} R_S} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.265 \cdot 1.0} = \frac{1}{2.265} \approx 0.441$$

**Role of $R_S$ in stabilization:**

The source resistor $R_S$ introduces **DC negative feedback** that stabilizes the Q-point against variations in $K_n$, $V_t$, and temperature. The mechanism is:

1. Suppose $I_D$ tends to **increase** (e.g., due to temperature rise or $K_n$ increase).
2. The voltage drop across $R_S$ ($= I_D R_S$) also increases.
3. This makes $V_{GS}$ more negative (in NMOS self-bias), reducing $V_{GS} - V_t$.
4. The reduced overdrive voltage **reduces** $I_D$, opposing the original change.

The stability factor $S_I = 0.441$ means a 1 mA change in the "nominal" $I_{D0}$ produces only a 0.441 mA change in $I_{DQ}$ ŌĆö a **factor of 2.3 improvement** over fixed bias (where $S_I = 1$).

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSING MISTAKES (from past KTU valuation keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting to discard the invalid root** of the quadratic (the one giving $V_{GS} < V_t$). *Cost: 1ŌĆō2 marks.*
> 2. **Sign error in self-bias**: $V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S$, NOT $+I_{DQ} R_S$. Many students lose a full mark here.
> 3. **Mixing up $K_n$ (conduction parameter) with $\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)$**: $K_n = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)$. Using the wrong one gives 2├Ś error.
> 4. **Not verifying saturation**: The Q-point is meaningless if the MOSFET is in triode. Always check $V_{DSQ} \geq V_{OV}$.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers: state $I_{DQ}$ in mA or ╬╝A, $V_{GSQ}$ in V, etc. Examiners deduct for sloppy units.
> 6. **Using $\lambda \neq 0$ in Q-point calculations** when it is not given: KTU hand calculations usually assume $\lambda = 0$ unless explicitly asked otherwise.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö Biasing of Discrete MOSFET Amplifier**

- **MOSFET biasing** establishes a stable Q-point in the **saturation region**: $I_{DQ} = K_n (V_{GSQ} - V_t)^2$.
- **Four standard biasing circuits**: Fixed bias, Self-bias, Voltage-divider bias, Drain-feedback bias.
- **Voltage-divider bias is the most commonly used** ŌĆö it offers the best stability-to-complexity trade-off.
- **Self-bias uses only $R_S$** for feedback ŌĆö simplest but may not be able to bias all devices.
- **Stability factor $S_I$**: smaller is better. $S_I = 1/(1 + g_{mQ} R_S)$ for self-bias.
- **Transconductance at Q-point**: $g_{mQ} = 2\sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}} = 2 K_n V_{OV}$.
- **Saturation check**: $V_{DSQ} \geq V_{OV} = V_{GSQ} - V_t$ ŌĆö **always verify this!**
- **Output resistance (with $\lambda$)**: $r_{ds} = 1/(\lambda I_{DQ})$ ŌĆö often neglected in DC biasing problems.
- **Quadratic in $I_{DQ}$**: Always check discriminant; **always discard the unphysical root**.
- **Design trade-off**: Larger $R_S$ ŌåÆ better stability, but smaller output swing (reduced $V_{DSQ}$).
- **Typical $V_{OV}$ for design**: $0.2 V_{DD} \leq V_{OV} \leq 0.5 V_{DD}$ for good headroom.
- **Gate is insulated**: $I_G = 0$ in DC analysis ŌĆö this is the key simplification for MOSFET biasing.
- **Two supply vs. one supply**: Fixed bias needs a separate $V_{GG}$ supply; the other three use only $V_{DD}$.
- **KTU focus**: Voltage-divider and self-bias appear in 80% of exam problems ŌĆö master them first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Source (CS) Configuration**: A field-effect transistor (FET/MOSFET) amplifier topology in which the **input signal is applied between the Gate and Source**, and the **amplified output is taken between the Drain and Source**, making the Source terminal *common* to both input and output ports (typically AC-grounded).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Small-Signal Hybrid-Pi (ŽĆ) Model**: A linear, AC-equivalent circuit model of a MOSFET that is valid when the input signal causes only small perturbations around a fixed DC operating point (Q-point). It replaces the nonlinear transistor with a linear network consisting of a voltage-controlled current source **$g_m v_{gs}$**, an output resistance **$r_o$**, and parasitic capacitances **$C_{gs}$** and **$C_{gd}$**.

The **purpose** of small-signal analysis is to compute three key AC performance metrics of the CS amplifier:
- **Voltage Gain ($A_v$)**
- **Input Resistance ($R_i$)**
- **Output Resistance ($R_o$)**

These parameters are extracted at mid-band frequencies (where coupling and bypass capacitors act as AC short circuits, and parasitic capacitances are open-circuited).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Think of a MOSFET as a **water tap (faucet)**:
- The **Gate** is the *handle* you turn. A tiny twist (small input voltage $v_{gs}$) controls a huge flow of water.
- The **Drain** is the *spout* where the water (current $i_d$) flows out.
- The **Source** is the *pipe base* (grounded for the CS configuration).

> [!TIP]
> **The Hybrid-Pi Model in Plain English**: Imagine the MOSFET as a "smart current valve." The model says: *"For every small wiggle at the gate ($v_{gs}$), the device pushes out a current equal to $g_m \times v_{gs}$ through the drain, while leaking a tiny bit through the internal resistance $r_o$."* This is exactly the function of the **voltage-controlled current source** in the small-signal model.

The **hybrid-ŽĆ model** is called "hybrid" because it mixes a current source (output side) with a voltage input (gate side), and "ŽĆ" because the parasitic capacitances form a shape resembling the Greek letter **ŽĆ**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumptions for Small-Signal Validity**:
> 1. $|v_{gs}| \ll 2(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ ŌĆö small-signal condition ensures linearity.
> 2. **Body effect is ignored** (source is at AC ground ŌåÆ $v_{bs} = 0$).
> 3. Mid-band frequency analysis: **$C_{in}, C_{out}, C_S$ ŌåÆ short circuit**, and **$C_{gs}, C_{gd}$ ŌåÆ open circuit**.
> 4. The MOSFET is biased in the **saturation region** for proper amplification.

Standard values used:
- **$g_m$** = transconductance (in Siemens, **S** or **A/V**)
- **$r_o$** = output resistance of MOSFET (typically **k╬® to M╬®**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Small-Signal Analysis

The analysis follows a strict KTU-expected procedure:

1. **Draw the DC bias circuit** and locate the Q-point ($I_D$, $V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$). Verify $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ (saturation).
2. **Compute the small-signal parameters** ($g_m$, $r_o$) at the Q-point.
3. **Replace the MOSFET with its hybrid-ŽĆ equivalent** in the AC equivalent circuit.
4. **Short all DC sources** (since they carry no AC variation) and **short all coupling/bypass capacitors** at mid-band.
5. **Apply KVL/KCL** to the linear AC circuit to derive $A_v$, $R_i$, $R_o$.
6. **Attach load $R_L$** (if external) in parallel with $R_D$ for loaded gain computation.

---

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Step 1ŌĆō2**: The hybrid-ŽĆ model parameters are *linearized around the Q-point*. Without a known bias, $g_m$ and $r_o$ cannot be computed.
- **Step 3**: The model is a *linearization* of the nonlinear Shockley equation. It is mathematically valid *only* for small perturbations.
- **Step 4**: Coupling capacitors block DC but pass AC. At mid-band, their reactance $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ is negligible, so they behave as wires.
- **Step 5**: Once linearized, Ohm's and Kirchhoff's laws apply directly.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transconductance $g_m$ | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | A/V (S) | Depends on Q-point current $I_D$ |
| Output resistance $r_o$ | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}$ | ╬® (k╬® range) | $\lambda$ = channel-length modulation |
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ (unloaded) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | dimensionless | Negative sign = 180┬░ phase inversion |
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ (loaded) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$ | dimensionless | With external load $R_L$ |
| Input Resistance $R_i$ | $R_i = R_G$ (or $\infty$ for ideal MOSFET) | ╬® | Set by gate-bias network |
| Output Resistance $R_o$ | $R_o = R_D \parallel r_o$ | ╬® | Looking into drain with $v_{in} = 0$ |
| Overall Voltage Gain $G_v$ | $G_v = A_v \cdot \dfrac{R_{in,amp}}{R_S + R_{in,amp}}$ | dimensionless | Includes source resistance $R_S$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention**: In a CS amplifier, the output is **180┬░ out of phase** with the input. This is indicated by the **negative sign** in $A_v$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is the CS Amplifier Used?**
> - **First stage of nearly every op-amp**, microphone preamplifier, and RF front-end.
> - Provides **high input impedance** (ideal for sensor interfacing, e.g., ECG, EEG electrodes).
> - Forms the **building block of CMOS digital inverters** and analog ICs.
> - Used in **mixers, oscillators, and voltage amplifiers** in 5G/IoT transceiver chips.

The hybrid-ŽĆ model is the universal language used by **SPICE, Cadence, and ADS** simulators to evaluate transistor performance at high frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Voltage Gain $A_v$ for the Basic CS Amplifier

### Step 1: The AC Equivalent Circuit (Mid-Band)

At mid-band, the coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are short circuits. The MOSFET is replaced by its hybrid-ŽĆ model: a current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$.

The drain is connected to $R_D$ (which goes to $V_{DD}$, now AC-grounded), and the source is at ground.

### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Drain Node

Let $v_{out}$ be the output voltage at the drain. The current leaving the drain through $R_D$ is $v_{out}/R_D$. The current leaving through $r_o$ is $v_{out}/r_o$. The current source pushes $g_m v_{gs}$ *into* the drain node.

$$
i_d = g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o}
$$

The KCL equation at the drain node gives:

$$
g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + \frac{v_{out}}{R_D} = 0
$$

### Step 3: Recognize that $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (Source is Grounded)

Since the source terminal is at AC ground, the gate-to-source voltage equals the input voltage:

$$
v_{gs} = v_{in} - 0 = v_{in}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $v_{out}$ in Terms of $v_{in}$

$$
g_m v_{in} = -v_{out} \left( \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o} \right)
$$

$$
v_{out} = -g_m v_{in} \cdot \left( R_D \parallel r_o \right)
$$

### Step 5: Define Voltage Gain

$$
A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result 1**: $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ ŌĆö the **fundamental CS amplifier gain** (without external load).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Gain with External Load $R_L$

When a load resistor $R_L$ is connected through $C_{out}$, it appears in parallel with $R_D$ and $r_o$ at the drain node (all share the same voltage $v_{out}$).

The equivalent resistance at the drain becomes:

$$
R_{eq} = R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L
$$

Following the identical KCL procedure:

$$
A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Loading the amplifier **reduces** the gain magnitude because $R_{eq} < (R_D \parallel r_o)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Input Resistance $R_i$

The gate of an ideal MOSFET draws **no DC current** because of the insulating SiOŌéé layer. In the hybrid-ŽĆ model, the gate is an open circuit.

Therefore, the input resistance of the CS amplifier is set entirely by the bias network:

$$
R_i = R_G
$$

(where $R_G$ is the gate-bias resistor, e.g., a high-value resistor of $1\,\text{M}\Omega$ or more).

For an ideal MOSFET:

$$
R_i \to \infty
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Output Resistance $R_o$

To find $R_o$, we apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output, set $v_{in} = 0$ (input shorted to ground), and measure the current $i_x$ entering the output port.

Since $v_{in} = 0$, the controlling voltage $v_{gs} = 0$, and the dependent current source $g_m v_{gs} = 0$ becomes an **open circuit**.

Looking into the drain, we see only $r_o$ in parallel with $R_D$:

$$
R_o = R_D \parallel r_o
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Useful Approximation**: If $r_o \gg R_D$ (a common condition when $\lambda$ is small), then $R_o \approx R_D$ and $A_v \approx -g_m R_D$.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Parameter Summary

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Expression** | **Typical Direction** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small-signal transconductance | $g_m$ | $g_m = 2 I_D / (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ | Set by bias |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ | $\uparrow$ as $I_D \downarrow$ |
| Mid-band voltage gain (unloaded) | $A_v$ | $-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | Negative, large |
| Mid-band voltage gain (loaded) | $A_v$ | $-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$ | Smaller magnitude |
| Input resistance | $R_i$ | $R_G$ (or $\infty$) | Very high |
| Output resistance | $R_o$ | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | Moderate |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem**: A CS amplifier uses a MOSFET with $I_D = 0.5\,\text{mA}$, $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 0.5\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $\lambda = 0.01\,\text{V}^{-1}$. Compute $g_m$, $r_o$, and $A_v$ (unloaded). Also compute $A_v$ when $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$ is connected.

**Solution**:

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}} = \frac{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5} = 2\,\text{mA/V} = 2\,\text{mS}
$$

$$
r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.01 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -2 \times 10^{-3} \times (4.7\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 200\,\text{k}\Omega)
$$

$$
R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{4.7 \times 200}{4.7 + 200} = \frac{940}{204.7} \approx 4.59\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
A_v \approx -2 \times 4.59 = -9.19\,\text{V/V}
$$

When loaded with $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$
R_{eq} = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 200\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 2.27\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
A_{v,\text{loaded}} = -2 \times 2.27 = -4.54\,\text{V/V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation**: The gain dropped from $\approx -9.19$ to $\approx -4.54$ ŌĆö nearly **halved** when the load matches the drain resistor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CS Amplifier Signal Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **signal flow** through the small-signal CS amplifier, mapped to its hybrid-ŽĆ equivalent:

```mermaid
graph TD
    sigin[Vin: Input Signal Source] --> c1[Coupling Capacitor Cin: AC Pass, DC Block]
    c1 --> gatenode[Gate Node of MOSFET]
    gatenode --> rg[R_G: High Value Gate Bias Resistor]
    rg --> gnd1[AC Ground]
    gatenode --> cgs[C_gs: Parasitic Gate Source Capacitance]
    cgs --> sourcenode[Source Node: AC Grounded]
    sourcenode --> gnd2[AC Ground]
    gatenode --> cgd[C_gd: Parasitic Gate Drain Capacitance]
    cgd --> drainnode[Drain Node of MOSFET]
    gatenode --> vctrl[Controlling Voltage v_gs]
    vctrl --> ccvs[Current Source: g_m times v_gs]
    ccvs --> drainnode
    drainnode --> ro[r_o: Channel Output Resistance]
    ro --> sourcenode
    drainnode --> rd[R_D: Drain Bias Resistor]
    rd --> vdd[AC Ground via V_DD]
    drainnode --> c2[Coupling Capacitor C_out]
    c2 --> rl[R_L: External Load]
    rl --> outport[V_out: Output to Next Stage]
    drainnode --> rout[Output Resistance R_o equals R_D parallel r_o]
    rout --> outport
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Follow the arrows from `sigin` to `outport`. The control path (`vctrl ŌåÆ ccvs`) shows how the small input voltage modulates the output current, which in turn produces the output voltage across the parallel combination of $R_D$ and $r_o$.

---

## 4.2 The Hybrid-Pi Model (Standalone View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    g[G: Gate] --- cgs2[C_gs]
    cgs2 --- s[S: Source]
    g --- cgd2[C_gd]
    cgd2 --- d[D: Drain]
    cgs2 --- gmctrl[Voltage v_gs]
    gmctrl --> gmcccs[Dependent Current Source: g_m times v_gs]
    gmcccs --> d
    ro2[r_o: Output Resistance] --- d
    ro2 --- s
    s --- gnd3[Common Source: Ground Reference]
```

---

## 4.3 Mid-Band AC Equivalent Circuit (Simplified)

```mermaid
graph TD
    vin2[Vin: AC Source] --- rg2[R_G: Gate Resistor]
    rg2 --- gate2[G: Gate of Hybrid Pi Model]
    gate2 --- vgsctrl[Voltage v_gs across Gate Source]
    vgsctrl --> gmcccs2[Current Source: g_m times v_gs]
    gmcccs2 --- drain2[D: Drain Node]
    ro3[r_o] --- drain2
    ro3 --- src2[S: Source AC Ground]
    drain2 --- rd2[R_D to AC Ground]
    drain2 --- vout2[V_out: Output Voltage]
    src2 --- gnd4[Ground]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mid-Band Simplification Rule**: In these mid-band diagrams, the parasitic capacitances $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ are **omitted** (treated as open circuits) because at mid-band frequencies their reactance is extremely large.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions** (KTU Pattern): Answer in 2ŌĆō3 sentences with a clear diagram or formula. Each carries **3 marks**.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Draw the small-signal hybrid-ŽĆ model of a MOSFET and label each component. Why is it called "small-signal"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The hybrid-ŽĆ model consists of a **voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$** at the output, an **output resistance $r_o$** in parallel with it, and **parasitic capacitances $C_{gs}$ (gate-to-source)** and **$C_{gd}$ (gate-to-drain)**. The gate and source are the input terminals (gate is the controlling node).

It is called "small-signal" because the model is valid only when the input signal causes **small perturbations** around the DC Q-point, ensuring the device operates in its **linear region** where superposition applies. If the signal were large, the nonlinear $i_D$ŌĆō$v_{GS}$ relationship of the MOSFET would invalidate the linearized model. **[3 marks]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** In a CS amplifier, why is the input resistance ideally infinite? What sets the practical value?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ideally, the input resistance is **infinite** because the MOSFET gate is **insulated from the channel** by a thin SiOŌéé layer, which prevents any DC gate current from flowing. Hence, the gate draws **no current**, making $R_i \to \infty$.

In practice, the input resistance is set by the **gate-bias resistor $R_G$** (typically $1\,\text{M}\Omega$ to $10\,\text{M}\Omega$) connected from gate to ground, so $R_i = R_G$. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern**: Each Part B question has internal choice. Two alternatives are given below. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Part B ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** For a Common Source (CS) MOSFET amplifier using the small-signal hybrid-ŽĆ model:

**(a)** Draw the mid-band AC equivalent circuit using the hybrid-ŽĆ model and derive the expression for **voltage gain $A_v$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **input resistance $R_i$** and **output resistance $R_o$**. What is the effect of the external load $R_L$ on voltage gain? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**:

1. Draw the AC equivalent: input $v_{in}$ applied to gate, MOSFET replaced by hybrid-ŽĆ ($g_m v_{gs}$ parallel with $r_o$), $R_D$ from drain to AC ground, source grounded. **[1 mark]**
2. Identify $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (since source is at AC ground). **[1 mark]**
3. Apply KCL at the drain node. The current source $g_m v_{gs}$ flows out of the drain (into the parallel combination of $R_D$ and $r_o$): **[1 mark]**

$$
g_m v_{gs} = -v_{out} \left( \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o} \right)
$$

4. Substitute $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ and rearrange: **[2 marks]**

$$
A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)
$$

5. State the **sign convention**: The negative sign indicates a **180┬░ phase shift** between input and output, which is a hallmark of CS configuration. **[2 marks]**

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**:

1. **Input Resistance**: Since the gate draws no current, $R_i$ is determined solely by the bias network. In a typical CS amplifier, $R_i = R_G$ (very high, often $\gg 1\,\text{M}\Omega$). **[2 marks]**
2. **Output Resistance**: Set $v_{in} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{gs} = 0 \Rightarrow$ current source becomes open. Looking into the drain, we see $R_D \parallel r_o$. So $R_o = R_D \parallel r_o$. **[2 marks]**
3. **Effect of $R_L$**: When load $R_L$ is connected (via $C_{out}$), it appears in parallel with $R_D$ and $r_o$ at the drain. The new effective load is $R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L$, and the loaded gain becomes $A_{v,\text{loaded}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$. Since adding a parallel resistor always reduces the equivalent resistance, **the magnitude of the voltage gain decreases** with loading. **[3 marks]**

---

### **Part B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A CS amplifier is biased with $I_D = 1\,\text{mA}$, $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$.

**(a)** Calculate the small-signal parameters $g_m$ and $r_o$. Hence determine the **unloaded voltage gain**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If a load $R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ is connected, compute the **loaded voltage gain** and the **output resistance $R_o$**. Also compute the input resistance if the gate resistor $R_G = 2\,\text{M}\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**:

1. **Transconductance $g_m$**: **[2 marks]**

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}} = \frac{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 2\,\text{mA/V} = 2\,\text{mS}
$$

2. **Output resistance $r_o$**: **[2 marks]**

$$
r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

3. **Unloaded voltage gain**: **[3 marks]**

$$
A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -2 \times 10^{-3} \times (5\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 50\,\text{k}\Omega)
$$

$$
R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{5 \times 50}{5 + 50} = \frac{250}{55} \approx 4.545\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
A_v = -2 \times 4.545 = -9.09\,\text{V/V}
$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**:

1. **Loaded voltage gain**: With $R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ added in parallel: **[2 marks]**

$$
R_{eq} = R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 50\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 5\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{5} = \frac{10 + 1 + 10}{50} = \frac{21}{50}
$$

$$
R_{eq} = \frac{50}{21} \approx 2.38\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
A_{v,\text{loaded}} = -g_m R_{eq} = -2 \times 2.38 = -4.76\,\text{V/V}
$$

2. **Output resistance $R_o$**: **[2 marks]**

$$
R_o = R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{5 \times 50}{5 + 50} = 4.545\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

3. **Input resistance $R_i$**: **[3 marks]**

$$
R_i = R_G = 2\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

(The MOSFET gate itself draws no current, so $R_i$ is set entirely by the external gate-bias resistor.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $A_v$: The CS amplifier **inverts** the signal. A positive value indicates a serious conceptual error.
> 2. **Ignoring $r_o$**: Although $r_o$ is often large, it must be included in parallel with $R_D$ for full marks. KTU examiners specifically look for the term "$R_D \parallel r_o$".
> 3. **Mixing up $g_m$ formulas**: $g_m = 2 I_D/(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ is **not** the same as $g_m = k_n (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$. Use the form consistent with the given parameters.
> 4. **Confusing $A_v$ with overall gain $G_v$**: $A_v$ is the intrinsic amplifier gain; $G_v$ accounts for source resistance loading.
> 5. **Forgetting units**: $g_m$ is in **A/V** or **S** (Siemens). $A_v$ is **dimensionless** (V/V).
> 6. **Assuming $R_i = \infty$ without stating the gate-bias resistor**: Always mention $R_G$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- Ō£ģ The **CS configuration** has input at gate, output at drain, source AC-grounded.
- Ō£ģ The **hybrid-ŽĆ model** linearizes the MOSFET around the Q-point and is valid only for small signals.
- Ō£ģ Key elements of the model: **$g_m v_{gs}$ (VCCS)**, **$r_o$ (output resistance)**, **$C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$** (parasitic caps).
- Ō£ģ **Mid-band analysis**: Coupling/bypass capacitors ŌåÆ short; parasitic capacitors ŌåÆ open.
- Ō£ģ **Voltage gain** (unloaded): $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$.
- Ō£ģ **Voltage gain** (loaded with $R_L$): $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$.
- Ō£ģ **Input resistance** $R_i = R_G$ (ideal MOSFET: $R_i \to \infty$).
- Ō£ģ **Output resistance** $R_o = R_D \parallel r_o$.
- Ō£ģ The **negative sign** denotes a **180┬░ phase inversion** between input and output.
- Ō£ģ **$g_m$** depends on the Q-point: $g_m = 2 I_D / (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$.
- Ō£ģ **$r_o$** depends on channel-length modulation: $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$.
- Ō£ģ The CS amplifier is the **workhorse of analog IC design** ŌĆö used in op-amp input stages, RF amplifiers, and sensor interfaces.
- Ō£ģ The **transistor's amplification is fundamentally transconductance-based**: voltage in ŌåÆ current out ŌåÆ voltage out via load resistor.
- Ō£ģ **Always verify the Q-point is in saturation** before applying the small-signal model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Small Signal Voltage and Current Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Small-Signal Analysis** is the linearized AC analysis of a transistor amplifier performed around a fixed DC quiescent operating point (Q-point). Because the transistor is inherently a **non-linear** device, we approximate its behavior as linear for infinitesimally small AC perturbations by replacing the device with its **small-signal equivalent model** (Hybrid-ŽĆ model for BJT / Small-signal model for FET).

The **Small-Signal Voltage Gain ($A_v$)** is defined as the ratio of the output signal voltage to the input signal voltage:

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i}$$

The **Small-Signal Current Gain ($A_i$)** is defined as the ratio of the output signal current delivered to the load to the input signal current drawn from the source:

$$A_i = \frac{i_o}{i_i}$$

These gains are evaluated assuming the signal amplitude is small enough (typically $\leq \mathbf{10\,mV}$ for BJT) so that the transistor's $i$ŌĆō$v$ characteristics can be replaced by their **tangent lines** at the Q-point.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Taped Valve on a Water Pipe**
> Imagine a large water valve. You first tighten the valve to a specific opening using a wrench (this is the **DC bias** ŌĆö sets the Q-point). Once the valve is fixed at that opening, you now tap the valve handle *gently* with your finger. Your tiny tap (the **small AC signal**) makes the water flow fluctuate slightly. Because of the pipe geometry, a small movement of the handle produces a *much larger* change in water flow ŌĆö this amplification is the **gain**.

In a transistor, the **DC bias** sets the operating current $I_C$, and the **small AC signal** $v_i$ superimposed on it produces a proportional amplified output current $i_c$. The ratio between output and input is the gain.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T \approx \mathbf{26\,mV}$ at **300 K** (room temperature)
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = I_C / V_T$ ŌĆö units of **Siemens (S)** or **A/V**
- **Base-emitter dynamic resistance:** $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ ŌĆö units of **ohms ($\Omega$)**
- **Forward current gain:** $\beta$ (dimensionless, typically **100ŌĆō400** for small-signal BJTs)
- Standard Q-point current range: $I_C = \mathbf{0.1\,mA}$ to $\mathbf{10\,mA}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The derivation of voltage and current gain follows a structured 6-step procedure used universally in KTU board examinations:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö DC Analysis:** Compute the Q-point ($I_C$, $V_{CE}$) using KVL/KCL on the bias network. This step is **performed once** and sets the operating point.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö AC Analysis Preparation:** Deactivate all DC sources (set $V_{CC} = 0$). Replace coupling and bypass capacitors by **short circuits** (because their reactance $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C) \to 0$ at signal frequency).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Model Substitution:** Replace the BJT with its **small-signal Hybrid-ŽĆ equivalent circuit** containing the dependent current source $g_m v_\pi$, input resistance $r_\pi$, and output resistance $r_o$ (often neglected in basic analysis).
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Apply KVL/KCL on the AC equivalent circuit:** Write loop or nodal equations involving $v_i$, $v_\pi$, $i_b$, $i_c$, and $v_o$.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Voltage Gain:** Compute $A_v = v_o / v_i$ by algebraic substitution.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Current Gain:** Compute $A_i = i_o / i_i$ by considering the load resistor $R_L$ and source loading.

## 2.2 The Hybrid-ŽĆ Small-Signal Model (BJT)

The Hybrid-ŽĆ model is the **canonical small-signal model** for BJTs approved by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Element | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input resistance | $r_\pi$ | $\beta / g_m$ | Resistance seen looking into the base |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | $V_A / I_C$ | Finite output impedance due to Early effect |
| Dependent source | $g_m v_\pi$ | ŌĆö | Voltage-controlled current source |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $I_C / V_T$ | Converts input voltage to output current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The relationship $\beta = g_m \cdot r_\pi$ is *the most frequently tested identity* in Module 1. Memorize this on day one.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (CE Amplifier Configurations)

> [!NOTE]
> Below: complete cheat sheet of gains and resistances for the **Common-Emitter (CE)** amplifier ŌĆö the most heavily tested topology in KTU examinations. Use $\vert$ notation carefully to avoid table-breaking pipe symbols.

| Parameter | CE (Emitter Bypassed) | CE (Emitter Unbypassed $R_E$) |
|---|---|---|
| Input Resistance $R_{in}$ | $R_B \;\Vert\; r_\pi$ | $R_B \;\Vert\; \left[r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E\right]$ |
| Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | $R_C \;\Vert\; r_o$ | $R_C \;\Vert\; r_o$ |
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ (no load) | $-g_m R_C$ | $\dfrac{-g_m R_C \, r_\pi}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E} = \dfrac{-\beta R_C}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$ |
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ (with $R_L$) | $-g_m (R_C \;\Vert\; R_L)$ | $\dfrac{-\beta (R_C \;\Vert\; R_L)}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$ |
| Current Gain $A_i$ (ideal) | $-\beta$ | $\dfrac{-\beta R_B}{R_B + r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$ |

For the **Common-Base (CB)** amplifier (also in KTU syllabus): $A_v \approx g_m R_C$ (positive, non-inverting), $A_i \approx -1$ (current follower).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio pre-amplifiers:** CE stages provide high voltage gain (~50ŌĆō500) for microphone signals.
- **RF amplifiers:** Voltage gain determines signal strength in radio receivers.
- **Operational amplifier input stages:** Differential pairs use matched CE stages.
- **Sensor signal conditioning:** Small signals from thermocouples/photodiodes require predictable linear gain ŌĆö exactly what small-signal analysis guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier (Emitter Bypassed)

**Starting Circuit:** A standard CE amplifier with bias resistors $R_1, R_2$, collector resistor $R_C$, and emitter resistor $R_E$ fully bypassed by capacitor $C_E$ at signal frequency.

### Step A ŌĆö Draw the AC Equivalent Circuit

After shorting $V_{CC}$ and shorting the coupling/bypass capacitors at signal frequency:
- $R_B = R_1 \Vert R_2$ appears in parallel with the base
- $R_C$ appears between collector and ground
- $R_E$ is short-circuited by $C_E$, so emitter is at AC ground

The input voltage source $v_i$ drives the base through some source resistance $R_S$ (assume ideal source for first derivation, so $v_i$ directly at base).

### Step B ŌĆö Identify the controlling voltage $v_\pi$

The emitter is at AC ground, so the full input voltage appears across the base-emitter junction:

$$v_\pi = v_i$$

### Step C ŌĆö Find the collector current $i_c$

The dependent current source produces:

$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_\pi = g_m \cdot v_i$$

### Step D ŌĆö Find the output voltage $v_o$

The collector current flows through $R_C$ (and $R_L$ if present) producing a voltage drop. By convention, $v_o$ is measured at the collector node with respect to ground. Since current flows *into* the collector node from $R_C$ towards $V_{CC}$ (now ground), and $i_c$ flows *out* of the collector into the device, the output voltage is:

$$v_o = -i_c \cdot (R_C \Vert R_L)$$

For the unloaded case ($R_L \to \infty$):

$$v_o = -i_c \cdot R_C = -g_m v_i R_C$$

### Step E ŌĆö Compute Voltage Gain

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{-g_m v_i R_C}{v_i}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m R_C = -\frac{\beta R_C}{r_\pi}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **180┬░ phase inversion** ŌĆö a hallmark of CE topology. This is the most important result of Module 1.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier (Emitter NOT Bypassed)

Now assume the bypass capacitor is removed. The emitter resistor $R_E$ is now present in the AC circuit.

### Step A ŌĆö Identify $v_\pi$

The input voltage $v_i$ is distributed across $r_\pi$ **and** $R_E$ (because the emitter current $i_e$ flows through $R_E$):

$$v_i = v_\pi + i_e \cdot R_E$$

### Step B ŌĆö Express $i_e$ in terms of $v_\pi$

Since $i_e = i_b + i_c = i_b + \beta i_b = (\beta+1) i_b$, and $v_\pi = i_b \cdot r_\pi$, so $i_b = v_\pi / r_\pi$:

$$i_e = \frac{(\beta+1)}{r_\pi} \cdot v_\pi$$

### Step C ŌĆö Substitute back into the KVL equation

$$v_i = v_\pi + \frac{(\beta+1)}{r_\pi} \cdot v_\pi \cdot R_E$$

$$v_i = v_\pi \left[ 1 + \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi} \right]$$

$$v_i = v_\pi \left[ \frac{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi} \right]$$

Therefore:

$$v_\pi = \frac{r_\pi \cdot v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

### Step D ŌĆö Output voltage and voltage gain

$$v_o = -g_m v_\pi \cdot R_C = -g_m R_C \cdot \frac{r_\pi \cdot v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{-g_m R_C \, r_\pi}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

Using the identity $\beta = g_m r_\pi$, we get the **alternate form**:

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{-\beta R_C}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Case Verification:** If $R_E \to 0$, then $A_v \to -\beta R_C / r_\pi = -g_m R_C$ Ō£ō (matches the bypassed case). This validates our derivation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Current Gain $A_i$ of CE Amplifier

### Step A ŌĆö Define input and output currents

Input current (delivered by source):

$$i_i = \frac{v_i}{R_{in,total}}$$

where $R_{in,total} = R_B \Vert R_{in,base}$ and $R_{in,base} = r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$.

Output current (delivered to load):

$$i_o = -i_c \cdot \frac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$$

The negative sign indicates direction (current flows out of the collector through $R_L$). The current divider gives the fraction of $i_c$ that goes through $R_L$.

### Step B ŌĆö Express $i_c$ in terms of $v_i$

From Step C of the previous derivation:

$$i_b = \frac{v_\pi}{r_\pi} = \frac{v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$$

$$i_c = \beta i_b = \frac{\beta \, v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$$

### Step C ŌĆö Form the ratio $A_i = i_o / i_i$

$$i_o = -\frac{\beta v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E} \cdot \frac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$$

For the input current, with $R_S$ neglected and the source directly driving $R_{in,base}$:

$$i_i \approx \frac{v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$$

Therefore:

$$A_i = \frac{i_o}{i_i} = \frac{-\beta v_i \cdot R_C / [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E] \cdot 1/(R_C+R_L)}{v_i / [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E]}$$

$$\boxed{A_i = \frac{-\beta \, R_C}{R_C + R_L}}$$

### Step D ŌĆö Special Case: $R_L \to \infty$ (no load)

$$A_i \to -\beta$$

This is the **intrinsic short-circuit current gain** of the transistor ŌĆö a fundamental device parameter. The negative sign indicates current inversion.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Configuration | Voltage Gain $A_v$ | Current Gain $A_i$ | Phase | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CE (bypassed) | $-g_m R_C$ | $-\beta R_C/(R_C+R_L)$ | 180┬░ | Very High |
| CE (unbypassed) | $-\beta R_C/[r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E]$ | Same as above | 180┬░ | Very High |
| CB | $+g_m R_C$ | $\approx -1$ | 0┬░ | Moderate |
| CC (Emitter Follower) | $\approx 1$ | $\approx \beta$ | 0┬░ | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Small-Signal Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Amplifier Circuit] --> B[Step 1: DC Analysis to find Q-point IC and VCE]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute small-signal parameters gm, rpi, ro]
    C --> D[Step 3: Deactivate DC sources Set VCC to zero]
    D --> E[Step 4: Replace coupling and bypass capacitors with short circuits at signal frequency]
    E --> F[Step 5: Substitute BJT with Hybrid-pi equivalent model]
    F --> G[Step 6: Apply KVL and KCL on AC equivalent circuit]
    G --> H[Step 7: Derive vi equation in terms of vpi]
    H --> I[Step 8: Compute output vo from dependent source current]
    I --> J[Step 9: Form ratio Av equals vo divided by vi]
    J --> K[Step 10: Form ratio Ai equals io divided by ii]
    K --> L[End: Validate using limiting cases and sign conventions]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic ŌĆö Hybrid-ŽĆ Equivalent Circuit (CE Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input vi] --> N1[Base node b]
    N1 --> RPI[rpi resistance]
    RPI --> N2[Internal node E prime]
    N2 --> GND1[Ground]
    N1 -. vpi across rpi .-> RPI
    N1 -. controls .-> GMSRC[g_m times vpi dependent current source]
    GMSRC --> N3[Collector node c]
    N3 --> RC[RC collector resistor]
    RC --> GND2[Ground]
    N3 --> OUT[Output vo]
    OUT --> RL[Load RL]
    RL --> GND3[Ground]
    N3 -. vo measured here .-> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Signal Flow Block Diagram ŌĆö Gain Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Input Source vs Rs] --> CIN[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    CIN --> AMP[Common Emitter Amplifier Stage with Av and Rin and Rout]
    AMP --> COUT[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    COUT --> LOAD[Load RL]
    AMP -. feedback .- FB[Emitter Resistor RE with optional bypass CE]
    FB --> AMP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows represent the *direction of signal flow*. The dotted line from the emitter resistor to the amplifier block indicates the **negative feedback path** that reduces gain but improves linearity ŌĆö a key trade-off tested in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**Define small-signal voltage gain of a BJT amplifier. Why is it called "small-signal"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ is defined as the ratio of output signal voltage to input signal voltage under AC conditions, evaluated at the Q-point. It is expressed as $A_v = v_o / v_i$. The term **"small-signal"** refers to the assumption that the AC input signal amplitude is small enough (typically $\leq 10\,\text{mV}$) that the transistor operates over a *linear* segment of its $i_C$ŌĆō$v_{BE}$ characteristic. Under this condition, the non-linear transistor can be replaced by a **linear small-signal equivalent model** (Hybrid-ŽĆ), making mathematical analysis tractable using superposition and Ohm's law. If the signal were large, the non-linear curvature would distort the output, invalidating the linear model. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**State the relationship between $\beta$, $g_m$, and $r_\pi$ for a BJT. Why is $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fundamental relationship is $\beta = g_m \cdot r_\pi$, where $\beta$ is the dimensionless forward current gain, $g_m = I_C / V_T$ is the transconductance (S), and $r_\pi = V_T / I_B$ is the base-emitter dynamic input resistance ($\Omega$). The thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q$ evaluates to approximately **26 mV at 300 K** (room temperature), where $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $q$ is the electron charge. This value is used because it sets the *scale* of the base-emitter junction's exponential $i$ŌĆō$v$ behavior, and it is approximately constant for typical indoor laboratory conditions. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper]
**(a)** Draw the small-signal Hybrid-ŽĆ equivalent circuit of a BJT in CE configuration. Derive the expression for voltage gain $A_v$ when the emitter resistor is **fully bypassed** by a capacitor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CE amplifier has $I_C = 2\,\text{mA}$, $\beta = 100$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $R_E$ is bypassed. Compute $g_m$, $r_\pi$, and $A_v$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the circuit diagram. The derivation proceeds as:

From the AC equivalent circuit (bypass capacitor shorts $R_E$):
$$v_\pi = v_i$$
$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_\pi = g_m \cdot v_i$$
$$v_o = -i_c \cdot R_C = -g_m v_i R_C$$
$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m R_C = -\frac{\beta R_C}{r_\pi}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180┬░ phase reversal** between input and output. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Given: $I_C = 2\,\text{mA} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$, $\beta = 100$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$.

**Step 1: Compute $g_m$:**
$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0769\,\text{S} = 76.9\,\text{mS}$$

**Step 2: Compute $r_\pi$:**
$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.0769} = 1300\,\Omega = 1.3\,\text{k}\Omega$$

(Verification: $r_\pi = V_T / I_B = 26\,\text{mV} / (I_C/\beta) = 26\,\text{mV} / 20\,\mu\text{A} = 1300\,\Omega$ Ō£ō)

**Step 3: Compute $A_v$:**
$$A_v = -g_m R_C = -(0.0769)(4700) = -361.5$$

In decibels: $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(361.5) = \mathbf{51.15\,\text{dB}}$ **(7 marks)**

### Question B: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Alternative Choice]
**(a)** Derive the expression for the voltage gain $A_v$ of a CE amplifier when the emitter resistor $R_E$ is **NOT bypassed** by any capacitor. Show that your result reduces to the bypassed case as $R_E \to 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CE amplifier with $R_C = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 470\,\Omega$, $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$, $\beta = 150$, and load $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, compute the voltage gain with and without the bypass capacitor. Comment on the role of $R_E$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** From Section 3.2 of these notes:
$$v_i = v_\pi + i_e R_E = v_\pi + \frac{(\beta+1)}{r_\pi} v_\pi R_E$$
$$v_\pi = \frac{r_\pi v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$$
$$v_o = -g_m v_\pi R_C$$
$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{-\beta R_C}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}}$$

**Limiting case:** As $R_E \to 0$, denominator $\to r_\pi$, giving $A_v \to -\beta R_C / r_\pi = -g_m R_C$. Ō£ō **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Given: $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$, $\beta = 150$, $R_C = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 470\,\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1: Compute small-signal parameters.**
$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1\,\text{mA}}{26\,\text{mV}} = 38.46\,\text{mS}$$
$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{150}{0.03846} = 3900\,\Omega = 3.9\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Compute $A_v$ with bypass capacitor.**
$$R_C' = R_C \Vert R_L = \frac{2.2 \times 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7} = \frac{10.34}{6.9} = 1.498\,\text{k}\Omega$$
$$A_v^{\text{bypassed}} = -g_m R_C' = -(0.03846)(1498) = -57.6$$

**Step 3: Compute $A_v$ without bypass capacitor.**
$$(\beta+1)R_E = 151 \times 470 = 70970\,\Omega \approx 71\,\text{k}\Omega$$
$$r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E = 3900 + 70970 = 74870\,\Omega$$
$$A_v^{\text{unbypassed}} = \frac{-\beta R_C'}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E} = \frac{-150 \times 1498}{74870} = \frac{-224700}{74870} = -3.0$$

**Comment on role of $R_E$:** The unbypassed emitter resistor reduces the gain from **57.6 to 3.0** ŌĆö a factor of ~19├Ś reduction. However, this sacrifices gain to gain **stability against temperature variations, $\beta$ variations, and linearity improvements** (negative feedback). The bypass capacitor restores the gain while maintaining the DC stability provided by $R_E$. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> - **Sign Error (ŌĆō1 mark):** Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v = -g_m R_C$. Always remember CE gives **180┬░ phase inversion**.
> - **Wrong denominator (ŌĆō2 marks):** Using $r_\pi + \beta R_E$ instead of $r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$. The "+1" is **frequently missed** and costs full marks.
> - **Mixing up $R_B$ and $R_{in}$:** The input resistance *seen by the source* is $R_B \Vert R_{in,base}$, not just $R_{in,base}$. Loss of 1ŌĆō2 marks if not explicitly mentioned.
> - **Using $V_T = 25\,\text{mV}$:** While commonly seen, KTU officially uses **$V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$** at 300 K. Stick to 26 mV unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> - **Skipping limiting-case verification:** KTU examiners *love* asking you to verify the formula reduces correctly. Always do this for partial credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1, Small-Signal Gains**

- **Small-signal assumption:** Signal amplitude small enough (Ōēż 10 mV) so transistor behaves linearly around Q-point.
- **Hybrid-ŽĆ model:** Replaces BJT with $r_\pi$ (input), $g_m v_\pi$ (dependent source), $r_o$ (output).
- **Master identity:** $\beta = g_m \cdot r_\pi$ ŌĆö the most important equation.
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = I_C / V_T$ ŌĆö proportional to Q-point current.
- **Input resistance:** $r_\pi = V_T / I_B = \beta V_T / I_C$ ŌĆö inversely proportional to $I_C$.
- **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K.
- **CE (bypassed) voltage gain:** $A_v = -g_m R_C$ ŌĆö large magnitude, inverting.
- **CE (unbypassed) voltage gain:** $A_v = -\beta R_C / [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E]$ ŌĆö reduced but stable.
- **Current gain (CE):** $A_i \approx -\beta$ for ideal shorted load; $-\beta R_C/(R_C+R_L)$ for finite load.
- **Phase:** CE inverts (180┬░), CB and CC do not invert (0┬░).
- **Procedure:** DC analysis ŌåÆ Q-point ŌåÆ parameters ŌåÆ AC equivalent ŌåÆ Hybrid-ŽĆ ŌåÆ KVL/KCL ŌåÆ gain.
- **Trade-off:** Bypass capacitor ŌåÆ high gain but unstable; unbypassed $R_E$ ŌåÆ lower gain but stable.
- **Deactivation rule:** $V_{CC} \to$ short, coupling/bypass capacitors $\to$ short (at signal frequency).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Input and Output Impedance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Input Impedance* ($Z_{in}$) and *Output Impedance* ($Z_{out}$) are the Thevenin-equivalent impedances looking into the input port and output port of a two-port network, respectively. They fundamentally govern **signal coupling**, **voltage transfer efficiency**, and **loading effects** in cascaded electronic systems.

### 1.1 Formal Definitions

**Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):**
The input impedance of a network is defined as the ratio of the applied voltage to the resulting current at the input terminals, with the output port terminated in a specified load $Z_L$.

$$Z_{in} = \left. \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} \right|_{Z_L \text{ connected}}$$

**Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):**
The output impedance is the Thevenin-equivalent impedance looking back into the output terminals, with the input source deactivated (voltage source shorted, current source opened).

$$Z_{out} = \left. \frac{V_{out (open)}}{I_{out (short)}} \right|_{V_{in}=0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In low-frequency analog design, $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ are purely resistive ($R_{in}$, $R_{out}$). At high frequencies, the reactive components ($L$, $C$) become significant, making them complex quantities $Z = R + jX$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Water Tap Model

Imagine a **water tap system** delivering water (signal) from a reservoir (source) to a bucket (load):

| Electronics Concept | Water Analogy |
|---------------------|---------------|
| **Source Impedance** ($Z_s$) | The pipe's resistance to water flow at the tap end |
| **Input Impedance** ($Z_{in}$) | How much the tap "opens" or "restricts" water entry |
| **Output Impedance** ($Z_{out}$) | Resistance of the pipe leading OUT of the device |
| **Load** ($Z_L$) | The bucket's own fill resistance |

If the **input impedance is LOW** (tap fully open), the source struggles to push signal in ŌĆö just as a wide-open tap causes pressure drop at the reservoir. If the **input impedance is HIGH** (tap barely open), the source can push signal easily. Conversely, a **low output impedance** acts like a stiff, non-collapsible pipe that delivers water (signal) faithfully to the load regardless of bucket size.

### 1.3 Why Input/Output Impedance Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Voltage Transfer Ratio** of a cascaded system (Source ŌåÆ Stage 1 ŌåÆ Stage 2 ŌåÆ Load) is critically dependent on impedance matching. A designer MUST analyze $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ to:
> 1. Maximize **signal transfer** (Maximum Power Transfer Theorem)
> 2. Minimize **loading effects** between cascaded stages
> 3. Ensure **bandwidth stability** in amplifier stages
> 4. Prevent **signal reflection** in high-frequency/RF systems

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Characteristic Impedance** (in RF/transmission lines): commonly **$50\ \Omega$** for RF systems, **$75\ \Omega$** for video/cable TV.
- **Standard Reference Resistance**: **$Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$** in most laboratory instruments (oscilloscopes, spectrum analyzers).
- **Audio Line Standard**: **$600\ \Omega$** (historically used in telephone/audio impedance matching).
- **Unit of Impedance**: **Ohm ($\Omega$)** ŌĆö same as resistance, since it's a complex generalization.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Port Network Framework

Every electronic system can be modeled as a **two-port network** with the following architecture:

- **Port 1 (Input)**: Connects to source $V_s$ with internal impedance $Z_s$
- **Port 2 (Output)**: Connects to load $Z_L$
- **The Network**: Active (transistor amplifier) or Passive (filter, attenuator)

The complete signal flow is:

$$V_s \rightarrow Z_s \rightarrow [\text{Two-Port Network}] \rightarrow Z_L$$

### 2.2 Why $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ Exist: The 'How'

When a signal source $V_s$ with internal impedance $Z_s$ drives a network's input port:

**Step 1:** Source voltage is divided between $Z_s$ and $Z_{in}$.

$$V_{in} = V_s \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}}$$

**Step 2:** The network amplifies/processes this voltage, generating an open-circuit output $V_{th} = A_v \cdot V_{in}$ (where $A_v$ is the voltage gain).

**Step 3:** The output voltage $V_{out}$ is then divided between $Z_{out}$ and $Z_L$.

$$V_{out} = V_{th} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L} = A_v \cdot V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$

**Step 4:** Combining Steps 1 and 3 gives the **overall voltage transfer function**:

$$A_{vs} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_s} = A_v \cdot \underbrace{\frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}}}_{\text{Input Loading Factor}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}}_{\text{Output Loading Factor}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The term "ideal amplifier" implicitly means $Z_{in} = \infty$ (so the input loading factor = 1) AND $Z_{out} = 0$ (so the output loading factor = 1). When either condition fails, **loading effect** occurs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Domain of Use |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Z_{in} = V_{in} / I_{in}$ | Definition of input impedance | All networks |
| 2 | $Z_{out} = V_{th} / I_{sc}$ | Definition of output impedance | All networks |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{th} \cdot Z_L / (Z_{out} + Z_L)$ | Loaded output voltage | Cascaded systems |
| 4 | $A_{vs} = A_v \cdot [Z_{in}/(Z_s + Z_{in})] \cdot [Z_L/(Z_{out} + Z_L)]$ | Overall source-to-load gain | Cascaded amplifier |
| 5 | $P_{max}$ when $Z_L = Z_{out}^*$ | Maximum Power Transfer Theorem | Power amplifiers |
| 6 | $V_{out} = 0.5 \cdot V_{th}$ when $Z_L = Z_{out}$ | Half-voltage loading effect | Voltage amplifiers |
| 7 | $Z_{in}$ (ideal voltage amp) $= \infty$ | Ideal voltage amplifier input | Ideal models |
| 8 | $Z_{out}$ (ideal voltage amp) $= 0$ | Ideal voltage amplifier output | Ideal models |
| 9 | $Z_{in}$ (ideal current amp) $= 0$ | Ideal current amplifier input | Ideal models |
| 10 | $Z_{out}$ (ideal current amp) $= \infty$ | Ideal current amplifier output | Ideal models |
| 11 | $A_i = I_{out}/I_{in}$ | Current gain | Current amplifiers |
| 12 | $Z_{in(BJT,CE)} \approx \beta \cdot r_e$ | Common-emitter input Z | Small-signal BJT |
| 13 | $Z_{out(BJT,CE)} \approx r_o$ | Common-emitter output Z | Small-signal BJT |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Audio Power Amplifier Design**: Output impedance is critical. Speakers (typically $4\ \Omega$, $8\ \Omega$) must match the amplifier's output stage for damping factor control.
- **Oscilloscope Probes**: The $10\times$ probe has $9\ \text{M}\Omega$ input resistance ŌĆö to MINIMIZE loading of the circuit under test.
- **Antenna Systems**: Mismatch between transmission line ($50\ \Omega$) and antenna causes standing waves, characterized by the **VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio)**.
- **Biomedical Instrumentation (EEG/ECG)**: High $Z_{in}$ (typically $> 10\ \text{M}\Omega$) is mandatory to avoid loading the body's high-impedance bioelectric signals.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Input Impedance of a Loaded Two-Port Network

Consider a general two-port network with h-parameters (hybrid parameters), loaded by $Z_L$ at the output.

The h-parameter equations are:

$$V_1 = h_{11} \cdot I_1 + h_{12} \cdot V_2$$

$$I_2 = h_{21} \cdot I_1 + h_{22} \cdot V_2$$

The output port has a load $Z_L$, so the constraint is:

$$V_2 = -I_2 \cdot Z_L$$

**Substitute the constraint into the second h-equation:**

$$I_2 = h_{21} \cdot I_1 + h_{22} \cdot (-I_2 \cdot Z_L)$$

$$I_2 = h_{21} \cdot I_1 - h_{22} \cdot I_2 \cdot Z_L$$

**Bring all $I_2$ terms to one side:**

$$I_2 + h_{22} \cdot I_2 \cdot Z_L = h_{21} \cdot I_1$$

$$I_2 \cdot (1 + h_{22} \cdot Z_L) = h_{21} \cdot I_1$$

$$I_2 = \frac{h_{21} \cdot I_1}{1 + h_{22} \cdot Z_L}$$

**Now substitute $I_2$ back into $V_2 = -I_2 \cdot Z_L$:**

$$V_2 = -\frac{h_{21} \cdot I_1 \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} \cdot Z_L}$$

**Substitute $V_2$ into the first h-equation:**

$$V_1 = h_{11} \cdot I_1 + h_{12} \cdot \left( -\frac{h_{21} \cdot I_1 \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} \cdot Z_L} \right)$$

$$V_1 = h_{11} \cdot I_1 - \frac{h_{12} \cdot h_{21} \cdot I_1 \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} \cdot Z_L}$$

**Factor out $I_1$:**

$$V_1 = I_1 \cdot \left[ h_{11} - \frac{h_{12} \cdot h_{21} \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} \cdot Z_L} \right]$$

**Therefore, the input impedance is:**

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = h_{11} - \frac{h_{12} \cdot h_{21} \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} \cdot Z_L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: The term $h_{11}$ is the "intrinsic" input impedance of the network. The second term represents the **feedback effect** of the load $Z_L$ through the reverse voltage ratio $h_{12}$ and forward current ratio $h_{21}$. For an ideal unilateral network ($h_{12} = 0$), this collapses to $Z_{in} = h_{11}$ ŌĆö the input becomes **independent of load**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Impedance Using the Same h-Parameters

With the input port driven by a source $V_s$ having impedance $Z_s$, the input constraint is:

$$V_1 = V_s - I_1 \cdot Z_s$$

Substituting into the first h-equation:

$$V_s - I_1 \cdot Z_s = h_{11} \cdot I_1 + h_{12} \cdot V_2$$

Solving for $I_1$:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_s - h_{12} \cdot V_2}{h_{11} + Z_s}$$

Substituting $I_1$ into the second h-equation:

$$I_2 = h_{21} \cdot \left( \frac{V_s - h_{12} \cdot V_2}{h_{11} + Z_s} \right) + h_{22} \cdot V_2$$

$$I_2 = \frac{h_{21} \cdot V_s - h_{21} \cdot h_{12} \cdot V_2}{h_{11} + Z_s} + h_{22} \cdot V_2$$

To find $Z_{out}$, set $V_s = 0$ (deactivate the source) and find $V_2 / I_2$:

$$I_2 = -\frac{h_{21} \cdot h_{12} \cdot V_2}{h_{11} + Z_s} + h_{22} \cdot V_2$$

$$I_2 = V_2 \cdot \left[ h_{22} - \frac{h_{21} \cdot h_{12}}{h_{11} + Z_s} \right]$$

**Therefore, the output impedance is:**

$$\boxed{Z_{out} = \frac{V_2}{I_2} \bigg\vert_{V_s=0} = \frac{1}{h_{22} - \dfrac{h_{21} \cdot h_{12}}{h_{11} + Z_s}}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Amplifier Configurations

| Configuration | $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{out}$ | Voltage Gain $A_v$ | Typical Application |
|---------------|----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **CE BJT** | Medium ($\beta r_e$) | High ($r_o$) | High | General purpose voltage amp |
| **CB BJT** | Low ($r_e$) | High ($r_o$) | High | High-frequency, RF |
| **CC BJT (Emitter Follower)** | High ($\beta r_e + (\beta+1) R_E$) | Low ($R_E \Vert r_o / (\beta+1)$) | $\approx 1$ (Unity) | Buffer, impedance matching |
| **CS MOSFET** | Infinite (DC) | $r_d$ | High | General voltage amp |
| **CD MOSFET (Source Follower)** | Infinite (DC) | $1/g_m$ | $\approx 1$ | Buffer stage |
| **Ideal Voltage Amp** | $\infty$ | $0$ | $A_v$ (constant) | Theoretical reference |
| **Ideal Current Amp** | $0$ | $\infty$ | ŌĆö | Theoretical reference |
| **Ideal Transconductance Amp** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | ŌĆö | Op-amp with $i$-out |
| **Ideal Transresistance Amp** | $0$ | $0$ | ŌĆö | Op-amp with $v$-out |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A CE amplifier has $Z_{in} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $Z_{out} = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, and unloaded voltage gain $A_v = 100$. The source impedance is $Z_s = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ and load is $Z_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$. Find the overall source-to-load voltage gain.

**Solution:**

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$

**Step 1: Input loading factor:**

$$\frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}} = \frac{2000}{1000 + 2000} = \frac{2000}{3000} = \frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 2: Output loading factor:**

$$\frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L} = \frac{10000}{50000 + 10000} = \frac{10000}{60000} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 3: Overall gain:**

$$A_{vs} = 100 \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{6} = \frac{200}{18} = 11.11$$

**Step 4: Gain in dB:**

$$A_{vs(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(11.11) = 20.92\ \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The intrinsic gain of $A_v = 100$ (40 dB) drops to $A_{vs} \approx 11.1$ (20.9 dB) due to loading effects. The **input loading loss** alone is 3.52 dB, and the **output loading loss** is 15.56 dB ŌĆö a classic KTU-style teaching scenario.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, PBRAT304]**
*Define input impedance and output impedance of a two-port network. Why is high input impedance and low output impedance desirable in a voltage amplifier?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** It is the impedance measured at the input terminals of a network with the output terminated by the load. Mathematically,

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}$$

**Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):** It is the Thevenin equivalent impedance looking into the output terminals with the input source deactivated.

$$Z_{out} = \left. \frac{V_{out}}{I_{out}} \right|_{V_s=0}$$

**Why desirable (2 marks):**
1. **High $Z_{in}$**: Prevents loading of the source, so $V_{in} \approx V_s$ (input loading factor approaches 1).
2. **Low $Z_{out}$**: Delivers signal to load without attenuation, so $V_{out} \approx V_{th}$ (output loading factor approaches 1).

This combination maximizes the **overall voltage transfer** from source to load.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, PBRAT304]**
*State the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem. Under what condition is it applied versus the voltage transfer requirement?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Maximum Power Transfer Theorem:** Maximum power is delivered from a source to a load when the load impedance equals the complex conjugate of the source impedance:

$$Z_L = Z_s^*$$

When $Z_s$ is purely resistive, $Z_L = Z_s$.

**Application conditions:**
- **Power transfer scenario** (e.g., RF communication, antenna matching): Use MPT ŌĆö set $Z_L = Z_s^*$.
- **Voltage transfer scenario** (e.g., voltage amplifiers, sensors): Make $Z_L \gg Z_{out}$ so that nearly all of $V_{th}$ appears across $Z_L$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often quote MPT for ALL amplifier designs. MPT is for **power delivery**. For **voltage amplifiers**, we want $Z_L \gg Z_{out}$, NOT $Z_L = Z_{out}$. Mismatching is the design goal!

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, PBRAT304, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the input impedance of a two-port network in terms of h-parameters when terminated by a load $Z_L$. Explain each term physically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A common-emitter amplifier has the following measurements:
- Voltage gain (unloaded): $A_{v} = 150$
- Input impedance: $Z_{in} = 3\ \text{k}\Omega$
- Output impedance: $Z_{out} = 40\ \text{k}\Omega$
- Source impedance: $Z_s = 600\ \Omega$
- Load: $Z_L = 8\ \text{k}\Omega$

Calculate the **(i)** overall voltage gain $A_{vs}$, **(ii)** gain in dB, and **(iii)** gain reduction due to loading in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

The h-parameter model of a two-port network gives:

$$V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2$$

$$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2$$

With the output loaded by $Z_L$, the constraint equation is:

$$V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$$

Substituting into the $I_2$ equation:

$$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 - h_{22} Z_L I_2 \Rightarrow I_2 (1 + h_{22} Z_L) = h_{21} I_1$$

$$\Rightarrow I_2 = \frac{h_{21} I_1}{1 + h_{22} Z_L}$$

Now finding $V_2$:

$$V_2 = -I_2 Z_L = -\frac{h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} I_1$$

Substituting into the $V_1$ equation:

$$V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} \left( -\frac{h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} I_1 \right)$$

$$V_1 = I_1 \left[ h_{11} - \frac{h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} \right]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = h_{11} - \frac{h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L}}$$

**Physical interpretation of terms (3 marks distribution):**
- $h_{11}$: Intrinsic short-circuit input impedance of the network alone.
- $h_{12}$: Reverse voltage feedback ratio (output to input coupling).
- $h_{21}$: Forward current gain (input to output transfer).
- $h_{22}$: Output admittance with input open.
- The second term represents the **reflected effect of $Z_L$** at the input port, mediated by the bilateral nature of the network ($h_{12} \neq 0$). For unilateral networks ($h_{12} = 0$), $Z_{in} = h_{11}$ ŌĆö load-independent.

---

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**(i) Overall voltage gain:**

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$

**Input loading factor:**

$$\frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}} = \frac{3000}{600 + 3000} = \frac{3000}{3600} = 0.833$$

**Output loading factor:**

$$\frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L} = \frac{8000}{40000 + 8000} = \frac{8000}{48000} = 0.1667$$

**Overall gain:**

$$A_{vs} = 150 \times 0.833 \times 0.1667 = 150 \times 0.1389 = 20.83$$

**(ii) Gain in dB:**

$$A_{vs(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(20.83) = 20 \times 1.3187 = 26.37\ \text{dB}$$

**(iii) Gain reduction due to loading:**

$$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(150) = 20 \times 2.1761 = 43.52\ \text{dB}$$

$$\text{Loading loss} = 43.52 - 26.37 = 17.15\ \text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget that **loading factor on output is the dominant loss** in CE amplifiers. In this problem, the output loading alone causes $20 \log_{10}(0.1667) = -15.56$ dB loss, while input loading causes only $-1.58$ dB. Always compute and report **both factors separately** for full marks.

---

**Question B: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, PBRAT304, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the concept of loading effect in cascaded amplifier stages. Show how the overall gain is reduced. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An audio amplifier has specifications: $Z_{in} = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, $Z_{out} = 0.5\ \Omega$, intrinsic gain $A_v = 50$. It drives a speaker load of $Z_L = 8\ \Omega$ from a source with $Z_s = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$.

Calculate the **power delivered to the speaker** if the source RMS voltage is $V_s = 2\ \text{V}$. Also compute the **maximum power that could be delivered** if the source were directly connected to the speaker. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Block diagram representation (3 marks):**

A cascaded system has Stage 1 (with $Z_{in1}$, $A_{v1}$, $Z_{out1}$) feeding Stage 2 (with $Z_{in2}$, $A_{v2}$, $Z_{out2}$), driving load $Z_L$.

**Loading effect derivation (4 marks):**

Stage 2's input impedance $Z_{in2}$ becomes the load for Stage 1's output. The voltage at Stage 2's input is:

$$V_{in2} = V_{out1} \cdot \frac{Z_{in2}}{Z_{out1} + Z_{in2}}$$

This shows that $V_{in2} < V_{out1}$ unless $Z_{in2} \gg Z_{out1}$.

The overall gain becomes:

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot \frac{Z_{in2}}{Z_{out1} + Z_{in2}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out2} + Z_L}$$

**Key insight:** Even though the individual stage gains multiply, the **inter-stage loading factors** can drastically reduce the effective gain. To minimize this, designers use **buffer stages** (e.g., emitter followers) with $Z_{in} \to \infty$ and $Z_{out} \to 0$ between high-gain stages.

---

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Voltage at amplifier input (after source loading):**

$$V_{in} = V_s \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}} = 2 \cdot \frac{50000}{1000 + 50000} = 2 \cdot \frac{50000}{51000} = 1.961\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Open-circuit (Thevenin) output voltage:**

$$V_{th} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = 50 \cdot 1.961 = 98.04\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3: Voltage delivered to speaker (output loading):**

$$V_{out} = V_{th} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L} = 98.04 \cdot \frac{8}{0.5 + 8} = 98.04 \cdot \frac{8}{8.5} = 92.27\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4: Power delivered to speaker:**

$$P_L = \frac{V_{out}^2}{Z_L} = \frac{(92.27)^2}{8} = \frac{8513.8}{8} = 1064.2\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5: Direct source-to-speaker connection (no amplifier):**

$$V_{speaker,direct} = V_s \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_s + Z_L} = 2 \cdot \frac{8}{1000 + 8} = 2 \cdot \frac{8}{1008} = 0.01587\ \text{V}$$

$$P_{direct} = \frac{(0.01587)^2}{8} = 3.15 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{W} = 31.5\ \mu\text{W}$$

**Step 6: Power amplification:**

$$\text{Power gain} = \frac{P_L}{P_{direct}} = \frac{1064.2}{3.15 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.38 \times 10^7 \quad (\approx 75.3\ \text{dB})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Warning ŌĆö Unit Pitfall:** When asked for "power delivered to the load," compute $V_{out}^2 / Z_L$ with **RMS values**. Using peak voltages gives 2├Ś error. Also, don't confuse $V_{th}$ (Thevenin/open-circuit) with $V_{out}$ (loaded). The amplifier's intrinsic $A_v$ is referenced to $V_{in}$, not $V_s$!

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 1**

- Ō£ģ **Input Impedance** = $Z_{in} = V_{in} / I_{in}$ (with load connected at output).
- Ō£ģ **Output Impedance** = $Z_{out} = V_{th} / I_{sc}$ (Thevenin equivalent, source deactivated).
- Ō£ģ **Ideal voltage amplifier**: $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$.
- Ō£ģ **Ideal current amplifier**: $Z_{in} = 0$, $Z_{out} = \infty$.
- Ō£ģ **Overall source-to-load gain** formula:

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$

- Ō£ģ **Maximum Power Transfer** (power amplifiers): $Z_L = Z_s^*$ (or $Z_L = Z_s$ for purely resistive).
- Ō£ģ **Voltage amplifiers** require $Z_L \gg Z_{out}$, NOT impedance matching.
- Ō£ģ **Buffer stage** (e.g., emitter follower, source follower) is used for impedance transformation: high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$, unity gain.
- Ō£ģ **CE BJT** has medium $Z_{in}$ ($\beta r_e$) and high $Z_{out}$ ($r_o$).
- Ō£ģ **CC BJT (emitter follower)** has very high $Z_{in}$ and very low $Z_{out}$ ŌĆö ideal buffer.
- Ō£ģ **CS MOSFET** has essentially infinite $Z_{in}$ at DC (gate is insulated).
- Ō£ģ **h-parameter input impedance formula** (master it for derivations):

$$Z_{in} = h_{11} - \frac{h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L}$$

- Ō£ģ **Loading effect** = voltage division between $Z_{out}$ of one stage and $Z_{in}$ of the next.
- Ō£ģ **Standard impedances to remember**: RF = $50\ \Omega$, video = $75\ \Omega$, audio = $600\ \Omega$, scope probe $10\times$ = $9\ \text{M}\Omega$ in series with $10\ \text{pF}$.
- Ō£ģ **Always check the output loading factor first** ŌĆö it is the dominant source of gain loss in CE stages.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-Amp Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Op-Amp Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 ŌĆö OPERATIONAL AMPLIFIER (OP-AMP) BASICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a **direct-coupled, high-gain, differential input electronic voltage amplifier** with a single-ended output, designed to be used with external feedback components to control its overall response characteristics. The term "operational" originates from its original use in analog computers to perform mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** *The operational amplifier is a multi-stage, direct-coupled active device that amplifies the difference between two input voltages, producing an output voltage that is typically hundreds of thousands of times larger than the input difference.*

The standard op-amp is a **5-terminal active device**:
1. **Inverting Input (VŌü╗ or INŌłÆ or VŌéü)**
2. **Non-Inverting Input (VŌü║ or IN+ or VŌéé)**
3. **Output (VŌéĆ)**
4. **Positive Supply Rail (+V_CC or +V_DD)**
5. **Negative Supply Rail (ŌłÆV_EE or ŌłÆV_SS)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)
> [!NOTE]
> **­¤ÄÜ’ĖÅ The "Volume Knob with Two Ears" Analogy**
>
