> Imagine a person standing at a podium listening with **two ears** (the two inputs). The brain computes the **difference** between the loudness reaching the left ear and the right ear. That tiny difference is then **amplified enormously** before being sent to the mouth (the output), which shouts out a much louder version of that difference.
>
> ŌĆó If both ears hear the *same* sound ŌåÆ the brain outputs *nothing* (this is **Common-Mode Rejection**).
> ŌĆó If the right ear hears a slightly louder whisper than the left ŌåÆ the mouth shouts that whispered difference **enormously amplified**.
> ŌĆó The mouth can only shout so fast ŌĆö this limit is the **Slew Rate**.

### 1.3 Why Op-Amps Matter in Engineering
Op-amps are the **"building blocks of analog electronics"**. Virtually every modern analog system ŌĆö from audio amplifiers and active filters to analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), instrumentation amplifiers, voltage regulators, oscillators, and PID controllers ŌĆö uses op-amps as the core active element. The **╬╝A741 (IC 741)**, introduced by Dave Fullagar at Fairchild Semiconductor in 1968, is the iconic industry-standard op-amp.

### 1.4 The Standard Op-Amp Symbol
The IEEE-standard schematic symbol for an op-amp is a **triangle** with two inputs on the flat side and one output on the apex:

```
       ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöéŌöĆŌŚĆŌöĆŌöĆ INŌłÆ (Inverting)
       Ōöé      Ōöé
       Ōöé     Ō¢▓
       Ōöé     Ōöé
       ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöéŌöĆŌŚĆŌöĆŌöĆ IN+ (Non-Inverting)
              ŌĢ▓
               ŌĢ▓________Ō¢Č VŌéĆ (Output)
```

The **minus (ŌłÆ)** sign denotes the **inverting input**; the **plus (+)** sign denotes the **non-inverting input**. The symbol often includes +V and ŌłÆV supply pins on the top and bottom of the triangle.

### 1.5 Difference Amplifier ŌĆö The Heart of an Op-Amp
At its very core, every op-amp begins life as a **differential amplifier** that takes two inputs and produces an output proportional to their difference:

$$V_{d} = V_{+} - V_{-}$$

This quantity is called the **differential input voltage** and is the *only* signal the op-amp amplifies (in ideal conditions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Diagram of a General Op-Amp
A real-world monolithic op-amp (like the ╬╝A741) is built from **four cascaded functional stages**:

| Stage | Function | Typical Active Device |
|-------|----------|----------------------|
| **Stage 1 ŌĆö Input Stage** | Differential amplifier providing high input impedance, differential gain (A_d), and common-mode rejection (CMRR). | BJT/FET Diff-Pair with active current-mirror load |
| **Stage 2 ŌĆö Gain Stage** | Provides the bulk of the voltage gain (A_V). | Common-Emitter / Common-Source amplifier |
| **Stage 3 ŌĆö Level Shifter** | Translates the DC level so that output can swing symmetrically around 0 V. | Emitter Follower / Source Follower / V_BE multiplier |
| **Stage 4 ŌĆö Output Stage** | Provides low output impedance and high current drive (push-pull Class-AB). | Complementary Emitter Follower (Q15/Q19 in 741) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** The total open-loop gain A_OL is the **product** of the gains of all four stages, often reaching **2 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ (Ōēł 106 dB)** for the 741.

## 2.2 The Ideal Op-Amp (Textbook Reference Model)
> [!NOTE]
> **The IDEAL op-amp is a pedagogical construct** ŌĆö no physical op-amp achieves all of these properties, but real op-amps approach them closely enough that ideal analysis gives highly accurate results.

| Parameter | Ideal Value | Real 741 Value |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Open-Loop Voltage Gain (A_OL) | **Ōł× (infinite)** | 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ (Ōēł 106 dB) |
| Input Impedance (Z_in) | **Ōł× (infinite)** | 2 M╬® |
| Output Impedance (Z_out) | **0 (zero)** | 75 ╬® |
| Input Bias Current (I_B) | **0** | 80 nA (typ) |
| Input Offset Voltage (V_io) | **0** | 2 mV (typ) |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) | **Ōł×** | 90 dB |
| Slew Rate (SR) | **Ōł×** | 0.5 V/╬╝s |
| Bandwidth (BW) | **Ōł×** | 1 MHz (unity-gain) |
| Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) | **Ōł×** | 76 dB |

### 2.2.1 The "Golden Rules" of the Ideal Op-Amp
Because A_OL ŌåÆ Ōł× and Z_in ŌåÆ Ōł×, two **inviolable rules** govern all negative-feedback analysis:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 ŌĆö The Virtual Short:** The differential input voltage is zero:  
> $$V_{d} = V_{+} - V_{-} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{+} = V_{-}$$
>
> **Rule 2 ŌĆö The Infinite Input Impedance:** No current flows into either input terminal:  
> $$I_{+} = I_{-} = 0$$

These two rules alone solve **every** op-amp circuit in negative feedback. They are the single most important KTU tool you will use for the rest of the course.

## 2.3 Op-Amp Parameters ŌĆö Detailed Engineering Definitions

### 2.3.1 Input Offset Voltage (V_io)
The small DC voltage that, when applied between the two input terminals, forces the output to **zero volts**. It arises from unavoidable mismatches in the input differential pair transistors.

$$V_{io} = V_{+} - V_{-} \big|_{V_{o} = 0}$$

> **Typical 741 value:** ┬▒2 mV (can drift with temperature, characterized by the temperature coefficient dV_io/dT).

### 2.3.2 Input Bias Current (I_B)
The average of the two DC currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals:

$$I_{B} = \frac{I_{B}^{-} + I_{B}^{+}}{2}$$

### 2.3.3 Input Offset Current (I_io)
The difference between the two input bias currents:

$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B}^{+} - I_{B}^{-} \vert$$

### 2.3.4 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
A measure of the op-amp's ability to **reject signals common to both inputs** (such as noise or power-supply hum). It is the ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain:

$$CMRR = \left\vert \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \right\vert \quad ; \quad CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

A higher CMRR means better rejection of common-mode noise ŌĆö critical in instrumentation and medical signal acquisition.

### 2.3.5 Slew Rate (SR)
The **maximum rate of change of the output voltage** under large-signal conditions. It is caused by the limited current available to charge the internal compensation capacitor C_C.

$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_{o}(t)}{dt} \right\vert_{max}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a sinusoid V_o(t) = V_p sin(2ŽĆft), the slew rate requirement is:  
> $$SR \geq 2\pi f V_{p}$$

If the input frequency is too high, the output becomes a **triangular wave** (slew-rate limited distortion).

### 2.3.6 Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)
A fundamental constant of the op-amp. When negative feedback reduces the closed-loop gain to A_CL, the usable bandwidth increases by the same factor:

$$GBW = A_{CL} \times f_{3dB} = A_{OL} \times f_{p}$$

For the 741, GBW Ōēł 1 MHz.

### 2.3.7 Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)
The op-amp's ability to reject ripples or noise from the power supply rails:

$$PSRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\Delta V_{S}}{\Delta V_{o}}\right)$$

### 2.3.8 Open-Loop Voltage Gain (A_OL)
The gain of the op-amp with **no feedback** applied:

$$A_{OL} = \frac{V_{o}}{V_{d}} = \frac{V_{o}}{V_{+} - V_{-}}$$

For the 741, A_OL Ōēł 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ Ōēł **106 dB**.

## 2.4 The Open-Loop Transfer Equation (Master Equation)
The single most important equation in op-amp theory:

$$V_{o} = A_{OL}(V_{+} - V_{-})$$

Expanding with both differential and common-mode components:

$$\boxed{V_{o} = A_{d}(V_{+} - V_{-}) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2}\right)}$$

Where:
- **A_d** = differential-mode gain
- **A_cm** = common-mode gain
- **(V+ + VŌłÆ)/2** = common-mode input voltage V_cm

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Audio preamplifiers** ŌåÆ use low-noise op-amps with high CMRR (e.g., NE5532, OPA2134)
- **Medical instrumentation (ECG/EEG)** ŌåÆ use instrumentation op-amps (e.g., INA128) with CMRR > 100 dB
- **Active filters (Sallen-Key, Multiple-Feedback)** ŌåÆ use op-amps as the gain element
- **Voltage regulators** ŌåÆ op-amp drives a pass transistor in linear regulators
- **Comparators & Schmitt Triggers** ŌåÆ op-amp in open-loop or with positive feedback
- **ADC/DAC front-ends** ŌåÆ op-amps provide buffering and gain scaling
- **Oscilloscope front-ends** ŌåÆ ultra-wide-band op-amps (e.g., LM7171, 1 GHz GBW)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage in Terms of Differential & Common-Mode Inputs

### 3.1.1 Setup
We define two arbitrary input voltages VŌéü and VŌéé applied to the inverting and non-inverting inputs respectively. We want to express V_o entirely in terms of V_d and V_cm.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define differential-mode and common-mode voltages:**

$$V_{d} = V_{+} - V_{-} = V_{1} - V_{2}$$

$$V_{cm} = \frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2} = \frac{V_{1} + V_{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve the linear system for V+ and VŌłÆ in terms of V_d and V_cm:**

Add the two definitions:

$$V_{d} + 2V_{cm} = (V_{+} - V_{-}) + (V_{+} + V_{-}) = 2V_{+}$$

$$\therefore \quad V_{+} = \frac{V_{d}}{2} + V_{cm}$$

Similarly, subtract:

$$2V_{cm} - V_{d} = (V_{+} + V_{-}) - (V_{+} - V_{-}) = 2V_{-}$$

$$\therefore \quad V_{-} = V_{cm} - \frac{V_{d}}{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the linear superposition principle.**

The op-amp output is a linear combination of the two independent inputs. Let A_d be the gain applied to V_d, and A_cm be the gain applied to V_cm:

$$V_{o} = A_{d}\cdot V_{d} + A_{cm}\cdot V_{cm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final result:**

$$\boxed{V_{o} = A_{d}(V_{+} - V_{-}) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2}\right)}$$

This is the **fundamental op-amp input-output equation** that all KTU problems are based on.

### 3.1.2 Verification: Why This Matters
If the op-amp is **ideal**, then A_d ŌåÆ Ōł× and A_cm ŌåÆ 0. The output V_o is bounded by the supply rails, so for V_o to remain finite, the term A_d┬ĘV_d must also remain finite, which forces V_d ŌåÆ 0 ŌĆö the **virtual short** principle.

## 3.2 Derivation: CMRR in Decibels and Percentage

**Step 1 ŌĆö CMRR as a dimensionless ratio:**

$$CMRR = \left\vert \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to decibels (KTU standard convention):**

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(CMRR)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Numerical example:**
If A_d = 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ and A_cm = 2, then:

$$CMRR = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{2} = 10^{5}$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10^{5}) = 100 \text{ dB}$$

This is a typical value for instrumentation-grade op-amps.

## 3.3 Derivation: Slew-Rate Limited Maximum Frequency

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with a sinusoidal output:**

$$V_{o}(t) = V_{p}\sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to time:**

$$\frac{dV_{o}}{dt} = 2\pi f V_{p}\cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find the maximum (occurs at t = 0 when cosine = 1):**

$$\left. \frac{dV_{o}}{dt} \right\vert_{max} = 2\pi f V_{p}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö To avoid slew-rate distortion, the op-amp's SR must equal or exceed this peak rate:**

$$\boxed{SR \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_{p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{p}}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical example (741 op-amp):**
Given SR = 0.5 V/╬╝s and V_p = 5 V, the maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency is:

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ V/s}}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{31.416} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}$$

This is exactly why the 741 cannot be used in audio/video applications ŌĆö its SR is too low.

## 3.4 Derivation: Input Offset Voltage Effect on Output

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume the op-amp is configured as a non-inverting amplifier with gain A_CL = 1 + R_F/R_in.**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Without compensation, the input offset voltage V_io is amplified by A_CL:**

$$V_{o,\text{offset}} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{io}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö For a 741 with V_io = 2 mV and A_CL = 100:**

$$V_{o,\text{offset}} = 100 \times 2 \text{ mV} = 200 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö This unwanted DC offset can be cancelled by injecting a small trimming voltage at the non-inverting input** via a potentiometer between the supply rails (the classic 741 offset-null pins 1 and 5 configuration).

## 3.5 Derivation: Open-Loop to Closed-Loop Gain Relationship

**Step 1 ŌĆö The op-amp in negative feedback has feedback factor ╬▓:**

$$\beta = \frac{V_{f}}{V_{o}} = \frac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_{F}} \quad \text{(in inverting configuration)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö The closed-loop gain of a feedback amplifier is the well-known result (Bode's feedback equation):**

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö For A_OL┬Ę╬▓ >> 1 (which is always true for op-amps), this simplifies to:**

$$A_{CL} \approx \frac{1}{\beta}$$

This is the *single most important practical result* ŌĆö **the closed-loop gain is set entirely by the external passive feedback network, not by the op-amp itself.** This is why op-amp circuits are stable, predictable, and linear.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Internal Stages of a Monolithic Op-Amp

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **four functional stages** of a general-purpose op-amp (╬╝A741 architecture) and the signal flow between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Source V1 Inverting] --> S1
    B[Input Source V2 Non Inverting] --> S1
    S1[Stage 1 Differential Input Stage High Zin Differential Gain CMRR Active Load Current Mirror] --> S2
    S2[Stage 2 High Gain Voltage Amplifier Provides Bulk of AOL 100 to 1000 times] --> S3
    S3[Stage 3 Level Shifter Translates DC Bias to Allow Zero Output with Zero Input] --> S4
    S4[Stage 4 Class AB Output Stage Low Zout High Current Drive Short Circuit Protection] --> O[Output Vo]
    PS1[Positive Supply VCC +15V] --> S1
    PS1 --> S2
    PS1 --> S4
    PS2[Negative Supply VEE -15V] --> S1
    PS2 --> S2
    PS2 --> S4
    CC[Internal Compensation Capacitor 30 pF Miller Capacitor] --> S2
```

## 4.2 Op-Amp Pin Configuration Diagram ŌĆö IC 741 (8-Pin DIP)

The 8-pin Dual-Inline Package (DIP) of the ╬╝A741 ŌĆö the workhorse of academic labs and KTU examination questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PKG[IC 741 8 Pin DIP Top View Notch at Top]
        direction TB
        P1[Pin 1 Offset Null] --- P2[Pin 2 Inverting Input Vn]
        P2 --- P3[Pin 3 Non Inverting Input Vp]
        P3 --- P4[Pin 4 VEE Negative Supply -15V]
        P4 --- P5[Pin 5 Offset Null]
        P5 --- P6[Pin 6 Output Vo]
        P6 --- P7[Pin 7 VCC Positive Supply +15V]
        P7 --- P8[Pin 8 NC No Connection]
    end
    style PKG fill:#f5f5dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Signal Flow Architecture ŌĆö Op-Amp in Negative Feedback

The general closed-loop op-amp architecture, showing how external feedback sets the closed-loop gain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN[Input Signal Source] --> SUMM[ Summing Junction]
    VF[Feedback Network Beta] --> SUMM
    SUMM --> AMP[Op-Amp Core AOL Open Loop Gain]
    AMP --> VOUT[Output Vo]
    VOUT --> VF
    AMP -.-> LIM[Output Limited by Supply Rails Plus Minus VCC]
    style AMP fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VOUT fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Concept Map ŌĆö Ideal vs Real Op-Amp Trade-offs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IDEA[Ideal Op-Amp Pedagogical Construct] --> P1[Infinite AOL]
    IDEA --> P2[Infinite Zin]
    IDEA --> P3[Zero Zout]
    IDEA --> P4[Infinite CMRR]
    IDEA --> P5[Infinite Slew Rate]
    REAL[Real Op-Amp IC 741 and Modern] --> R1[AOL equals 2 times 10 to the 5]
    REAL --> R2[Zin equals 2 MegaOhm BJT or 10 to the 12 Ohm FET input]
    REAL --> R3[Zout equals 75 Ohm]
    REAL --> R4[CMRR equals 90 dB]
    REAL --> R5[SR equals 0.5 V per microsecond]
    R1 --> ADV[Trade off: gain for bandwidth]
    R2 --> ADV
    R3 --> ADV
    R4 --> ADV
    R5 --> ADV
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 / Model QP]`
**Define an operational amplifier. List any four ideal characteristics of an op-amp.**

**Model Answer:**
An **operational amplifier (op-amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain, differential-input voltage amplifier with a single-ended output, intended for use with external feedback to perform linear mathematical operations on analog signals.

**Four ideal characteristics:**

1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain (A_OL ŌåÆ Ōł×)**
2. **Infinite input impedance (Z_in ŌåÆ ╬®)** ŌåÆ no current flows into the input terminals.
3. **Zero output impedance (Z_out = 0)** ŌåÆ output voltage is independent of load.
4. **Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR ŌåÆ Ōł×)** ŌåÆ common-mode signals are completely rejected.

*(Other acceptable characteristics: infinite bandwidth, infinite slew rate, zero output when inputs are equal, zero noise, zero power consumption.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 / Model QP]`
**What is meant by CMRR of an op-amp? Write its expression in dB.**

**Model Answer:**
**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is a measure of the ability of the op-amp to reject signals that are common (i.e., identical in amplitude and phase) to both input terminals, while amplifying only the differential signal.

It is defined as the ratio of the **differential-mode gain (A_d)** to the **common-mode gain (A_cm)**:

$$CMRR = \left\vert \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$$

In decibels:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

**Higher CMRR ŌåÆ better noise rejection.** Typical value for IC 741: **90 dB**; for precision op-amps: **> 100 dB**.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 / Model QP]` ŌĆö **14 Marks**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the internal stages of a general-purpose op-amp. State the function of each stage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define the following op-amp parameters and state the typical value for IC 741:
    (i) Input offset voltage
    (ii) Input bias current
    (iii) Slew rate
    (iv) CMRR **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Question A

**(a) Internal stages of an op-amp** ŌĆö *[7 Marks]*

A general-purpose monolithic op-amp consists of **four cascaded stages** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram):

**Stage 1 ŌĆö Differential Input Stage:**
- Built from a **transistor differential pair** with a **current-mirror active load**.
- Provides **high input impedance** (Ōēź 2 M╬® for 741), **differential gain A_d**, and the **common-mode rejection** (high CMRR).
- Has a **differential output** (taken between the two collector nodes of the diff-pair).

**Stage 2 ŌĆö Gain Stage (Intermediate Amplifier):**
- A common-emitter amplifier with an **active load**.
- Provides the **bulk of the voltage gain** (typically 60ŌĆō80 dB).
- Includes the **Miller compensation capacitor C_C (Ōēł 30 pF in 741)** that ensures frequency stability by introducing a dominant pole.
- Converts the differential signal into a **single-ended signal**.

**Stage 3 ŌĆö Level Shifter (DC Bias Stage):**
- An emitter follower or V_BE multiplier circuit.
- Shifts the DC operating point **downward** so that the output can swing symmetrically around 0 V when both inputs are grounded.
- Provides high input impedance and low output impedance to avoid loading the gain stage.

**Stage 4 ŌĆö Output Stage (Push-Pull):**
- A **Class-AB complementary emitter follower** (NPN + PNP push-pull pair).
- Provides **low output impedance** (Ōēł 75 ╬®) and **high current drive capability** (┬▒20 mA for 741).
- Includes **short-circuit protection** circuitry to prevent damage.

**Overall open-loop gain** A_OL is the product of the four stage gains: A_OL = A_1 ┬Ę A_2 ┬Ę A_3 ┬Ę A_4 Ōēł 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ.

---

**(b) Definitions and 741 values** ŌĆö *[7 Marks]*

**(i) Input Offset Voltage (V_io):**
The small DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to make the output voltage exactly zero. Caused by **mismatch in the input differential pair transistors**.
**Typical 741 value:** ┬▒2 mV (can be trimmed to zero using the offset-null pins 1 and 5).

**(ii) Input Bias Current (I_B):**
The average DC current that flows into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals due to the biasing of the input transistors.
**Formula:** $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B}^{+} + I_{B}^{-}}{2}$
**Typical 741 value:** 80 nA (can be 500 nA maximum).

**(iii) Slew Rate (SR):**
The maximum rate at which the op-amp output voltage can change, limited by the internal compensation capacitor's charging current.
**Formula:** $SR = \left.\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}\right\vert_{max}$
**Typical 741 value:** 0.5 V/╬╝s.

**(iv) CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):**
The ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain, expressed in dB.
**Formula:** $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$
**Typical 741 value:** 90 dB.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 / Model QP]` ŌĆö **14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Derive the general op-amp output equation: $V_o = A_d(V_+ - V_-) + A_{cm}\left(\dfrac{V_+ + V_-}{2}\right)$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An op-amp has the following specifications: $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$, $A_{cm} = 4$, $V_+ = 5.05$ mV, $V_- = 5.00$ mV. Calculate:
    (i) Differential input voltage V_d
    (ii) Common-mode input voltage V_cm
    (iii) Output voltage V_o
    (iv) CMRR in dB **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Question B

**(a) Derivation of the op-amp output equation** ŌĆö *[7 Marks]*

Let VŌéü and VŌéé be the voltages applied to the inverting (ŌłÆ) and non-inverting (+) inputs respectively.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the differential-mode voltage V_d:**

$$V_{d} = V_{+} - V_{-} = V_{1} - V_{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the common-mode voltage V_cm (average of the two inputs):**

$$V_{cm} = \frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2} = \frac{V_{1} + V_{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express V+ and VŌłÆ in terms of V_d and V_cm.**

Adding V_d and 2V_cm:

$$V_{d} + 2V_{cm} = (V_{+} - V_{-}) + (V_{+} + V_{-}) = 2V_{+}$$

$$\therefore \quad V_{+} = \frac{V_{d}}{2} + V_{cm}$$

Similarly:

$$2V_{cm} - V_{d} = 2V_{-} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{-} = V_{cm} - \frac{V_{d}}{2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö By the principle of superposition, the output is the linear sum of two independent contributions:**

- Differential contribution: $A_d \cdot V_d$
- Common-mode contribution: $A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final result:**

$$\boxed{V_{o} = A_{d}\cdot V_{d} + A_{cm}\cdot V_{cm} = A_{d}(V_{+} - V_{-}) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2}\right)}$$

This is the **fundamental input-output equation** of any real op-amp and is the starting point for all KTU analysis questions.

---

**(b) Numerical problem** ŌĆö *[7 Marks]*

**Given:** $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$, $A_{cm} = 4$, $V_+ = 5.05$ mV, $V_- = 5.00$ mV

**(i) Differential input voltage:**

$$V_{d} = V_{+} - V_{-} = 5.05 \text{ mV} - 5.00 \text{ mV} = 0.05 \text{ mV} = 50\ \mu V$$

**(ii) Common-mode input voltage:**

$$V_{cm} = \frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2} = \frac{5.05 + 5.00}{2} = \frac{10.05}{2} = 5.025 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Output voltage:**

$$V_{o} = A_{d} V_{d} + A_{cm} V_{cm}$$

$$V_{o} = (2 \times 10^{5})(50 \times 10^{-6}) + (4)(5.025 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$V_{o} = 10 \text{ V} + 0.0201 \text{ V} = 10.0201 \text{ V}$$

*(Note: In practice this output would be clipped at +V_CC, typically +15 V or +12 V. We are performing an open-loop analytical calculation here.)*

**(iv) CMRR in dB:**

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{4}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{4})$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \times [\log_{10}(5) + 4] = 20 \times [0.6990 + 4] = 20 \times 4.6990$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 93.98 \text{ dB} \approx 94 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> **Pitfall 1 (most common, 2-mark penalty):** Forgetting to convert the millivolt/microvolt values to **Volts** before substituting. Mixing units (e.g., using mV in a ratio that expects V) is the #1 reason for arithmetic errors.
> 
> **Pitfall 2 (1-mark penalty):** Using `logŌéüŌéĆ` instead of `logŌéüŌéĆ(10 ├Ś CMRR)` for dB conversion. **Remember:** CMRR uses a factor of **20**, not 10, because it is a voltage (not power) ratio.
> 
> **Pitfall 3 (1-mark penalty):** Confusing the open-loop gain with the closed-loop gain. The open-loop value A_d = 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ is the internal gain of the op-amp, **not** the gain of any feedback configuration.
> 
> **Pitfall 4 (1-mark penalty):** In the derivation, students often jump from the definitions directly to the final equation without showing the algebraic manipulation of V+ and VŌłÆ. KTU requires the **explicit** intermediate steps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ­¤ōī **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 2: Op-Amp Basics**

- **Definition:** Op-amp = direct-coupled, high-gain, differential-input, single-ended-output amplifier. (3 marks guaranteed if you remember this verbatim.)
- **Standard symbol:** Triangle with `+` (non-inverting) and `ŌłÆ` (inverting) inputs; output at the apex; ┬▒V supply pins on top/bottom.
- **IC 741 pinout (8-pin DIP):** Pin 1, 5 = Offset Null | Pin 2 = Inverting | Pin 3 = Non-Inverting | Pin 4 = V_EE | Pin 6 = V_o | Pin 7 = V_CC | Pin 8 = NC.
- **Four internal stages:** Differential input ŌåÆ Voltage gain ŌåÆ Level shifter ŌåÆ Push-pull output. (Draw this in every block-diagram question ŌĆö examiners reward completeness.)
- **Master equation:** $V_o = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-)$. Expand to $V_o = A_d V_d + A_{cm} V_{cm}$ for real analysis.
- **Ideal Golden Rules (in negative feedback):**  
  ŌæĀ VŌéŖ = VŌéŗ (virtual short)  
  ŌæĪ IŌéŖ = IŌéŗ = 0 (infinite input impedance)
- **Key 741 specs (memorize these!):** A_OL = 2├Ś10ŌüĄ, Z_in = 2 M╬®, Z_out = 75 ╬®, I_B = 80 nA, V_io = 2 mV, CMRR = 90 dB, SR = 0.5 V/╬╝s, GBW = 1 MHz.
- **CMRR formula:** $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ ŌĆö uses **20**, not 10.
- **Slew rate limit on sine wave:** $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$ ŌĆö converts a sine into a triangle if exceeded.
- **Feedback equation (Bode):** $A_{CL} = A_{OL} / (1 + A_{OL}\beta) \approx 1/\beta$ ŌĆö explains why op-amp gain is set by **passive** components.
- **Virtual short ŌēĀ physical short** ŌĆö no current flows between the two input nodes.
- **Closed-loop is always used** in practical op-amp circuits; open-loop is used only in **comparators** and **Schmitt triggers**.
- **Open-loop bandwidth of 741 Ōēł 5 Hz**, but with feedback the bandwidth increases by the factor of gain reduction (GBW product constant).
- **Common examiners' trick:** They will give V+ and VŌłÆ in mV or ╬╝V ŌĆö always convert to Volts before substituting into the master equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of an Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Block Diagram of an Op-Amp ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp):** A direct-coupled, high-gain, DC voltage amplifier consisting of a differential input stage, an intermediate gain stage, a level shifter, and a push-pull output stage, designed to amplify the difference between two input voltages while rejecting common-mode signals. It is fundamentally a **Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source (VCVS)** with extremely high input impedance and near-zero output impedance. The **block diagram of an op-amp** is a high-level architectural representation that decomposes the monolithic op-amp IC into **four primary functional stages**, each responsible for a specific electrical characteristic. This representation is essential in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 because it bridges the gap between the **abstract symbol** (a triangle) used in circuit analysis and the **actual transistor-level implementation** found inside ICs like the ╬╝A741, LM324, or TL081. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The block diagram is the *conceptual prerequisite* for understanding op-amp internal compensation, slew rate, and frequency response ŌĆö all of which appear in later modules. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine an op-amp as a **highly sensitive weighing scale in a chemistry lab**: | Block Diagram Stage | Lab Analogy | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Input Differential Stage** | Two precision sensing probes placed on either side of a beaker | Detects the **tiny difference** between two weights while ignoring vibrations common to both | | **Gain Stage** | A mechanical lever amplification system | Multiplies the small detected difference into a **large, measurable signal** | | **Level Shifter** | A hydraulic elevator platform | Raises or lowers the **DC baseline** so the output signal is centered around 0V (ground) | | **Output Stage** | A powerful industrial pump | Delivers the amplified signal with **maximum strength** to the load without distortion | | **Biasing Circuit** | A regulated water supply tank | Provides a **stable internal reference** for all stages to operate correctly | The **differential input** is the key innovation ŌĆö like noise-cancelling headphones, the op-amp *subtracts* the two inputs to **reject common noise** (such as power supply hum or thermal drift) that affects both inputs equally. --- ### 1.3 Standard Physical & Electrical Constants > [!NOTE] > **Key Reference Values for the Ideal Op-Amp (KTU Reference):** > - **Differential Voltage Gain (A):** $A \to \infty$ (in practice, **A = 200,000** or **106 dB** for the classic ╬╝A741) > - **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** $Z_{in} \to \infty$ (practically **> 2 M╬®**) > - **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):** $Z_{out} \to 0$ (practically **< 75 ╬®**) > - **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** **Ōł× dB** (ideally), **90 dB** (typical for ╬╝A741) > - **Bandwidth:** **0 Hz to ~1 MHz** (for general-purpose op-amps like ╬╝A741) > - **Supply Voltages:** Typically **┬▒15 V DC** (symmetric dual supply) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four-Stage Architecture ŌĆö Why Each Block Exists The op-amp block diagram is not arbitrary. Each block exists to **solve a specific engineering problem** that arises from the previous stage. Understanding the **"Why"** behind each stage is critical for KTU board questions. #### Stage 1: Input Differential Amplifier (Dual Input, Balanced Output) - **Transistor configuration:** Emitter-coupled pair (BJT) or source-coupled pair (FET/MOSFET) with a **tail current source**. - **Why it is first:** Provides the **highest input impedance** (so the op-amp does not "load down" the source signal) and the **highest CMRR** (to reject noise common to both inputs). - **Output type:** **Balanced** (differential) ŌĆö taken between the two collectors/drains. - **Gain contribution:** Small voltage gain (typically 5 to 50), but enormous **CMRR** (> 90 dB). #### Stage 2: Intermediate Gain Stage (Dual Input, Unbalanced Output) - **Transistor configuration:** Another differential pair, often cascoded, followed by an **active load** (current mirror) and a common-emitter amplifier. - **Why it is needed:** The input stage alone provides insufficient voltage gain. This stage multiplies the differential signal by an additional factor of **1,000 to 10,000**. - **Output type:** **Unbalanced** (single-ended) ŌĆö referenced to ground. - **Gain contribution:** Massive voltage gain (the **primary gain provider** of the op-amp). This is where the bulk of the **~200,000 V/V** open-loop gain is generated. #### Stage 3: DC Level Shifter (Emitter Follower / Source Follower) - **Why it is needed:** After two stages of direct coupling, the DC voltage at the output drifts far from 0V (often sits at +10V to +12V due to cascaded emitter-follower offsets). The level shifter **slides the DC operating point back to 0V** without attenuating the AC signal. - **Configuration:** Emitter follower (BJT) with a **constant current source** as the emitter load, OR a PNP/NPN complementary shifter. - **Gain contribution:** **Unity voltage gain** ($A_v \approx 1$) ŌĆö does not amplify, but **preserves signal integrity** while shifting the DC level. #### Stage 4: Output Stage (Push-Pull Complementary Symmetry) - **Transistor configuration:** Class-AB push-pull pair ŌĆö an **NPN transistor** for sourcing current (positive half-cycle) and a **PNP transistor** for sinking current (negative half-cycle). - **Why it is needed:** The intermediate stage has high output impedance and cannot deliver significant current to a load. The output stage **buffers** the signal and provides **low output impedance** and **high current drive capability** (typically ┬▒20 mA for ╬╝A741). - **Gain contribution:** Slight current gain (voltage gain Ōēł 1). - **Protection:** Often includes **short-circuit current limiting** transistors to prevent damage. #### Stage 5 (Auxiliary): Biasing Circuit - **Function:** Provides **stable, temperature-compensated reference currents** to all stages using **current mirrors** (Wilson mirror, Widlar mirror). - **Why it is critical:** Without stable biasing, the op-amp's operating point drifts with temperature, destroying the linearity of the amplifier. --- ### 2.2 Two-Port Model of the Op-Amp From the block diagram, the op-amp can be reduced to a **universal two-port equivalent circuit** used in all KTU derivations: - **Input port:** Modeled as a **resistance $R_i$** (or $Z_i$) between the inverting (ŌłÆ) and non-inverting (+) terminals. No current flows into the ideal input. - **Output port:** Modeled as a **dependent voltage source $A \cdot V_d$** in series with an **output resistance $R_o$** (or $Z_o$). - **$V_d$ (Differential Input Voltage):** $V_d = V_+ - V_-$ --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Standard Value (╬╝A741) | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Differential Input Voltage** | $V_d = V_+ - V_-$ | Depends on inputs | Volts (V) | | 2 | **Open-Loop Voltage Gain** | $A = \dfrac{V_o}{V_d}$ | $2 \times 10^5$ (Ōēł 106 dB) | V/V (unitless) | | 3 | **Output Voltage Equation** | $V_o = A \cdot V_d = A(V_+ - V_-)$ | Depends on inputs | Volts (V) | | 4 | **Gain in Decibels** | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A\vert$ | $20 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) = 106$ dB | Decibels (dB) | | 5 | **Input Resistance** | $R_i = \dfrac{V_d}{I_{in}}$ | $> 2 \text{ M}\Omega$ | Ohms (╬®) | | 6 | **Output Resistance** | $R_o$ | $< 75 \text{ }\Omega$ | Ohms (╬®) | | 7 | **CMRR** | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | 90 dB | Decibels (dB) | | 8 | **Slew Rate** | $\text{SR} = \left.\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}\right\rfloor_{max}$ | $0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ | V/╬╝s | | 9 | **Overall Block Diagram Gain** | $A_{total} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^5$ | V/V | | 10 | **Level Shifter Constraint** | $V_{DC,\,out} = 0$ V | 0 V (must be ground) | Volts (V) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The "A" in $V_o = A \cdot V_d$ is the **open-loop differential gain** ŌĆö the product of gains from all four stages. When a feedback network is added (covered in Module 3), this "A" becomes part of the **closed-loop gain** equation. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The block diagram is the foundation for: - **PCB Design:** Engineers use the block diagram to identify which pins (input, output, power, null offset) connect to which internal stage. - **Failure Analysis:** When an op-amp malfunctions, technicians trace the fault back to a specific stage (e.g., a saturated output stage indicates an issue upstream). - **IC Design:** Modern op-amp design tools (Cadence, LTSpice) simulate each block separately before integrating them. - **Application Areas:** Instrumentation amplifiers, active filters, voltage regulators, A/D and D/A converters, sample-and-hold circuits, and analog computers ŌĆö **all rely on the multi-stage architecture** described by the block diagram. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage as a Function of Stage Gains **Problem Statement:** Given an op-amp block diagram with four cascaded stages having individual voltage gains $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4$, derive the total open-loop voltage gain and the output voltage expression. **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the input to the system.** The differential input signal is applied between the non-inverting (+) and inverting (ŌłÆ) terminals. $$ V_d = V_+ - V_- $$ This is the input to **Stage 1 (Differential Amplifier)**. **Step 2 ŌĆö Output of Stage 1 (Balanced Output).** The differential pair amplifies $V_d$ by a factor $A_1$ and produces a balanced (floating) output between its two output nodes. $$ V_1 = A_1 \cdot V_d $$ The output is still differential: $V_1 = V_{1a} - V_{1b}$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Output of Stage 2 (Intermediate Gain Stage).** This stage takes the differential signal $V_1$ and converts it to a single-ended (unbalanced) output referenced to ground, while multiplying by $A_2$. $$ V_2 = A_2 \cdot V_1 $$ Substituting the expression for $V_1$: $$ V_2 = A_2 \cdot (A_1 \cdot V_d) = A_1 A_2 \cdot V_d $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Output of Stage 3 (Level Shifter).** The level shifter has approximately unity voltage gain ($A_3 \approx 1$), but shifts the DC level. The AC signal is preserved. $$ V_3 = A_3 \cdot V_2 \approx V_2 $$ Therefore: $$ V_3 = A_1 A_2 A_3 \cdot V_d $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Output of Stage 4 (Push-Pull Output Stage).** The output stage is a unity-gain buffer (emitter follower configuration) that provides current amplification. $$ V_4 = A_4 \cdot V_3 $$ With $A_4 \approx 1$: $$ V_4 = A_1 A_2 A_3 A_4 \cdot V_d $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Final Output Voltage Expression.** $$ V_o = A \cdot V_d $$ where the total open-loop gain is: $$ A = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4 $$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Numerical Example (╬╝A741 Op-Amp).** For a typical ╬╝A741, the gain distribution is: - $A_1 = 10$ (input differential stage) - $A_2 = 20{,}000$ (intermediate gain stage with active load) - $A_3 = 1$ (level shifter) - $A_4 = 1$ (output stage) Therefore: $$ A_{total} = 10 \times 20{,}000 \times 1 \times 1 = 200{,}000 \text{ V/V} $$ In decibels: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) = 20 \times 5.301 = 106.02 \text{ dB} $$ For an input differential voltage of $V_d = 0.1$ mV = $10^{-4}$ V, the output would be: $$ V_o = 2 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-4} = 20 \text{ V} $$ Since the supply is ┬▒15V, this output would **saturate** at +15V, demonstrating why op-amps require negative feedback in practical applications. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Stage-by-Stage Comparison | Property | Stage 1 (Input Diff Amp) | Stage 2 (Gain Stage) | Stage 3 (Level Shifter) | Stage 4 (Output Stage) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Input Type** | Dual (V+, VŌłÆ) | Dual (balanced) | Single (unbalanced) | Single (unbalanced) | | **Output Type** | Balanced (differential) | Unbalanced (single-ended) | Unbalanced | Unbalanced | | **Voltage Gain (AßĄź)** | Low (5ŌĆō50) | Very High (1000ŌĆō20000) | Unity (Ōēł 1) | Unity (Ōēł 1) | | **Current Gain (AßĄó)** | Moderate | Moderate | High (buffer) | Very High (Ōēł 1000) | | **Function** | High $Z_{in}$, High CMRR | Provide bulk of $A$ | DC level shift to 0V | Low $Z_{out}$, drive load | | **Key Configuration** | Emitter-coupled pair + tail current source | Diff pair + current mirror + CE amplifier | Emitter follower + constant $I$ source | Class-AB push-pull (NPN + PNP) | | **Power Consumption** | Low | Low | Low | High (drives the load) | | **Typical BJT Count (╬╝A741)** | ~8 transistors | ~15 transistors | ~3 transistors | ~10 transistors | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram of an Op-Amp ```mermaid graph LR A[Vin_plus
Non-Inverting Input] --> S1 B[Vin_minus
Inverting Input] --> S1 S1[Stage 1
Input Differential Amplifier
Dual Input Balanced Output
High Zin High CMRR] --> S2 S2[Stage 2
Intermediate Gain Stage
Dual Input Unbalanced Output
High Voltage Gain] --> S3 S3[Stage 3
DC Level Shifter
Unity Gain Emitter Follower
Shifts DC to Zero Volts] --> S4 S4[Stage 4
Push-Pull Output Stage
Class AB Complementary
Low Zout High Current] --> O[Vo
Output Voltage] BC[Biasing Circuit
Current Mirrors and
Constant Current Sources] -.-> S1 BC -.-> S2 BC -.-> S3 BC -.-> S4 PS1[+VCC Supply] -.-> S1 PS1 -.-> S2 PS1 -.-> S4 PS2[-VEE Supply] -.-> S1 PS2 -.-> S2 PS2 -.-> S4 ``` ### 4.2 Two-Port Equivalent Model (For Circuit Analysis) ```mermaid graph LR VIP[Vin_plus] --> INPORT VIM[Vin_minus] --> INPORT INPORT[Input Port
Ri between plus and minus] --> AMP AMP[Dependent Voltage Source
A times Vd] --> OUTPORT[Output Port
Ro in series] OUTPORT --> VO[Vo] Vd[Vd = Vplus minus Vminus] --> AMP ``` ### 4.3 Signal Flow and Gain Distribution Chart ```mermaid graph TD VD[Input: Vd = Vplus - Vminus] -->|Multiply by A1| A1B[After Stage 1
Gain A1 of 10] A1B -->|Multiply by A2| A2B[After Stage 2
Gain A1A2 equals 200000] A2B -->|Multiply by A3 approx 1| A3B[After Stage 3
DC Level Shifted to 0V] A3B -->|Multiply by A4 approx 1| A4B[After Stage 4
Vo = A times Vd] A4B --> OUT[Output: Vo] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice that the **biasing circuit** and **power supplies** are drawn with **dotted lines**, indicating that they are *supporting infrastructure* rather than signal-carrying paths. The **solid arrows** show the actual signal flow from input to output. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** *Draw the block diagram of an op-amp and explain the function of each block.* **Model Answer:** The op-amp block diagram consists of **four main stages** plus a **biasing circuit**: 1. **Input Differential Amplifier Stage (Dual Input, Balanced Output):** Uses an emitter-coupled pair with a tail current source. Provides high input impedance ($Z_{in}$) and high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR). Voltage gain is moderate. 2. **Intermediate Gain Stage (Dual Input, Unbalanced Output):** Cascaded differential amplifier with an active current-mirror load. Provides the **bulk of the open-loop voltage gain** (typically 10,000 to 20,000). Converts differential signal to single-ended output. 3. **DC Level Shifter:** Emitter follower with constant current source. Shifts the DC operating point back to 0V without attenuating the AC signal. Voltage gain Ōēł 1. 4. **Output Stage (Push-Pull Complementary Symmetry):** Class-AB configuration using complementary NPN and PNP transistors. Provides low output impedance ($Z_{out}$) and high current drive capability to the load. Includes short-circuit protection. 5. **Biasing Circuit:** Current mirrors (Wilson/Widlar) provide stable, temperature-compensated reference currents to all stages. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]** *What is the function of the level shifter stage in an op-amp? Why is it required?* **Model Answer:** The **level shifter** is the third stage of the op-amp block diagram. Its primary function is to **shift the DC operating point** of the signal from a high positive voltage (typically +10V to +12V, accumulated due to direct coupling in the first two stages) **back to 0V** at the output, so that the op-amp output can swing symmetrically between the positive and negative supply rails. **Why it is required:** Direct-coupled amplifiers suffer from **DC drift** ŌĆö each stage adds its $V_{BE}$ offset. Without the level shifter, the output DC level would be far from 0V, causing severe **asymmetric clipping** and rendering the op-amp unusable in AC applications. The level shifter is configured as an **emitter follower with a constant current source** as the emitter load. It provides unity voltage gain (so the AC signal is preserved) and high current gain (to drive the output stage). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Draw the detailed block diagram of a general-purpose op-amp. Explain the function and characteristics of each stage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of the op-amp in terms of the differential input voltage and the individual stage gains. If $A_1 = 10$, $A_2 = 20{,}000$, $A_3 = 1$, and $A_4 = 1$, calculate the total open-loop voltage gain in V/V and in dB. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a):** [Refer to the block diagram drawn in SECTION 4 above] | Stage | Function | Key Characteristic | |---|---|---| | 1. Input Diff Amp | Detects small differential signals | $Z_{in} > 2$ M╬®, CMRR > 90 dB | | 2. Gain Stage | Provides bulk of voltage gain | $A_v = 10{,}000$ to $20{,}000$ | | 3. Level Shifter | Shifts DC level to 0V | $A_v \approx 1$, high current gain | | 4. Output Stage | Delivers current to load | $Z_{out} < 75$ ╬®, $I_{out} = \pm 20$ mA | | 5. Biasing | Stable reference currents | Temperature compensation | **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Step 1:** Define the differential input: $$ V_d = V_+ - V_- $$ **Step 2:** Cascade through all four stages: $$ V_o = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4 \cdot V_d $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the given values: $$ A_{total} = 10 \times 20{,}000 \times 1 \times 1 $$ $$ A_{total} = 200{,}000 \text{ V/V} $$ **Step 4:** Convert to decibels: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(200{,}000) $$ $$ A_{dB} = 20 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) $$ $$ A_{dB} = 20 \times [0.301 + 5] $$ $$ A_{dB} = 20 \times 5.301 $$ $$ \boxed{A_{dB} = 106.02 \text{ dB}} $$ --- **Q4. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme] (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how the various stages of an op-amp are interconnected. Discuss the role of the biasing circuit. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An op-amp has an open-loop gain $A = 2 \times 10^5$. The differential input voltage is $V_d = 50 \, \mu\text{V}$. Calculate: (i) The output voltage assuming no saturation, and (ii) The output voltage considering supply voltages of ┬▒15V. What is your conclusion? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a):** The op-amp is internally constructed as a **cascaded chain of four amplifier stages**, with a parallel biasing network providing reference currents. - **Stage 1 (Input)** is connected directly to the external input pins (+ and ŌłÆ). Its balanced output drives Stage 2. - **Stage 2 (Gain)** receives the balanced signal and produces a single-ended high-gain output. - **Stage 3 (Level Shifter)** is coupled to Stage 2 by a capacitor or direct connection and drives Stage 4. - **Stage 4 (Output)** is the final buffer that connects to the external output pin. The **biasing circuit** is a network of current mirrors (typically using a reference resistor and a master current source) that distributes stable bias currents to the tail of the input diff pair, the active load of the gain stage, and the emitter/source resistors of the level shifter and output stage. It ensures **thermal stability** and **predictable operating points** across temperature variations. **Model Solution for Part (b):** **(i) Output without saturation:** $$ V_o = A \cdot V_d = 2 \times 10^5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ V_o = 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ V_o = 10 \text{ V} $$ **(ii) Output considering supply rails:** Since the calculated $V_o = 10$ V is within the supply range of ┬▒15V, the op-amp operates in the **linear region** and the output is: $$ \boxed{V_o = 10 \text{ V}} $$ **Conclusion:** The output 10V is achievable. If $V_d$ were larger (e.g., $V_d > 75 \, \mu$V), the output would saturate at +15V or ŌłÆ15V, demonstrating that the high open-loop gain causes saturation for very small input signals ŌĆö this is the primary reason **negative feedback** is essential in practical op-amp circuits. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing balanced vs unbalanced outputs:** Stage 1 produces a *balanced* (differential) output, NOT single-ended. Stage 2 is what *converts* it to unbalanced. Drawing Stage 1 with a single-ended output will cost 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the level shifter:** Many students omit Stage 3 entirely. The level shifter is **mandatory** in the KTU 2024 syllabus and is worth 1ŌĆō2 marks. > 3. **Wrong decibels conversion:** Use $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage gain, NOT $10 \log_{10}$ (which is for power). > 4. **Ignoring the biasing circuit:** Even though it does not appear in the main signal path, the examiner expects it drawn with dotted lines and labeled. > 5. **Saturation oversight:** Always check if the calculated $V_o$ exceeds $V_{CC}$. If yes, the output saturates at $V_{CC}$ or $V_{EE}$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 ŌĆö Block Diagram of Op-Amp:** - **Op-Amp = VCVS** (Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source) with four cascaded stages. - **Stage 1:** Differential amplifier ŌåÆ **High $Z_{in}$, High CMRR**; Input is dual, output is balanced. - **Stage 2:** Intermediate gain stage ŌåÆ **Bulk of voltage gain** (~ 20,000); Input is dual, output is unbalanced. - **Stage 3:** Level shifter ŌåÆ **Shifts DC to 0V**; Unity voltage gain, high current gain. - **Stage 4:** Push-pull output ŌåÆ **Low $Z_{out}$, high current drive**; Class-AB complementary symmetry. - **Biasing circuit:** Current mirrors for thermal stability; not in signal path (dotted lines). - **Master Equation:** $V_o = A \cdot V_d = A_1 A_2 A_3 A_4 \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$. - **╬╝A741 Typical Values:** $A = 2 \times 10^5$ (106 dB), $Z_{in} > 2$ M╬®, $Z_{out} < 75$ ╬®, CMRR = 90 dB. - **dB Conversion:** Always use $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage gain ratios. - **Saturation Rule:** If $V_o > V_{CC}$, the op-amp output saturates at $V_{CC}$; if $V_o < V_{EE}$, it saturates at $V_{EE}$. - **Why level shifter is needed:** Direct coupling causes DC drift; the level shifter brings the output DC level back to 0V for symmetric swing. - **Open-loop vs Closed-loop:** Block diagram describes the **open-loop** internal architecture; feedback networks (covered in Module 3) create closed-loop configurations.

topics.title: Op-Amp Parameters - Slew Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Op-Amp Parameters - Slew Rate**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slew Rate (SR)** of an operational amplifier is formally defined as the **maximum rate of change of the output voltage with respect to time**, under large-signal conditions. Mathematically, it is expressed as:
>
> $$SR = \left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max}$$
>
> The standard unit of measurement is **Volts per microsecond (V/╬╝s)**.

Slew Rate is a critical **dynamic (large-signal) parameter** of an operational amplifier that quantifies how *fast* the output of the op-amp can swing in response to a step change in the input signal. It is fundamentally limited by the internal architecture of the op-amp, specifically the **internal compensation capacitor** and the **maximum bias current** available to charge or discharge it.

In the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for PBRAT304, Slew Rate is grouped under the operational characteristics of the ideal and practical op-amp and is one of the most frequently tested parameters in both internal assessments and the End Semester Examination (ESE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Small-signal bandwidth** (Gain-Bandwidth Product) governs *linear* high-frequency response.
> - **Slew Rate** governs *large-signal* transient response.
> - Confusing these two concepts is a common cause of mark deduction in KTU valuation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are sitting inside a **sports car** with a top speed of **200 km/h** and excellent precise steering (small-signal response). However, the car's **accelerator pedal** can only be pressed to a maximum depth ŌĆö meaning the car cannot go from **0 to 200 km/h instantaneously**; it takes a few seconds to reach top speed.

In this analogy:
- The **car's top speed** = the op-amp's maximum output voltage swing (saturation limits).
- The **rate of acceleration** = the **Slew Rate** of the op-amp.
- The **small-signal bandwidth** = the steering responsiveness (how well it handles tiny corrections).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** Think "Slew Rate = Speed Limit of Voltage Change". An op-amp with SR = 0.5 V/╬╝s can only change its output by 0.5 V every microsecond, no matter how large or fast the input is.

## 1.3 Why Slew Rate Exists ŌĆö The Physical Origin

Slew rate is **not** a design choice ŌĆö it is a *physical consequence* of the op-amp's internal transistor stages. The dominant mechanism is:

1. A **compensation capacitor (C)** is intentionally placed inside the op-amp (typically around 30 pF in a 741-type op-amp) to ensure **frequency stability** and prevent oscillations.
2. The op-amp can only supply a **finite bias current (I_max)** to charge or discharge this capacitor.
3. The voltage across a capacitor cannot change faster than:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I}{C}$$

4. Therefore, the output of the op-amp is **fundamentally limited** by this charging rate.

This is the deep reason why *every practical op-amp has a finite slew rate* ŌĆö and why faster, wider-bandwidth op-amps use clever internal designs (current mirrors, feedforward paths, JFET inputs) to push SR into the hundreds or thousands of V/╬╝s.

## 1.4 Typical Slew Rate Values (Reference Data)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are **typical manufacturer-specified** values for popular op-amps. These are useful for KTU numerical problems.

| Op-Amp Family | Typical Slew Rate |
|---------------|-------------------|
| LM741 (general purpose BJT) | **0.5 V/╬╝s** |
| LM324 (general purpose) | **0.4 V/╬╝s** |
| TL081 (JFET input) | **16 V/╬╝s** |
| LF351 (JFET) | **13 V/╬╝s** |
| OP-27 (precision) | **2.8 V/╬╝s** |
| LM318 (high-speed) | **70 V/╬╝s** |
| AD844 (current feedback) | **2000 V/╬╝s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Mechanism

The slew rate phenomenon can be understood by following the signal path inside the op-amp:

1. **Input Stage Differential Amplifier:** The differential input pair amplifies the difference between the two input terminals (V+ and VŌłÆ).
2. **High-Gain Intermediate Stage:** This stage provides most of the voltage gain of the op-amp. The **Miller compensation capacitor (C_c)** is connected across this stage.
3. **Output Stage (Push-Pull Emitter Follower):** This stage provides current drive capability to the load.
4. **Current Limitation:** A constant bias current source (I_bias) sets the maximum current available to charge/discharge C_c.
5. **Resulting Behavior:** When a *large* input step is applied, the op-amp does not respond instantaneously. Instead, the output rises (or falls) **linearly** with time at a rate of I_bias / C_c until it approaches the final value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Slew rate is a *non-linear, large-signal* effect. It occurs because internal transistors enter **saturation or cutoff** when the input is large, leaving the bias current as the only mechanism that can move charge.

## 2.2 Slew Rate of a Sine Wave ŌĆö Mathematical Derivation

When a sinusoidal signal is applied to an op-amp circuit, the output is also a sinusoid. The instantaneous rate of change of the output voltage is:

$$V_o(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The **maximum** rate of change occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$, i.e., at the zero-crossings of the sine wave:

$$\left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max} = \omega V_p = 2\pi f V_p$$

For the op-amp to reproduce the sine wave **without slew-induced distortion**, its slew rate must be at least equal to the maximum rate of change of the output:

$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$$

If the input frequency or amplitude exceeds this limit, the output waveform becomes **triangular** instead of sinusoidal ŌĆö a phenomenon called **slew-induced distortion** (or "slew rate limiting").

## 2.3 Full-Power Bandwidth (f_P)

The **Full-Power Bandwidth** is defined as the maximum frequency at which the op-amp can produce a *full-amplitude* (unclipped) sinusoidal output. It is directly derived from the slew rate specification:

$$f_P = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

Where:
- $SR$ = Slew Rate in V/s (convert V/╬╝s ŌåÆ V/s by multiplying by $10^6$)
- $V_p$ = Peak amplitude of the output sine wave
- $f_P$ = Full-power bandwidth in Hz

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students frequently forget to convert V/╬╝s to V/s before plugging into the formula. Always do the unit conversion explicitly: $SR (\text{in V/╬╝s}) \times 10^6 = SR (\text{in V/s})$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** for solving KTU exam problems on Slew Rate. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Slew Rate (Definition) | $SR = \left. \dfrac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max}$ | V/╬╝s or V/s | Maximum rate; large-signal parameter |
| 2 | Internal Origin | $SR = \dfrac{I_{\max}}{C_c}$ | V/s | $I_{\max}$ = bias current; $C_c$ = compensation capacitor |
| 3 | Sine Wave Condition | $SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$ | V/s | Required to avoid slew-induced distortion |
| 4 | Full-Power Bandwidth | $f_P = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$ | Hz | Maximum freq for full amplitude output |
| 5 | Maximum Frequency for Sine | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$ | Hz | Same as $f_P$ |
| 6 | Rise Time Approximation | $t_r \approx \dfrac{\Delta V}{SR}$ | ╬╝s | For large-signal step response |
| 7 | Slew Rate in V/s from V/╬╝s | $SR_{\text{V/s}} = SR_{\text{V/╬╝s}} \times 10^6$ | ŌĆö | Unit conversion factor |
| 8 | Slope of Output Triangle | $\text{Slope} = \pm SR$ | V/╬╝s | During slew-limited region |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Slew rate is not merely an academic concept. It has direct consequences in engineering applications:

- **Audio Amplifiers:** Insufficient slew rate causes **TIM distortion** (Transient Intermodulation Distortion) in high-fidelity audio systems, producing harsh, unnatural sound.
- **Video Amplifiers:** Bandwidth and slew rate together determine the ability to reproduce sharp transitions in video signals. Insufficient SR causes **smearing of edges** in CRT/LCD displays.
- **Data Acquisition Systems:** Sample-and-hold circuits and ADC input buffers require high SR to track rapidly changing signals without losing amplitude.
- **Pulse Amplification:** In radar, pulse-code modulation, and digital communication, **rise time of pulses** is governed by SR.
- **Function Generators and Oscilloscope Front-Ends:** These demand op-amps with extremely high SR (hundreds to thousands of V/╬╝s).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For an op-amp driving a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_p$ at frequency $f$, always choose an op-amp with $SR \geq 2\pi f V_p \times 3$ to have a **safety margin of 3├Ś** against slew-induced distortion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why the Output of a Slew-Limited Op-Amp Becomes a Triangle Wave

**Problem Setup:**
A unity-gain buffer using an op-amp with slew rate $SR$ is fed with a sinusoidal input $V_i(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$. Derive the condition under which the output becomes slew-limited (triangular), and find the maximum frequency for an undistorted sinusoidal output of peak amplitude $V_p$.

### Step 1: Express the Output Voltage

The output of the buffer ideally equals the input:

$$V_o(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 2: Differentiate the Output to Find the Instantaneous Rate of Change

Taking the time derivative:

$$\frac{dV_o(t)}{dt} = V_p \cdot 2\pi f \cdot \cos(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 3: Identify the Maximum Rate of Change

The cosine function $\cos(2\pi f t)$ has a maximum value of **+1** and a minimum value of **ŌłÆ1**. Therefore, the maximum magnitude of the rate of change of the output is:

$$\left\vert \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

### Step 4: Apply the Slew Rate Constraint

The op-amp can change its output at a maximum rate equal to its slew rate $SR$. If the required rate of change exceeds $SR$, the op-amp cannot keep up, and the output is **slew-limited**.

The condition for **no distortion** (faithful sine reproduction) is:

$$2\pi f V_p \leq SR$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Maximum Frequency

Rearranging the inequality:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

This is the **Full-Power Bandwidth**, $f_P$.

### Step 6: Numerical Example

**Given:** $SR = 0.5$ V/╬╝s, $V_p = 5$ V. Find the maximum sine wave frequency.

**Solution:**

First, convert slew rate to SI units:

$$SR = 0.5 \, \frac{\text{V}}{\mu\text{s}} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \, \frac{\text{V}}{\text{s}} = 5 \times 10^5 \, \frac{\text{V}}{\text{s}}$$

Apply the formula:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{10^5}{2\pi} \approx 15{,}915 \, \text{Hz} \approx 15.9 \, \text{kHz}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Rise Time from Slew Rate

**Problem Setup:**
A non-inverting amplifier with op-amp slew rate $SR$ has a step input that causes the output to swing from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$. Derive the rise time.

### Step 1: Define the Total Output Swing

$$\Delta V_o = V_{sat} - (-V_{sat}) = 2V_{sat}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Definition of Slew Rate

During the slew-limited region, the output rises linearly at rate $SR$:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = SR \implies \Delta V_o = SR \cdot t_r$$

### Step 3: Solve for Rise Time

$$t_r = \frac{\Delta V_o}{SR} = \frac{2V_{sat}}{SR}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

**Given:** Op-amp 741 with $SR = 0.5$ V/╬╝s, supply $\pm 15$ V (so $V_{sat} \approx 13$ V typically). Find $t_r$.

**Solution:**

$$t_r = \frac{2 \times 13}{0.5} = \frac{26}{0.5} = 52 \, \mu\text{s}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Maximum Undistorted Sine Amplitude

**Problem Setup:**
An op-amp with slew rate $SR$ must reproduce a sine wave of frequency $f$ without distortion. Find the maximum peak amplitude $V_{p,\max}$.

### Step 1: Start with the Slew Rate Condition

$$SR = 2\pi f V_{p,\max}$$

### Step 2: Solve Algebraically

$$V_{p,\max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi f}$$

### Step 3: Verify Units

If $SR$ is in V/s and $f$ in Hz, then $V_{p,\max}$ is in Volts. If $SR$ is in V/╬╝s, multiply by $10^6$ first.

### Step 4: Numerical Example

**Given:** $SR = 0.5$ V/╬╝s, $f = 10$ kHz. Find $V_{p,\max}$.

**Solution:**

Convert: $SR = 0.5 \times 10^6$ V/s.

$$V_{p,\max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10^4} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^2}{2\pi} = \frac{50}{2\pi} \approx 7.96 \, \text{V}$$

This is approximately **8 V peak**, which is a typical KTU numerical problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Causes, Effects, and Mitigation

| Aspect | Description | Engineering Implication |
|--------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Primary Cause** | Limited internal bias current $I_{\max}$ to charge compensation capacitor $C_c$ | Cannot be eliminated; only mitigated by design |
| **Secondary Cause** | Saturation of internal transistor stages under large signals | Inherent to BJT/JFET op-amp architectures |
| **Effect on Sine Wave** | Triangular output at high frequency or amplitude | Slew-induced distortion; unusable in precision AC applications |
| **Effect on Pulse** | Linear ramp instead of sharp edge | Limits rise time of pulse amplifiers |
| **Effect on DC** | No effect; DC is not slew-rate limited | Slew rate is purely a dynamic limitation |
| **Measurement Method** | Apply large step input, measure slope of output | Standard test in datasheets |
| **Improvement Method** | Use op-amps with feedforward, JFET, or current-feedback topology | Higher SR; cost increases |
| **Trade-off** | Higher SR often means higher quiescent current | Power consumption increases |
| **Relationship to Bandwidth** | Independent of GBP for slow signals; dominant for fast large signals | Two separate but related specifications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** The three most common numerical problems on Slew Rate are:
> 1. Compute $f_{\max}$ given $SR$ and $V_p$.
> 2. Compute $V_{p,\max}$ given $SR$ and $f$.
> 3. Compute rise time given $SR$ and output swing.
> Memorize the single master equation $SR = 2\pi f V_p$ and the three variations follow by simple algebra.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Slew Rate Mechanism Inside an Op-Amp

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **functional architecture** of an op-amp and pinpoints exactly where the slew rate limitation originates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Differential Input Stage Vplus and Vminus"] --> nodeB["High Gain Intermediate Stage with Miller Capacitor Cc"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Output Buffer Push Pull Emitter Follower"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Load and External Output Vo"]
    nodeE["Constant Bias Current Source Imax"] --> nodeB
    nodeF["Slew Rate Limitation Point dVo over dt equals Imax over Cc"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG["Output Slew Limited Linear Ramp"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Comparator: Compare 2 pi f Vp with SR"]
    nodeH -->|"SR greater or equal"| nodeI["Undistorted Sine Wave Output"]
    nodeH -->|"SR less than 2 pi f Vp"| nodeJ["Triangular Distorted Output Slew Induced Distortion"]
    style nodeA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    style nodeF fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000
    style nodeG fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    style nodeH fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style nodeI fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style nodeJ fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Timeline Diagram: Slew-Rate Limited Step Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Time t equals 0: Large Step Input Applied"] --> nodeB["Output Begins Linear Ramp at Rate SR"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Slope equals SR in V per microsecond"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Time equals t1: Output Reaches Final Value"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Output Settles Within Slew Free Region"]
    style nodeA fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Sine Wave Distortion Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Sine Wave Input with Frequency f and Peak Vp"] --> nodeCompute["Compute Required Slew Rate: 2 pi f Vp"]
    nodeCompute --> nodeCheck{"Op-Amp SR greater or equal to 2 pi f Vp?"}
    nodeCheck -->|"Yes"| nodePass["Output Faithfully Reproduces Sine Wave: No Distortion"]
    nodeCheck -->|"No"| nodeFail["Output Becomes Triangular: Slew Induced Distortion Present"]
    nodeFail --> nodeMitigate["Mitigation: Use Faster Op-Amp, Reduce Amplitude, or Reduce Frequency"]
    style nodeStart fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    style nodeCompute fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style nodeCheck fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    style nodePass fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style nodeFail fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style nodeMitigate fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#558B2F,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram Description: Op-Amp Internal Architecture

The op-amp can be functionally decomposed into **three cascaded stages** for understanding slew rate:

1. **Input Differential Pair:** Converts differential input voltage to a differential current.
2. **High-Gain CE Stage with Miller Capacitor:** Provides voltage gain; the compensation capacitor C_c is the bottleneck for slew rate. The bias current $I_{\max}$ can only charge/discharge this capacitor at a finite rate.
3. **Output Emitter Follower (Class AB):** Provides current drive to the load; does not limit slew rate but delivers the buffered output.

The **slew rate bottleneck** is the **second stage**, where the Miller capacitor must be charged or discharged by a fixed bias current. This is why $SR = I_{\max} / C_c$ is the fundamental physical relationship.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define the Slew Rate of an operational amplifier. State its typical unit and a typical value for the 741 op-amp.**

**Model Answer:**

Slew Rate (SR) of an op-amp is defined as the **maximum rate of change of the output voltage with respect to time** under large-signal conditions. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max}$$

The standard unit is **Volts per microsecond (V/╬╝s)**. For the popular **LM741 op-amp**, the typical slew rate is **0.5 V/╬╝s**.

Slew rate is caused by the **limited internal bias current** available to charge the **internal compensation capacitor** of the op-amp. A higher slew rate indicates that the op-amp can respond to rapid changes in the input signal more faithfully, which is essential for high-frequency and large-signal applications.

*(Word count: ~85 ŌĆö appropriate for 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is meant by Full-Power Bandwidth? How is it related to the slew rate of an op-amp?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Full-Power Bandwidth ($f_P$)** of an op-amp is defined as the **maximum frequency at which the op-amp can produce a sinusoidal output of maximum (unclipped) amplitude without slew-induced distortion**.

It is related to the slew rate by the following equation:

$$f_P = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

Where $V_p$ is the **peak amplitude** of the output sine wave, and $SR$ is the slew rate (in V/s).

If the input frequency exceeds $f_P$, the op-amp cannot change its output fast enough, and the output waveform becomes **triangular** (slew-limited) instead of sinusoidal.

*(Word count: ~80 ŌĆö appropriate for 3-mark answer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]

**[A] (a)** Define Slew Rate. Derive the relationship between Slew Rate, frequency, and peak amplitude of a sine wave. **[7 Marks]**

**[A] (b)** An op-amp with slew rate 0.5 V/╬╝s is used in a unity-gain buffer configuration. If the input is a sine wave of 10 V peak amplitude, calculate:
   (i) The maximum frequency at which the output remains undistorted.
   (ii) The maximum peak amplitude at 25 kHz without slew-induced distortion.
   (iii) The rise time of the output for a swing of 20 V peak-to-peak. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for [A] (a):

**Definition of Slew Rate:**

Slew Rate (SR) of an op-amp is defined as the maximum rate of change of the output voltage with respect to time under large-signal conditions:

$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max}$$

**Derivation:**

Consider a sine wave output:

$$V_o(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Maximum value of $\cos(\omega t)$ is 1, occurring at zero-crossings of the sine wave:

$$\left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max} = \omega V_p = 2\pi f V_p$$

For distortion-free reproduction, the op-amp slew rate must satisfy:

$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$$

Hence, the relationship is:

$$\boxed{SR = 2\pi f V_p}$$

#### Model Solution for [A] (b):

**Given:** $SR = 0.5$ V/╬╝s, unity-gain buffer (so $V_o = V_i$).

**Convert SR to V/s:**

$$SR = 0.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/s}$$

**(i) Maximum frequency at $V_p = 10$ V:**

$$f_{\max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10}$$

$$f_{\max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^5}{2\pi} = \frac{50000}{6.2832} \approx 7957.7 \, \text{Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{\max} \approx 7.96 \, \text{kHz}}$$

**(ii) Maximum peak amplitude at $f = 25$ kHz:**

$$V_{p,\max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi f} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{p,\max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{1.5708 \times 10^5} \approx 3.183 \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{p,\max} \approx 3.18 \, \text{V}}$$

**(iii) Rise time for 20 V peak-to-peak swing:**

$$t_r = \frac{\Delta V}{SR} = \frac{20}{0.5} = 40 \, \mu\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_r = 40 \, \mu\text{s}}$$

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]

**[B] (a)** Explain the physical origin of Slew Rate in an op-amp. Discuss why the output becomes triangular when the slew rate is exceeded. **[7 Marks]**

**[B] (b)** A 741 op-amp with slew rate 0.5 V/╬╝s is used in a non-inverting amplifier of gain 10. If the input is a 1 V peak, 50 kHz sine wave, determine:
   (i) The output peak amplitude.
   (ii) Whether the output is slew-rate limited.
   (iii) If slew-limited, what is the new maximum allowable input frequency to avoid distortion. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for [B] (a):

**Physical Origin of Slew Rate:**

Every practical op-amp contains an **internal compensation capacitor (C_c)**, typically about 30 pF in the 741 op-amp. This capacitor is deliberately included to ensure **frequency stability** by introducing a dominant pole in the open-loop transfer function.

A **constant bias current source (I_max)** inside the op-amp provides the current to charge or discharge this capacitor. Since:

$$i = C \frac{dv}{dt}$$

The maximum rate of change of voltage across the capacitor is:

$$\frac{dV}{dt}\bigg\vert_{\max} = \frac{I_{\max}}{C_c} = SR$$

When a **large step input** is applied, the internal transistors of the op-amp enter saturation or cutoff, and the only mechanism that can move the output voltage is the limited bias current charging the compensation capacitor. Thus, the output rises (or falls) **linearly** at a rate equal to $I_{\max}/C_c$.

**Why the Output Becomes Triangular:**

When a sine wave of frequency $f$ and peak amplitude $V_p$ is applied, the **required** rate of change at the zero-crossings is $2\pi f V_p$. If this exceeds the op-amp's slew rate $SR$:

$$2\pi f V_p > SR$$

ŌĆ”the op-amp cannot keep up with the input near the zero-crossings (where the required $dV/dt$ is maximum). The output is "**slew-limited**" ŌĆö it can only change at the rate of $SR$, producing a straight-line segment with slope $SR$. The result is a **triangular waveform** at the peaks of the sine wave, with the curved portions only being approximated. This is called **slew-induced distortion**.

#### Model Solution for [B] (b):

**Given:** $SR = 0.5$ V/╬╝s, gain $A_v = 10$, $V_{in,p} = 1$ V, $f = 50$ kHz.

**(i) Output peak amplitude:**

$$V_{o,p} = A_v \times V_{in,p} = 10 \times 1 = 10 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) Check for slew-rate limiting:**

Required slew rate:

$$SR_{\text{required}} = 2\pi f V_{o,p} = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 10$$

$$SR_{\text{required}} = \pi \times 10^6 \approx 3.1416 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/s} = 3.1416 \, \text{V/╬╝s}$$

Compare with op-amp SR:

$$SR_{\text{op-amp}} = 0.5 \, \text{V/╬╝s}$$

Since $3.14 \, \text{V/╬╝s} \gg 0.5 \, \text{V/╬╝s}$, the op-amp is **severely slew-limited**. The output will be triangular, not sinusoidal.

**(iii) Maximum frequency to avoid slew-limiting with $V_{o,p} = 10$ V:**

$$f_{\max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{o,p}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^5}{2\pi} \approx 7957.7 \, \text{Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{\max} \approx 7.96 \, \text{kHz}}$$

So at 50 kHz with 10 V output, the 741 op-amp is completely unable to reproduce the sine wave faithfully. The student should recommend a faster op-amp (e.g., LM318 with 70 V/╬╝s) for this application.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (2ŌĆō3 marks lost):** Failing to convert V/╬╝s to V/s before substituting into $f_P$ or $V_{p,\max}$ formulas. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Confusing SR with Bandwidth (1ŌĆō2 marks lost):** Slew rate is NOT the same as Gain-Bandwidth Product. GBP is a *small-signal* parameter; SR is a *large-signal* parameter.
> 3. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor (2 marks lost):** The relationship is $SR = 2\pi f V_p$, not $SR = f V_p$. Always include $2\pi$ in the formula.
> 4. **Wrong identification of waveform (1 mark lost):** When slew-limited, the output is **triangular**, not "distorted sine" or "clipped". Use the exact term "triangular waveform" in KTU answers.
> 5. **Missing physical reasoning (2 marks lost):** In 7-mark questions, marks are reserved for **explaining the origin** of slew rate (compensation capacitor + bias current), not just the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PBRAT304 Module 2 ŌĆö Slew Rate**

- **Slew Rate (SR)** is the **maximum rate of change of output voltage** with respect to time, measured in **V/╬╝s**.
- **Master Formula:** $SR = 2\pi f V_p = 2\pi f_p V_p$. Memorize this single equation; all three numerical problems follow by simple rearrangement.
- **Origin:** Limited internal bias current $I_{\max}$ charging the compensation capacitor $C_c$. Hence $SR = I_{\max} / C_c$.
- **741 Op-Amp Typical SR:** **0.5 V/╬╝s**.
- **Faster Op-Amps:** TL081 (16 V/╬╝s), LM318 (70 V/╬╝s), AD844 (2000 V/╬╝s).
- **Slew-Limited Output:** When $2\pi f V_p > SR$, the sine output becomes **triangular**, not clipped.
- **Full-Power Bandwidth:** $f_P = SR / (2\pi V_p)$. Maximum frequency for full-amplitude sine output.
- **Rise Time Formula:** $t_r = \Delta V / SR$ (for large-signal step response).
- **Unit Conversion:** $SR$ in V/╬╝s ├Ś $10^6$ = $SR$ in V/s. **Always do this before substitution.**
- **Small-signal vs Large-signal:** GBP is for small signals; SR is for large signals. They are **independent** specifications.
- **Improvement:** Use current-feedback op-amps or JFET-input op-amps for higher SR. Higher SR = higher quiescent current = higher power consumption.
- **Real-World Impact:** Insufficient SR causes audio TIM distortion, video edge smearing, and ADC tracking errors.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance:** Direct 3-mark short questions and 7-mark sub-parts of 14-mark long questions are commonly asked on this topic in **ESE and internal assessments**.
- **Quick Sanity Check:** If $f$ is in kHz and $V_p$ is in volts, then $2\pi f V_p$ is in **kV/s**. To compare with $SR$ in V/╬╝s, divide by $10^3$.

**Final Memory Equation for the Night-Before Exam:**

$$\boxed{SR \, (\text{V/╬╝s}) = 2\pi \times f \, (\text{MHz}) \times V_p \, (\text{V}) \times 10^{-3}}$$

Use this compact form to mentally verify all numerical answers in under 10 seconds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bandwidth ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Analog and Digital Electronics (PBRAT304)**, **Bandwidth (BW)** is formally defined as the measure of the range of frequencies over which a system, circuit, amplifier, or communication channel can operate effectively.
