It is quantified as the difference between the upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$) and the lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$) of a system's frequency response, typically measured at the **-3 dB points** (half-power points). $$BW = f_H - f_L$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2, Bandwidth is studied under the broader framework of amplifier frequency response, transient response, and the Gain-Bandwidth Product. It forms a foundational concept linking time-domain and frequency-domain analyses. ### 1.2 Types of Bandwidth Encountered in Electronics | Type | Context | Typical Unit | |------|---------|--------------| | **Amplifier Bandwidth** | Operational amplifiers, BJT/FET amplifiers | **Hz (Hertz)** | | **Channel Bandwidth** | Communication links, transmission media | **Hz** | | **Digital Bandwidth** | Data transmission capacity (bits/sec) | **bps** | | **Optical Bandwidth** | Fiber optic links, photodetectors | **nm** or **Hz** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Highway System:** > Imagine a multi-lane highway as an electronic system: > - **Bandwidth** = the number of lanes available for traffic (signals) to flow. > - **Low frequency (fL)** = slow, heavy trucks (low-frequency signals). > - **High frequency (fH)** = fast, nimble motorcycles (high-frequency signals). > - If the highway is **narrow** (low bandwidth), only a limited range of vehicles can pass. A **wider highway** (higher bandwidth) allows a broader range of traffic, from slow trucks to fast bikes, to coexist efficiently. > - The **-3 dB point** is akin to a toll booth where roughly 50% of the maximum flow capacity is permitted ŌĆö a benchmark for "acceptable" performance. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Universal Standard:** The **-3 dB point** is the universally accepted cutoff, corresponding to a power drop to **0.5** of maximum (or voltage drop to **0.707** of maximum). - **Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$):** The frequency at which the output power falls to **half** of its midband value. - **Midband Gain ($A_{mid}$):** The flat, constant gain region of an amplifier where response is maximum and undistorted. - **Rise Time ($t_r$):** The time required for the output to transition from **10%** to **90%** of its final value ŌĆö inversely tied to bandwidth. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physics of Bandwidth ŌĆö Why It Exists Every electronic system contains **parasitic capacitances**, **inductances**, and **storage elements** (transistor junction capacitances, coupling capacitors, etc.). These energy-storage components inherently resist rapid changes in voltage or current. As a function of frequency: 1. **At very low frequencies:** Coupling and bypass capacitors exhibit high impedance, attenuating the signal. 2. **At midband frequencies:** All reactive elements are negligible; the system behaves as a pure resistive network, delivering **maximum gain**. 3. **At very high frequencies:** Internal transistor capacitances (Miller effect, $C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$) dominate, shunting the signal and reducing gain. This behavior yields the classic **bandpass response** characteristic of amplifiers. ### 2.2 The -3 dB Cutoff Criterion ŌĆö The "Why" Behind It When a sinusoidal input passes through a system, the output amplitude is governed by the transfer function $|H(j\omega)|$. The **-3 dB point** is defined where: $$20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{|H(j\omega_c)|}{|H(j\omega)_{max}|}\right) = -3 \text{ dB}$$ Solving: $$\frac{|H(j\omega_c)|}{|H(j\omega)_{max}|} = 10^{-3/20} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$$ In terms of power: $$\frac{P_{out}}{P_{max}} = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why -3 dB?** Because it represents the **half-power point** ŌĆö the engineering convention where signal power drops to 50% of maximum. This is widely accepted as the boundary of "useful" operation. ### 2.3 Rise Time and Bandwidth Relationship For a single-pole low-pass system, there exists a fundamental relationship between the **rise time** in the time domain and the **bandwidth** in the frequency domain: $$t_r \approx \frac{0.35}{f_H} = \frac{0.35}{BW}$$ This is derived from the step response of a first-order RC low-pass filter, where $t_r$ (10%ŌĆō90%) corresponds to approximately 2.2 times the time constant $\tau = RC$. ### 2.4 Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) A critically important concept for operational amplifiers and cascaded systems is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product**, which remains **constant** for a given amplifier: $$GBW = A_{CL} \times f_H = \text{constant}$$ Where: - $A_{CL}$ = Closed-loop gain - $f_H$ = Upper -3 dB cutoff frequency > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Trade-off Insight:** Doubling the closed-loop gain halves the usable bandwidth. This is the fundamental trade-off designers must navigate in amplifier selection. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |:---|:---|:---:| | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Total Bandwidth of bandpass system | **Hz** | | $BW \approx f_H$ (when $f_L \ll f_H$) | Approximation for AC-coupled (high-pass-dominated) systems | **Hz** | | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$ | Upper cutoff from equivalent RC network | **Hz** | | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$ | Lower cutoff from coupling/bypass capacitors | **Hz** | | $t_r = \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ | Rise timeŌĆōBandwidth relation | **seconds** | | $GBW = A_{CL} \times f_H$ | Gain-Bandwidth Product | **Hz** | | $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_H)^2}}$ | Single-pole low-pass magnitude response | **dimensionless** | | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Gain in decibels | **dB** | | $A_{dB}(\omega_c) = A_{mid} - 3 \text{ dB}$ | -3 dB criterion for cutoff | **dB** | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Engineering:** A high-fidelity audio amplifier requires **20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz** bandwidth to reproduce the full human hearing range. - **RF Communications:** Cell phones operate in allocated spectral bands (e.g., 700 MHz ŌĆō 2.6 GHz) with strict bandwidth regulations. - **Oscilloscope Design:** A 100 MHz oscilloscope can faithfully display signals up to 100 MHz (with rise time ~3.5 ns). - **Op-Amp Selection:** A general-purpose 741 op-amp has GBW ~1 MHz, while precision op-amps (e.g., OP07) may extend to 10 MHz or more. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Rise Time ŌĆō Bandwidth Relationship **Starting Point:** Consider a first-order low-pass RC filter with transfer function: $$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ and $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, yielding: $$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$ **Step 2:** Apply a unit step input $V_{in}(t) = u(t)$, whose Laplace transform is $\dfrac{1}{s}$. The output in the s-domain is: $$V_{out}(s) = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC} = \frac{1}{s(1 + sRC)}$$ **Step 3:** Perform partial fraction decomposition: $$\frac{1}{s(1 + sRC)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{1 + sRC}$$ Multiplying both sides by $s(1 + sRC)$: $$1 = A(1 + sRC) + Bs$$ **Step 4:** Solve for constants. Set $s = 0$: $1 = A \Rightarrow A = 1$. Set $s = -\dfrac{1}{RC}$: $1 = B \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{RC}\right) \Rightarrow B = -RC$. **Step 5:** Therefore: $$V_{out}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{RC}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 6:** Take inverse Laplace transform: $$V_{out}(t) = 1 - e^{-t/RC} = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$$ Where $\tau = RC$ is the time constant. **Step 7:** The 10%ŌĆō90% rise time is found by: - At $t_1$ where $V_{out}(t_1) = 0.1$: $$0.1 = 1 - e^{-t_1/\tau} \Rightarrow t_1 = -\tau \ln(0.9) \approx 0.1054\tau$$ - At $t_2$ where $V_{out}(t_2) = 0.9$: $$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t_2/\tau} \Rightarrow t_2 = -\tau \ln(0.1) \approx 2.3026\tau$$ **Step 8:** Compute rise time: $$t_r = t_2 - t_1 = \tau[\ln(0.9) - \ln(0.1)] = \tau \ln(9) \approx 2.1972\tau$$ **Step 9:** Since $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$, we have $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_H}$, so: $$t_r = 2.1972 \times \frac{1}{2\pi f_H} = \frac{0.3499}{f_H} \approx \frac{0.35}{f_H}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{t_r \approx \frac{0.35}{BW} = \frac{0.35}{f_H}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Gain-Bandwidth Product for a Non-Inverting Op-Amp **Starting Point:** For a non-inverting op-amp configuration with feedback resistors $R_1$ and $R_f$, the closed-loop gain is: $$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$ **Step 1:** The op-amp's open-loop transfer function (single-pole model) is: $$A_{OL}(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_1}$$ Where $A_0$ is the DC open-loop gain and $\omega_1 = 2\pi f_1$ is the dominant pole frequency. **Step 2:** Closed-loop transfer function: $$A_{CL}(s) = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \cdot \beta}$$ Where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = \dfrac{1}{A_{CL}}$ (for non-inverting). **Step 3:** Substituting $A_{OL} \beta \gg 1$ for typical operation: $$A_{CL}(s) \approx \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s/(\beta A_0 \omega_1)} = A_{CL}(0) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$ **Step 4:** The new -3 dB cutoff frequency is: $$\omega_H = \beta A_0 \omega_1$$ Equivalently: $$f_H = \beta A_0 f_1 = \frac{f_1 \cdot A_0}{A_{CL}}$$ **Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $A_{CL}$: $$A_{CL} \times f_H = A_0 \times f_1 = GBW$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{GBW = A_{CL} \times f_H = \text{constant}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bandwidth Behavior in Common Amplifier Stages | **Amplifier Type** | **Lower Cutoff $f_L$ Source** | **Upper Cutoff $f_H$ Source** | **Typical $f_L$** | **Typical $f_H$** | |:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | Common-Emitter (BJT) | Coupling & Emitter bypass caps | Miller capacitance, $C_{bc}$ | **10 ŌĆō 100 Hz** | **1 ŌĆō 100 MHz** | | Common-Source (FET) | Coupling cap | Gate-Drain $C_{gd}$ | **10 ŌĆō 100 Hz** | **1 ŌĆō 50 MHz** | | Op-Amp (741) | Internal compensation | Dominant pole (30 pF cap) | **DC (0 Hz)** | **~1 MHz** | | Cascode Amplifier | Coupling cap | Reduced Miller effect | **50 Hz** | **100 ŌĆō 500 MHz** | | Emitter Follower | Coupling cap | Base-collector cap | **50 Hz** | **50 ŌĆō 200 MHz** | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A non-inverting op-amp has $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 9 \text{ k}\Omega$. The op-amp's gain-bandwidth product is $GBW = 1 \text{ MHz}$. Determine the closed-loop gain and the -3 dB bandwidth. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate closed-loop gain: $$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{9 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 10$$ **Step 2:** Apply GBW relation: $$f_H = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{10} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 3:** Compute corresponding rise time: $$t_r = \frac{0.35}{f_H} = \frac{0.35}{100 \times 10^3} = 3.5 \text{ ╬╝s}$$ **Conclusion:** The amplifier has a closed-loop gain of **10 (20 dB)** and a usable bandwidth of **100 kHz** with a rise time of **3.5 ╬╝s**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Frequency Response of a Bandpass Amplifier The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the conceptual architecture of bandwidth in a typical amplifier, mapping the input signal path through the system and the resulting frequency-dependent output. ```mermaid flowchart LR input["Input Signal
Vin with variable frequency f"] coupling["Coupling Capacitor
Cc blocks DC"] ampStage["Amplifying Stage
Transistor or Op-Amp"] loadC["Load and Output Capacitor
CL shunts high frequencies"] output["Output Signal
Vout amplitude varies with f"] input -->|"f low blocked"| coupling coupling -->|"f midband passes"| ampStage ampStage -->|"f high attenuated"| loadC loadC --> output subgraph "Frequency Response Characteristics" fL["fL: Lower Cutoff
= 1 / 2ŽĆ Req Cc"] fH["fH: Upper Cutoff
= 1 / 2ŽĆ Req CL"] bw["Bandwidth BW = fH - fL"] fL --- fH fH --- bw end ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bandwidth Analysis Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Start: Define System Transfer Function H(s)"] stepB["Identify Poles and Zeros"] stepC["Calculate fL from Low-Frequency Poles"] stepD["Calculate fH from High-Frequency Poles"] stepE["Compute BW = fH - fL"] stepF["Determine Midband Gain Amid"] stepG["Apply -3 dB Criterion: 20 log H fc Amid = -3"] stepH["Compute Rise Time tr = 0.35 / BW"] stepI["For Op-Amps: Verify GBW = ACL x fH"] stepJ["End: Bandwidth Specification Complete"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepB --> stepD stepC --> stepE stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepE --> stepG stepF --> stepH stepG --> stepI stepH --> stepJ stepI --> stepJ ``` ### 4.3 Visual Reference: Typical Amplifier Bode Plot Description ```mermaid flowchart LR axis["Frequency Axis f in Hz
log scale 10 to 10 million"] gainAxis["Gain Axis in dB
0 dB at top, descending"] region1["Region 1: Low Frequency Roll-off
Slope = +20 dB/decade
f less than fL"] region2["Region 2: Midband Flat Region
Gain = Amid constant
fL to fH"] region3["Region 3: High Frequency Roll-off
Slope = -20 dB/decade
f greater than fH"] point1["fL: Gain = Amid - 3 dB"] point2["fH: Gain = Amid - 3 dB"] region1 -->|"passes through"| point1 point1 -->|"enters"| region2 region2 -->|"exits at"| point2 point2 --> region3 ``` ### 4.4 Cascaded Amplifier Bandwidth Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR stage1["Stage 1
Gain A1, Bandwidth BW1"] stage2["Stage 2
Gain A2, Bandwidth BW2"] stage3["Stage N
Gain AN, Bandwidth BWN"] totalGain["Total Gain: A_total = A1 x A2 x ... x AN"] totalBW["Total Bandwidth: BW_total"] stage1 --> stage2 stage2 --> stage3 stage3 --> totalGain totalGain --> totalBW totalBW["BW_total approx 1 over sqrt 1 over BW1 squared + 1 over BW2 squared + ... + 1 over BWN squared"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **For cascaded stages**, the total bandwidth is **not** simply the sum or product of individual bandwidths. It follows the **root-sum-square** approximation for non-identical stages, which is critical when designing multi-stage amplifiers. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question 1, 3 Marks]** > **Q1.** Define the term **Bandwidth** of an amplifier. What are the **-3 dB points** and why are they used as the standard cutoff reference? **Model Answer:** Bandwidth of an amplifier is defined as the range of frequencies over which the amplifier delivers useful gain, typically measured between the two **-3 dB points** (lower cutoff frequency $f_L$ and upper cutoff frequency $f_H$): $$BW = f_H - f_L$$ The **-3 dB points** correspond to frequencies at which the output power drops to **half** (50%) of its maximum midband value, or equivalently, the output voltage drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ of its midband value. The -3 dB criterion is adopted universally because it represents the **half-power point**, a well-defined engineering boundary for "useful" signal transmission. Beyond $f_H$, the gain rolls off at -20 dB/decade for a single-pole system. --- > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question 2, 3 Marks]** > **Q2.** State and explain the relationship between **rise time** and **bandwidth** of an amplifier. **Model Answer:** For a first-order low-pass system, the rise time $t_r$ (measured from 10% to 90% of the final value) is related to the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ (and hence the bandwidth, when $f_L \approx 0$) by: $$t_r \approx \frac{0.35}{BW} = \frac{0.35}{f_H}$$ This relation arises from the step response of an RC low-pass filter, where the rise time equals approximately 2.2 times the time constant $\tau = RC$. **Engineering significance:** A larger bandwidth corresponds to a faster rise time, enabling the amplifier to faithfully reproduce rapid signal transitions. For example, a 100 MHz bandwidth amplifier exhibits $t_r \approx 3.5$ ns. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, 14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice]** > **Q3 (a).** Derive the relationship between the **rise time** and **bandwidth** of an amplifier. Explain the underlying time-domain and frequency-domain interpretations. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Consider a first-order low-pass RC filter with transfer function: $$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 2:** For $s = j\omega$ and $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$: $$H(jf) = \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$ **Step 3:** Apply unit step input. Output in s-domain: $$V_{out}(s) = \frac{1}{s(1 + sRC)}$$ **Step 4:** Partial fractions yield: $$V_{out}(t) = 1 - e^{-t/RC} = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$$ **Step 5:** Rise time calculation: $$t_r = \tau \cdot \ln(9) = 2.2\tau$$ **Step 6:** Substituting $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_H}$: $$t_r = \frac{0.35}{f_H} = \frac{0.35}{BW}$$ **Frequency-domain interpretation:** $f_H$ defines the highest frequency the system can pass without significant attenuation (above -3 dB). **Time-domain interpretation:** $t_r$ defines how quickly the system can respond to a sudden input change. The inverse relationship confirms that **fast rise time demands wide bandwidth**. --- > **Q3 (b).** An amplifier has a midband gain of **40 dB** and upper cutoff frequency $f_H = 50 \text{ kHz}$. The lower cutoff $f_L = 100 \text{ Hz}$. > (i) Calculate the **bandwidth**. > (ii) Calculate the **gain** at $f = f_H$ in dB and as a voltage ratio. > (iii) If the gain is rolled off at **-20 dB/decade** beyond $f_H$, find the gain (in dB) at $f = 500 \text{ kHz}$. > **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Bandwidth:** $$BW = f_H - f_L = 50{,}000 - 100 = 49{,}900 \text{ Hz} \approx 49.9 \text{ kHz}$$ **(ii) Gain at $f = f_H$:** By definition of -3 dB point: $$A(f_H) = 40 \text{ dB} - 3 \text{ dB} = 37 \text{ dB}$$ Voltage ratio: $$A_{V} = 10^{37/20} = 10^{1.85} \approx 70.79$$ **(iii) Gain at $f = 500$ kHz:** Frequency ratio: $\dfrac{f}{f_H} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{50{,}000} = 10$ Number of decades above $f_H$: $\log_{10}(10) = 1$ decade At -20 dB/decade roll-off: $$\text{Additional attenuation} = 1 \times 20 = 20 \text{ dB}$$ $$A(500 \text{ kHz}) = 37 \text{ dB} - 20 \text{ dB} = 17 \text{ dB}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, 14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice]** > **Q4 (a).** What is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**? Explain its significance with reference to operational amplifiers. Derive the expression $GBW = A_{CL} \times f_H$ for a non-inverting op-amp configuration. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** of an operational amplifier is the product of its closed-loop gain and the corresponding -3 dB bandwidth, which remains a **constant** for a given op-amp. **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Open-loop transfer function (single-pole model): $$A_{OL}(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_1}$$ **Step 2:** For non-inverting configuration, closed-loop gain: $$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{1}{\beta}$$ **Step 3:** Closed-loop transfer function: $$A_{CL}(s) = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$ **Step 4:** Solving for the new -3 dB frequency $\omega_H$: $$\omega_H = \beta \cdot A_0 \cdot \omega_1 = \frac{A_0 \omega_1}{A_{CL}}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$A_{CL} \times f_H = A_0 \times f_1 = GBW = \text{constant}$$ **Significance:** GBW is a critical specification used by designers to predict the maximum bandwidth achievable at a desired gain. For example, a 741 op-amp with $GBW = 1 \text{ MHz}$ configured for $A_{CL} = 100$ will have $f_H = 10 \text{ kHz}$. --- > **Q4 (b).** A non-inverting op-amp circuit uses $R_1 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 18 \text{ k}\Omega$. The op-amp has a gain-bandwidth product of $GBW = 2 \text{ MHz}$. > (i) Determine the **closed-loop gain** $A_{CL}$ and the corresponding **bandwidth** $f_H$. > (ii) Compute the **rise time** of the amplifier. > (iii) If the gain is increased to 50 by changing $R_f$, what will be the new bandwidth? > **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Closed-loop gain and bandwidth:** $$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{18 \text{ k}\Omega}{2 \text{ k}\Omega} = 10$$ $$f_H = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{10} = 200 \text{ kHz}$$ **(ii) Rise time:** $$t_r = \frac{0.35}{f_H} = \frac{0.35}{200 \times 10^3} = 1.75 \text{ ╬╝s}$$ **(iii) New bandwidth for $A_{CL} = 50$:** $$f_H^{new} = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}^{new}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{50} = 40 \text{ kHz}$$ **Insight:** Increasing the gain by a factor of 5 reduces the bandwidth by the same factor, demonstrating the fundamental gain-bandwidth trade-off in op-amp design. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing bandwidth with -3 dB point:** Bandwidth is the *range* ($f_H - f_L$), not a single frequency. > 2. **Unit errors in GBW calculations:** Forgetting to convert MHz to Hz before dividing by gain. > 3. **Applying $t_r = 0.35/BW$ only when $f_L \approx 0$:** For bandpass systems where $f_L$ is significant, students should consider the more general impulse-response analysis. > 4. **Assuming GBW applies to open-loop configurations:** GBW is strictly a closed-loop specification for op-amps. > 5. **Mixing up dB conversions:** $20 \log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in})$ for voltage gain, NOT $10 \log_{10}$. > 6. **Neglecting the -3 dB convention:** Writing 0.5 dB or -6 dB as cutoff; always -3 dB. > 7. **Skipping derivation steps:** KTU examiners award partial credit for each logical step; skipping partial fractions or inverse Laplace transforms loses significant marks. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Bandwidth (BW)** is the usable frequency range, $BW = f_H - f_L$, measured at **-3 dB** points (half-power). - The **-3 dB criterion** corresponds to voltage ratio $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ and power ratio $1/2 = 0.5$. - **Rise time** and bandwidth are inversely related: $t_r \approx 0.35 / BW$. - **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is constant for a given op-amp: $GBW = A_{CL} \times f_H$. - Higher closed-loop gain **reduces** the usable bandwidth (fundamental trade-off). - **Cascaded amplifier bandwidth** uses root-sum-square approximation, not simple multiplication. - **Single-pole roll-off** = -20 dB/decade (-6 dB/octave) beyond $f_H$. - **Bode plot conventions:** Use log scale for frequency, dB scale for gain magnitude. - Always **state units** explicitly: Hz, kHz, MHz for frequency; dB for gain; seconds for rise time. - The **half-power point** is the universal standard for defining cutoff frequencies in all KTU 2024 electronics problems. ---

topics.title: CMRR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CMRR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is a fundamental performance metric of a differential amplifier (especially an operational amplifier) that quantifies the device's ability to **reject signals common to both input terminals** while **amplifying the difference between them**. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the **differential-mode gain ($A_d$)** to the **common-mode gain ($A_c$)**.

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right|
$$

In logarithmic (decibel) form, which is the standard KTU representation:

$$
\text{CMRR (in dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right| \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CMRR is a direct indicator of how well an op-amp suppresses noise, interference, and unwanted signals that appear simultaneously and in-phase on **both** the inverting (VŌü╗) and non-inverting (VŌü║) input terminals. A higher CMRR value (in dB) indicates superior noise immunity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are sitting in a noisy cafeteria trying to listen to your friend sitting right across the table. 

- **Your friend's voice** is the *differential signal* ŌĆö it reaches your **left ear slightly earlier and louder** than your right ear. Your brain uses this tiny time/intensity difference to "amplify" your friend's voice.
- **The background chatter of the entire cafeteria** is the *common-mode signal* ŌĆö it reaches **both ears equally** (same intensity, same time, same phase). Your brain's "internal circuit" ignores this.

**Your ears + brain act like a differential amplifier with a very high CMRR.** The common-mode chatter is rejected, and only the differential (your friend's) voice is processed.

In an op-amp:
- **Differential signal ($V_{id}$)** = ($V_1 - V_2$) ŌĆö the useful signal we want to amplify.
- **Common-mode signal ($V_{ic}$)** = $\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ ŌĆö unwanted noise/interference.
- **CMRR** measures how well the op-amp amplifies $V_{id}$ while ignoring $V_{ic}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Summary:**
> - High CMRR Ōēł Your brain in the cafeteria (excellent at hearing your friend, ignoring the crowd).
> - Low CMRR Ōēł A cheap hearing aid that amplifies everything equally (friend's voice + background noise together).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Differential-Mode Gain ($A_d$):** Dimensionless ratio; typically ranges from **$10^2$ to $10^6$** in real op-amps.
- **Common-Mode Gain ($A_c$):** Dimensionless ratio; ideally **0**, typically very small in well-designed amplifiers.
- **Ideal Op-Amp CMRR:** $\infty$ (infinite) or **$\infty$ dB**.
- **Standard Typical CMRR:** **70 dB to 120 dB** for commercial op-amps (e.g., ┬ĄA741 Ōēł 90 dB, OP-27 Ōēł 120 dB).
- **Common-Mode Input Range:** The maximum common-mode voltage that can be applied without saturating the input stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Keyword (KTU):** When stating CMRR, always quote it in **decibels (dB)** unless a numerical ratio is specifically asked. The standard unit is **dB**.

---

## 1.4 Why is CMRR Critical in Engineering?

In real-world applications, sensors (thermocouples, strain gauges, ECG electrodes, bridge circuits) produce **small differential signals** in the presence of **large common-mode noise** (50 Hz mains hum, electromagnetic interference, ground loops). An op-amp with poor CMRR would amplify the noise as much as the signal, rendering the output useless. Therefore, **CMRR is a non-negotiable specification** in instrumentation, biomedical, and industrial measurement systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of CMRR

The output voltage of a real (non-ideal) differential amplifier is **not purely a function of the difference** between its two inputs. It contains two distinct contributions:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_c \cdot V_{ic}
$$

Where:
- $A_d$ = Differential-mode gain (gain applied to the **difference** of inputs)
- $A_c$ = Common-mode gain (gain applied to the **average** of inputs)
- $V_{id}$ = Differential input voltage = $V_1 - V_2$
- $V_{ic}$ = Common-mode input voltage = $\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose Inputs:** Any pair of input voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ can be uniquely expressed as a sum of a differential component and a common-mode component.
>
> **Logic Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Gains:** The op-amp amplifies the differential part by $A_d$ and *unfortunately* also amplifies the common-mode part by $A_c$.
>
> **Logic Step 3 ŌĆö Rejection:** A *good* amplifier must have $A_d \gg A_c$, so that the common-mode contribution to $V_{out}$ is negligible.
>
> **Logic Step 4 ŌĆö Quantification:** The ratio $A_d / A_c$ captures this ability in a single number ŌĆö the CMRR.

---

## 2.2 Input-Output Relationship of a Real Op-Amp

A general-purpose op-amp (like ┬ĄA741) has the following transfer characteristic:

$$
V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left( \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right)
$$

Substituting $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$ and $V_{ic} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_c \cdot V_{ic}
$$

For the **ideal op-amp**:
- $A_d \to \infty$
- $A_c \to 0$ Ō¤╣ $\text{CMRR}_{ideal} \to \infty$

For the **practical op-amp**:
- $A_d$ is large but finite (e.g., $2 \times 10^5$ for ┬ĄA741)
- $A_c$ is small but non-zero (e.g., $6.3$ for ┬ĄA741)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Standard Form** | **Unit** | **Typical Range (┬ĄA741)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Input Voltage | $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$ | $V_1 - V_2$ | Volts (V) | mV range |
| Common-Mode Input Voltage | $V_{ic} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | $\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | Volts (V) | Up to $\pm V_{CC}$ |
| Op-Amp Output Equation | $V_{out} = A_d V_{id} + A_c V_{ic}$ | ŌĆö | Volts (V) | ŌĆö |
| Differential-Mode Gain | $A_d$ | Ratio | Dimensionless | $2 \times 10^5$ |
| Common-Mode Gain | $A_c$ | Ratio | Dimensionless | $6.3$ |
| **CMRR (Absolute)** | $\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$ | $\vert A_d / A_c \vert$ | Dimensionless | $\approx 3.17 \times 10^4$ |
| **CMRR (in dB)** | $20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$ | $20 \log(\vert A_d / A_c \vert)$ | **dB** | $\approx 90$ dB |
| Common-Mode Output Voltage | $V_{oc} = A_c \cdot V_{ic}$ | ŌĆö | Volts (V) | mV range |
| Ideal CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = \infty$ | ŌĆö | dB | $\infty$ dB |
| Effect on $V_{out}$ due to $V_{ic}$ | $V_{out(c)} = \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR}} \cdot V_{ic}$ | $\frac{A_d V_{ic}}{\text{CMRR}}$ | Volts (V) | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` (LaTeX) to preserve markdown table integrity. **Do not use the pipe `|` symbol inside table cells.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CMRR is the cornerstone of **Instrumentation Amplifiers (INA)**, which are essentially three-op-amp configurations engineered to achieve CMRR values of **100 dB or higher**. Applications include:

- **Biomedical Signal Acquisition:** ECG, EEG, EMG ŌĆö extracting micro-volt-level heart/brain signals buried in 50/60 Hz mains interference.
- **Bridge Amplifiers:** Weighing scales, strain gauge bridges ŌĆö where the differential signal is in millivolts and the common-mode voltage is in volts.
- **Audio Systems:** Professional microphone pre-amplifiers use balanced lines and high-CMRR op-amps to reject hum.
- **Industrial Sensors:** Thermocouple amplifiers in noisy factory environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** A CMRR of **80 dB** means the op-amp will reject common-mode noise by a factor of **10,000:1** relative to the differential signal. A CMRR of **100 dB** is a factor of **100,000:1**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From the Op-Amp Output Equation to the CMRR Definition

### Starting Equation (KTU Standard Model)

A real op-amp treats its two input voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ in a non-ideal manner. The general transfer function is:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \cdot \left( \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right)
$$

### Step 1: Identify the Differential Component

By definition, the **differential input voltage** is:

$$
V_{id} = V_1 - V_2
$$

Substituting this:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_c \cdot \left( \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right)
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Common-Mode Component

By definition, the **common-mode input voltage** is:

$$
V_{ic} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}
$$

Substituting this:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_c \cdot V_{ic}
$$

### Step 3: Re-express the Output in Terms of CMRR

Rearranging the equation to factor out $A_d$:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_d \cdot \left( \frac{A_c}{A_d} \right) \cdot V_{ic}
$$

### Step 4: Introduce the CMRR

By definition:

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_c} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{A_c}{A_d} = \frac{1}{\text{CMRR}}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_d \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\text{CMRR}} \right) \cdot V_{ic}
$$

### Step 5: Final Form (KTU Board-Exam Standard)

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + \frac{A_d \cdot V_{ic}}{\text{CMRR}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU equation** that examiners expect for any problem linking CMRR to output voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conversion of CMRR from Linear Ratio to Decibels

### Step 1: Start with the Linear Definition

$$
\text{CMRR}_{linear} = \frac{A_d}{A_c}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Decibel Conversion Formula

For any voltage-like ratio, the decibel form uses a factor of **20**:

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)
$$

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

Suppose $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and $A_c = 6.3$ (typical ┬ĄA741 values).

First, compute the linear ratio:

$$
\frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{6.3} = 3.1746 \times 10^4
$$

Then, convert to decibels:

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \cdot \log_{10} (3.1746 \times 10^4)
$$

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \cdot \left[ \log_{10}(3.1746) + \log_{10}(10^4) \right]
$$

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \cdot [0.5017 + 4]
$$

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \cdot 4.5017
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR (dB)} \approx 90.03 \text{ dB}}
$$

This matches the ┬ĄA741 datasheet specification of **90 dB** Ō£ō.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Finite CMRR on a Non-Inverting Amplifier

### Circuit Setup

A non-inverting amplifier with feedback resistors $R_f$ and $R_1$ has an ideal closed-loop gain:

$$
A_{CL,ideal} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

### Step 1: Express the Inputs in Terms of $V_{in}$ and $V_{ic}$

For a non-inverting amplifier with finite CMRR, the inputs can be modeled as:

$$
V_1 = V_{in}, \quad V_2 = V_{in} - V_{id,closed}
$$

where $V_{id,closed}$ is the small differential voltage required to produce the output. However, the key is to recognize that any common-mode voltage present at the inputs is amplified by the closed-loop gain and produces a **common-mode error** at the output.

### Step 2: Output Voltage with Finite CMRR

$$
V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in} + \frac{A_{CL}}{\text{CMRR}} \cdot V_{ic}
$$

### Step 3: The Common-Mode Error Voltage

The unwanted output component due to common-mode voltage is:

$$
V_{error} = \frac{A_{CL} \cdot V_{ic}}{\text{CMRR}}
$$

### Step 4: Practical Interpretation

If $A_{CL} = 10$, $\text{CMRR} = 80 \text{ dB} = 10{,}000$, and $V_{ic} = 1$ V, then:

$$
V_{error} = \frac{10 \times 1}{10{,}000} = 1 \text{ mV}
$$

Even a 1 V common-mode input (such as 50 Hz mains interference) produces only 1 mV of error at the output ŌĆö a 10,000:1 rejection.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Op-Amp Specifications

| **Op-Amp Model** | **$A_d$ (Typical)** | **$A_c$ (Typical)** | **CMRR (dB)** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ┬ĄA741 | $2 \times 10^5$ | $6.3$ | **90 dB** | General purpose |
| OP-27 | $1.8 \times 10^6$ | $1.8$ | **120 dB** | Precision instrumentation |
| LM358 | $1 \times 10^5$ | $20$ | **74 dB** | Low-cost, single supply |
| AD620 (INA) | $10^7$ | ŌĆö | **100 dB+** | ECG / Strain gauge |
| Ideal Op-Amp | $\infty$ | $0$ | **$\infty$ dB** | Theoretical benchmark |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Op-Amp with Differential and Common-Mode Inputs

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how an op-amp processes the differential and common-mode components of its inputs:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input V1"] --> C["Summing Node"]
    B["Input V2"] --> C
    C --> D["Vid = V1 minus V2"]
    C --> E["Vic = V1 plus V2 over 2"]
    D --> F["Multiply by Ad Differential Gain"]
    E --> G["Multiply by Ac Common Mode Gain"]
    F --> H["Output Summing Junction"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Vout = Ad Vid plus Ac Vic"]
    J["CMRR = Ad over Ac"] -.-> G
    K["CMRR in dB = 20 log Ad over Ac"] -.-> J
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Real-World Signal Extraction using High CMRR

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Differential Sensor Output"] --> B["Two Wires carrying Signal plus Common Mode Noise"]
    B --> C["Op-Amp with High CMRR"]
    D["Common Mode Noise from Mains or EMI"] -.-> B
    C --> E["Amplified Differential Signal Clean"]
    F["CMRR Metric of Op-Amp"] -.-> C
    G["Higher CMRR means Better Noise Rejection"] -.-> C
    H["Typical CMRR Range 70 dB to 120 dB"] -.-> F
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Ideal vs. Practical Op-Amp

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Op-Amp Inputs V1 and V2"] --> B{"Is Op-Amp Ideal?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Ac equals 0, CMRR equals Infinity"]
    B -- No --> D["Ac is small but nonzero"]
    D --> E["CMRR equals Ad over Ac in dB"]
    E --> F["Vout equals Ad Vid plus Ac Vic"]
    C --> G["Vout equals Ad Vid only"]
    F --> H["Common Mode Noise appears at Output"]
    G --> I["Perfect Common Mode Rejection"]
```

---

## 4.4 Visual Block Diagram: Instrumentation Amplifier (3-Op-Amp Topology)

> This is the standard topology used to achieve **>100 dB CMRR** in real-world applications:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Input V1"] --> A2["Buffer Op-Amp A1"]
    A4["Input V2"] --> A3["Buffer Op-Amp A2"]
    A2 --> A5["Gain Resistor Rg"]
    A3 --> A5
    A5 --> A6["Difference Amplifier Stage A3 and A4"]
    A2 --> A6
    A3 --> A6
    A6 --> A7["High CMRR Single Ended Output"]
    B1["CMRR of IA greater than 100 dB"] -.-> A7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of an operational amplifier. Mention its ideal value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to the common-mode gain ($A_c$) of a differential amplifier. It measures the ability of an op-amp to reject signals that are common to both input terminals.

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right|
$$

In decibels:

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right| \text{ dB}
$$

> **Ideal Value:** For an ideal op-amp, $A_c = 0$, so $\text{CMRR}_{ideal} = \infty$ (infinite dB). **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for ideal value)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between differential-mode gain and common-mode gain of an op-amp.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Differential-Mode Gain ($A_d$)** | **Common-Mode Gain ($A_c$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Gain for the *difference* of inputs | Gain for the *average* of inputs |
| Input Quantity | $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$ | $V_{ic} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ |
| Magnitude | Very large ($10^2$ to $10^6$) | Ideally zero; practically very small |
| Sign Convention | Amplifies the *difference* (desired) | Amplifies the *common* part (undesired) |
| KTU Goal | Maximize | Minimize |

> CMRR is the ratio $A_d / A_c$. **(3 marks for tabulated differentiation with correct terminology)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a practical op-amp in terms of differential-mode gain ($A_d$), common-mode gain ($A_c$), differential input ($V_{id}$), and common-mode input ($V_{ic}$). Define CMRR. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an op-amp, $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and $A_c = 6.3$. Calculate:
   (i) CMRR in linear ratio
   (ii) CMRR in dB
   (iii) The common-mode output voltage $V_{oc}$ when $V_{ic} = 5$ V. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

The op-amp has two input terminals with voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$. Any input pair can be decomposed as:

**Step 1:** Differential input voltage:

$$
V_{id} = V_1 - V_2
$$

**Step 2:** Common-mode input voltage:

$$
V_{ic} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** The output of a practical (non-ideal) op-amp contains both terms:

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_c \cdot V_{ic}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the definitions:

$$
V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left( \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Definition of CMRR:

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right|
$$

> **Mark Distribution (a):** 2 marks for differential/common-mode definitions, 3 marks for output equation derivation, 2 marks for CMRR definition.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$, $A_c = 6.3$, $V_{ic} = 5$ V.

**(i) CMRR in linear ratio:**

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{6.3} = 3.1746 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR} \approx 3.17 \times 10^4} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}
$$

**(ii) CMRR in dB:**

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(3.1746 \times 10^4)
$$

$$
= 20 \times [\log_{10}(3.1746) + 4] = 20 \times [0.5017 + 4]
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR} \approx 90.03 \text{ dB}}
$$

**(iii) Common-mode output voltage:**

$$
V_{oc} = A_c \cdot V_{ic} = 6.3 \times 5 = 31.5 \text{ V}
$$

> **Note:** This exceeds typical supply voltages, indicating saturation in practice.

> **Mark Distribution (b):** 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) [including logarithmic step-by-step], 2 marks for (iii).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2022 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the significance of CMRR in the context of an instrumentation amplifier. Why is a high CMRR essential in biomedical signal acquisition? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A non-inverting amplifier has a closed-loop gain of 50 and operates with a common-mode input of 0.5 V. If the op-amp has CMRR = 86 dB, determine the common-mode error voltage at the output. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) are typically in the **microvolt-to-millivolt** range.

**Step 2:** These signals are acquired in environments saturated with **50/60 Hz mains interference** and **electromagnetic noise**, which appears as a **common-mode voltage** on both input leads.

**Step 3:** A high CMRR ensures that this large common-mode interference is rejected while the small differential biomedical signal is amplified.

**Step 4:** In an **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)**, the three-op-amp topology achieves **CMRR > 100 dB**, equivalent to a rejection ratio of **100,000:1**.

**Step 5:** Without high CMRR, the noise would completely swamp the signal, making diagnosis impossible.

> **Mark Distribution (a):** 1 mark for biomedical signal range, 2 marks for noise sources, 2 marks for IA topology, 2 marks for practical numerical implication.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $A_{CL} = 50$, $V_{ic} = 0.5$ V, $\text{CMRR} = 86$ dB.

**Step 1:** Convert CMRR from dB to linear ratio:

$$
86 = 20 \log_{10}(\text{CMRR}_{linear})
$$

$$
\log_{10}(\text{CMRR}_{linear}) = \frac{86}{20} = 4.3
$$

$$
\text{CMRR}_{linear} = 10^{4.3} = 19{,}952.6
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the common-mode error formula:

$$
V_{error} = \frac{A_{CL} \cdot V_{ic}}{\text{CMRR}_{linear}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$
V_{error} = \frac{50 \times 0.5}{19{,}952.6} = \frac{25}{19{,}952.6}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{error} \approx 1.253 \text{ mV}}
$$

> **Mark Distribution (b):** 2 marks for dB-to-linear conversion, 2 marks for formula identification, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with units.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 20 (not 10):** Many students mistakenly use $10 \log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$. **Always use 20** for voltage ratios in CMRR calculations.
> 2. **Unit Omission:** Writing "CMRR = 90" instead of "CMRR = 90 dB" costs 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Sign Errors in $V_{ic}$:** $V_{ic} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$, **not** $(V_1 - V_2)/2$.
> 4. **Inverting $A_d$ and $A_c$:** CMRR is $A_d / A_c$, **not** $A_c / A_d$.
> 5. **Not stating Ideal CMRR = $\infty$** in definition questions.
> 6. **Conflating PSRR with CMRR:** Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) is a different parameter; do not interchange.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CMRR** = ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain; **always quoted in dB** in KTU exams.
- **Ideal Op-Amp CMRR** = **$\infty$ dB** (since $A_c = 0$).
- **Conversion Formula:** $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$.
- **Typical Range:** **70 dB (LM358) to 120 dB (OP-27, precision op-amps).**
- **┬ĄA741 Reference Value:** $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$, $A_c = 6.3$, **CMRR = 90 dB**.
- **Output Equation (Board-Exam Standard):**

$$
V_{out} = A_d V_{id} + A_c V_{ic} = A_d \left[ V_{id} + \frac{V_{ic}}{\text{CMRR}} \right]
$$

- **Common-Mode Error at Output:** $V_{error} = \frac{A_{CL} \cdot V_{ic}}{\text{CMRR}_{linear}}$.
- **Instrumentation Amplifiers** achieve >100 dB CMRR using the 3-op-amp topology.
- **Always use the factor 20** when converting voltage ratios to dB.
- **Higher CMRR = Better noise rejection** = Cleaner differential signal extraction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Gain

In the context of Analog Electronics and Amplifier Design, **Gain** is defined as the ratio of the magnitude (or amplitude) of the output signal to the magnitude (or amplitude) of the input signal at the **steady-state** operating condition. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the amplification capability of an electronic amplifier or a two-port network.

Mathematically, for a linear two-port network under sinusoidal steady-state excitation, gain $G$ is universally expressed as:

$$G = \frac{\text{Output Quantity (Magnitude)}}{\text{Input Quantity (Magnitude)}}$$

Depending on the type of input and output quantities being compared, gain is broadly classified into three fundamental categories:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Three Primary Categories of Gain**
> 1. **Voltage Gain ($A_v$)** ŌĆö Ratio of output voltage to input voltage
> 2. **Current Gain ($A_i$)** ŌĆö Ratio of output current to input current
> 3. **Power Gain ($A_p$)** ŌĆö Ratio of output power to input power

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are speaking into a microphone at a small classroom meeting, and your voice is being amplified through a loudspeaker so that people in a large auditorium can hear you clearly. The loudspeaker system is essentially performing the same function as an electronic amplifier.

- Your **soft voice** is the *input signal*.
- The **loud sound** emanating from the speaker is the *output signal*.
- The factor by which the system has "boosted" your voice is, in essence, the **Gain**.

If the speaker makes your voice **10 times louder**, the system has a power gain of **10**. In another scenario, if you have a small water pipe (input) feeding into a wider pipe (output), the volumetric flow rate increases ŌĆö analogous to current gain. If a small input force is used to lift a heavy weight via a hydraulic jack, that is analogous to power gain.

## 1.3 The Decibel (dB) Scale ŌĆö A Standardized Metric

Since gain values can span many orders of magnitude, engineers use the **decibel (dB)** scale, which is a logarithmic representation. The reference standards are:

> [!NOTE] **Standard Decibel Reference Values**
> - **Voltage Gain (dB):** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$
> - **Current Gain (dB):** $A_i(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{out}}{I_{in}}\right)$
> - **Power Gain (dB):** $A_p(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why the factor of 20 for voltage/current and 10 for power?**
> Because $P = \frac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R$, squaring a ratio introduces a factor of 2, and $20 \log_{10}(x) = 10 \log_{10}(x^2)$.

## 1.4 Gain Sign Convention

In AC analysis, the gain is a **complex phasor** $\mathbf{A} = A_m e^{j\phi}$, where $A_m$ is the magnitude and $\phi$ is the phase shift between output and input.

- A **negative sign** in the gain expression (e.g., $A_v = -100$) indicates a **180┬░ phase inversion** between input and output.
- A **positive sign** indicates the output is in **phase** with the input.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the negative sign (phase inversion) in the Common-Emitter (CE) and Common-Source (CS) amplifier gain derivations. This leads to direct mark deduction in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Gain Concept

The concept of gain can be broken down into the following structured logic steps:

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the two-port network:** An amplifier is modelled as a two-port network with an input port (where the source signal is applied) and an output port (where the amplified signal is delivered).
2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the input excitation:** A small sinusoidal AC signal $v_{in}$ or $i_{in}$ is applied at the input port, superimposed on a DC operating point (Q-point).
3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Small-signal linearization:** The transistor is replaced by its small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ or T-model. The DC supply is shorted to ground for AC analysis.
4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate the output response:** Using Kirchhoff's laws, mesh analysis, or nodal analysis, the output voltage $v_{out}$ or output current $i_{out}$ is computed.
5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Form the ratio:** The gain is formed as the ratio of output to input phasor quantities.
6. **Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the decibel conversion:** For Bode plots and frequency response, the gain is converted to dB using the standard logarithmic formulae.

## 2.2 Gain in BJT Amplifier Configurations

The three fundamental BJT amplifier configurations each have distinct gain characteristics:

### 2.2.1 Common-Emitter (CE) Configuration
The CE amplifier provides **moderate voltage gain**, **moderate current gain**, **high power gain**, and a **180┬░ phase inversion** between input and output. It is the most widely used configuration.

**Small-signal voltage gain (with emitter fully bypassed):**
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Including un-bypassed emitter resistor $R_E$:**
$$A_v = \frac{-g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx \frac{-(R_C \parallel R_L)}{R_E}$$

### 2.2.2 Common-Base (CB) Configuration
The CB amplifier provides **high voltage gain**, **low current gain** (less than unity), and **moderate power gain**. It is non-inverting (no phase shift) and has excellent high-frequency response.

$$A_v = g_m (R_C \parallel R_L), \quad A_i \approx \alpha \approx 1$$

### 2.2.3 Common-Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower Configuration
The CC amplifier provides **unity voltage gain** ($A_v \approx 1$), **high current gain** ($A_i \approx \beta+1$), and **moderate power gain**. Output is in phase with the input.

$$A_v = \frac{g_m (R_E \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m (R_E \parallel R_L)} \approx 1$$

## 2.3 Gain in FET Amplifier Configurations

### 2.3.1 Common-Source (CS) Configuration
The CS amplifier is the FET counterpart of the CE amplifier. It provides **high voltage gain** with a **180┬░ phase inversion**.

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$$

### 2.3.2 Common-Drain (CD) / Source Follower Configuration
The CD amplifier provides **unity voltage gain** ($A_v \approx 1$) and is used as a buffer stage.

$$A_v = \frac{g_m (R_S \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m (R_S \parallel R_L)} \approx 1$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] The following table is the single most important reference for solving any KTU exam problem on Gain. Memorize these expressions and their conditions.

| **Configuration** | **Voltage Gain $A_v$** | **Current Gain $A_i$** | **Phase** | **Key Assumption** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| CE (BJT, bypassed $R_E$) | $-g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | $\beta$ | 180┬░ | $r_\pi \ll \beta R_E$ |
| CE (BJT, unbypassed $R_E$) | $\dfrac{-(R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_e + R_E}$ | $\dfrac{\beta R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E}$ | 180┬░ | Approximation valid for $R_E \gg r_e$ |
| CB (BJT) | $g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | $\alpha \approx 1$ | 0┬░ | Low input impedance |
| CC / Emitter Follower | $\dfrac{g_m (R_E \parallel R_L)}{1+g_m (R_E \parallel R_L)}$ | $\beta + 1$ | 0┬░ | Unity voltage gain |
| CS (FET) | $-g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$ | $-g_m R_D \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+g_m R_S}$ | 180┬░ | Infinite input impedance |
| CD / Source Follower | $\dfrac{g_m (R_S \parallel R_L)}{1+g_m (R_S \parallel R_L)}$ | $\approx 1$ | 0┬░ | Buffer stage |
| General Power Gain | $A_p = A_v \cdot A_i$ | $A_p = \vert A_v \vert \cdot \vert A_i \vert$ | ŌĆö | Always positive |
| Decibel (Power) | $A_p(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(A_p)$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | For power ratios |
| Decibel (Voltage) | $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_v \vert)$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | For voltage/current ratios |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Amplifiers:** Multi-stage CE amplifiers cascade to achieve high voltage gain (60ŌĆō100 dB) for speaker systems.
- **RF Front-Ends:** CB and CS configurations are used at high frequencies due to their superior bandwidth.
- **Operational Amplifiers:** The open-loop gain of an op-amp (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$) is a single high-gain CE differential stage.
- **Communication Systems:** Gain in dB is essential for link budget calculations in cellular and satellite systems.
- **Instrumentation:** Buffer amplifiers (CC/CD) with unity voltage gain but high current gain drive low-impedance loads without signal loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Gain of Common-Emitter (CE) BJT Amplifier with Fully Bypassed Emitter Resistor

### Circuit Description
Consider a CE amplifier with emitter resistor $R_E$ fully bypassed by a large capacitor $C_E$ (so $C_E$ acts as a short circuit for AC signals). The small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ model is used.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the small-signal parameters from the bias point:
- Transconductance: $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$
- Base-emitter dynamic resistance: $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$
- Thermal voltage: $V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at room temperature

**Step 2:** Apply input voltage $v_{in}$ across the base-emitter junction. The small-signal base current is:
$$i_b = \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi}$$

**Step 3:** The collector current (output current source in hybrid-$\pi$ model) is:
$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_{be} = g_m \cdot v_{in} = \beta \cdot i_b$$

**Step 4:** The output voltage appears across the parallel combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$:
$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) = -g_m \cdot v_{in} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 5:** Form the voltage gain ratio:
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 6:** Express in decibel form:
$$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left[\vert g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) \vert\right]$$

> [!NOTE] **Physical Interpretation:** The negative sign confirms the **180┬░ phase inversion** ŌĆö when the input base voltage rises, the collector current increases, causing a larger voltage drop across $R_C$, which lowers the collector voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier with Un-bypassed Emitter Resistor

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** With $C_E$ removed (open), the emitter is not grounded for AC signals. Apply KVL to the base-emitter loop:
$$v_{in} = i_b \cdot r_\pi + (i_b + i_c) \cdot R_E$$

**Step 2:** Since $i_c = g_m v_{be}$ and $v_{be} = i_b r_\pi$, we have $i_c = g_m r_\pi i_b = \beta i_b$. Substituting:
$$v_{in} = i_b r_\pi + (i_b + \beta i_b) R_E = i_b \left[r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E\right]$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $i_b$:
$$i_b = \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi + (1+\beta)R_E}$$

**Step 4:** Output voltage:
$$v_{out} = -i_c (R_C \parallel R_L) = -\beta i_b (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $i_b$:
$$v_{out} = \frac{-\beta (R_C \parallel R_L) \cdot v_{in}}{r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E}$$

**Step 6:** Voltage gain:
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-\beta (R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi + (1+\beta)R_E}$$

**Step 7:** Using the approximation $r_\pi + (1+\beta)R_E \approx (1+\beta)(r_e + R_E)$ where $r_e = \dfrac{1}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E}$:
$$A_v \approx \frac{-(R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_e + R_E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight:** The unbypassed $R_E$ introduces **negative feedback**, stabilizing the gain against variations in $\beta$ and temperature, but reduces the magnitude of the gain. The factor $(1+\beta)R_E$ on the denominator is sometimes called the "reflected emitter resistance."

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Gain of Common-Source (CS) FET Amplifier

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** In the CS configuration, the gate draws no DC current ($I_G = 0$), so the input impedance is essentially infinite. Apply input voltage $v_{in} = v_{gs}$ at the gate.

**Step 2:** The drain current in the small-signal model is:
$$i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs} = g_m \cdot v_{in}$$

**Step 3:** The output voltage across the parallel combination of $R_D$ and $R_L$:
$$v_{out} = -i_d \cdot (R_D \parallel R_L) = -g_m \cdot v_{in} \cdot (R_D \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 4:** Voltage gain:
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 5:** Power gain (since input current is virtually zero, current gain is infinite in the ideal case):
$$A_p = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{v_{out}^2 / R_L}{v_{in}^2 / R_{in}} = A_v^2 \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_L}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Solving a KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A CE amplifier has $g_m = 50 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. The emitter is fully bypassed. Calculate (a) the voltage gain, (b) the voltage gain in dB, and (c) the power gain if input resistance $R_{in} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Voltage Gain:**
$$A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -50 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (4700 \parallel 10000)$$
$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4700 \times 10000}{4700 + 10000} = \frac{47 \times 10^6}{14700} \approx 3197 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$A_v = -0.050 \times 3197 \approx -159.87 \approx -160$$

**(b) Voltage Gain in dB:**
$$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(160) = 20 \times 2.204 = 44.08 \text{ dB}$$

**(c) Power Gain:**
$$A_p = A_v^2 \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_L} = (160)^2 \cdot \frac{2000}{10000} = 25600 \times 0.2 = 5120$$
$$A_p(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(5120) = 10 \times 3.709 = 37.09 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Gain Across Configurations

| **Parameter** | **CE** | **CB** | **CC** | **CS** | **CD** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Voltage Gain Magnitude | High | High | $\approx 1$ | High | $\approx 1$ |
| Current Gain Magnitude | $\beta$ | $\approx 1$ | $\beta+1$ | Infinite (ideal) | Infinite (ideal) |
| Power Gain | Very High | Moderate | Moderate | High | Low |
| Input Impedance | Medium | Low | High | Very High | Very High |
| Output Impedance | High | Very High | Low | High | Low |
| Phase Inversion | Yes (180┬░) | No (0┬░) | No (0┬░) | Yes (180┬░) | No (0┬░) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Amplifier with Gain Stages

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the gain concept integrates into a multi-stage amplifier system, mapping the flow of signal amplification from source to load.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcSignal[Input Source] --> biasCircuit[Biasing Network]
    biasCircuit --> couplingC1[Coupling Capacitor C1]
    couplingC1 --> stage1[Stage 1 BJT CE Amplifier]
    stage1 --> interstage[Interstage Coupling C2]
    interstage --> stage2[Stage 2 BJT CE Amplifier]
    stage2 --> couplingC3[Coupling Capacitor C3]
    couplingC3 --> powerStage[Power Amplifier CC]
    powerStage --> load[Load Speaker R L]
    
    stage1 --- gainNote1[A v1 negative feedback]
    stage2 --- gainNote2[A v2 voltage gain]
    powerStage --- gainNote3[Unity voltage gain high current]
    
    style srcSignal fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style stage1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style stage2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style powerStage fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow Diagram: Input vs Output Phasor Relationship

This diagram maps the conceptual flow from input phasor to output phasor, highlighting the gain magnitude, phase shift, and decibel conversion.

```mermaid
graph TD
    vin[Input Phasor V in] --> smallSig[Small Signal Model]
    smallSig --> transistor[Active Device BJT or FET]
    transistor --> outputNode[Output Node V out]
    outputNode --> gainCalc[Gain Calculation]
    gainCalc --> av[A v equals V out over V in]
    gainCalc --> ai[A i equals I out over I in]
    gainCalc --> ap[A p equals A v times A i]
    av --> dbConv[Convert to Decibels]
    ai --> dbConv
    ap --> dbConv
    dbConv --> final[Final Gain Specification in dB]
    
    style vin fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style final fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000
    style av fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style ai fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style ap fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style dbConv fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Selecting the Right Amplifier Configuration Based on Gain Requirements

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start Gain Requirement] --> q1{Need voltage gain above 100?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Need phase inversion?}
    q2 -->|Yes| ceSelect[Select CE BJT or CS FET]
    q2 -->|No| cbSelect[Select CB BJT for high frequency]
    q1 -->|No| q3{Need current gain above 50?}
    q3 -->|Yes| ccSelect[Select CC Emitter Follower]
    q3 -->|No| bufferSelect[Select CD Source Follower Buffer]
    ceSelect --> end1[Apply A v equals negative g m times R C parallel R L]
    cbSelect --> end2[Apply A v equals g m times R C parallel R L]
    ccSelect --> end3[Apply A v approximately 1]
    bufferSelect --> end4[Apply A v approximately 1]

    style ceSelect fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style cbSelect fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style ccSelect fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000
    style bufferSelect fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000
```
