# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of an op-amp. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**CMRR** is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode voltage gain ($A_d$) to the common-mode voltage gain ($A_{cm}$) of an op-amp. It quantifies the op-amp's ability to reject common-mode signals (such as noise) while amplifying differential signals.

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

A higher CMRR indicates better noise rejection. For an ideal op-amp, $A_{cm} = 0$, so CMRR = $\infty$ dB. For a practical op-amp like the $\mu$A741, CMRR is typically **90ŌĆō100 dB**.

### Question 2: What is the difference between differential mode and common mode input signals? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Differential Mode Signal | Common Mode Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Difference between the two input voltages | Average of the two input voltages |
| **Formula** | $v_d = v_2 - v_1$ | $v_c = (v_1 + v_2)/2$ |
| **Nature** | Desired signal to be amplified | Unwanted noise/interference |
| **Ideal Op-Amp Response** | Amplified by $A_d$ | Rejected (multiplied by 0) |

The differential mode represents the **useful information**, while the common mode represents **noise** that should be suppressed. A real op-amp amplifies the differential mode by $A_d$ and leaks some common-mode signal by $A_{cm}$, with the ratio $A_d/A_{cm}$ giving the CMRR.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for differential mode voltage ($v_d$) and common mode voltage ($v_c$) in terms of the two input voltages $v_1$ and $v_2$ applied to an op-amp. Explain the physical significance of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has a differential mode gain of $A_d = 4 \times 10^4$ and a common mode gain of $A_{cm} = 0.2$. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The CMRR in linear scale and in dB.
- (ii) The output voltage if the inputs are $v_1 = 2.48$ V and $v_2 = 2.52$ V.
- (iii) The common-mode output voltage component.
- (iv) The percentage of the output due to common-mode leakage.

### Question A ŌĆö Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of $v_d$ and $v_c$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definitions.**
The differential mode input voltage is defined as the **difference** between the non-inverting and inverting input voltages:

$$v_d = v_2 - v_1 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

The common mode input voltage is defined as the **average** of the two input voltages:

$$v_c = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solving for $v_1$ and $v_2$.**
Adding equations (1) and twice equation (2):

$$v_d + 2v_c = (v_2 - v_1) + (v_1 + v_2) = 2v_2$$

$$v_2 = v_c + \frac{v_d}{2}$$

Similarly:

$$v_1 = v_c - \frac{v_d}{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Physical Significance.**

> **Differential mode voltage $v_d$:** This represents the **useful signal** that the op-amp is designed to amplify. It is the component that carries the information content (e.g., the heart's electrical activity in an ECG, the strain in a load cell).

> **Common mode voltage $v_c$:** This represents the **common offset or noise** that appears equally on both input terminals (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum picked up by both sensor leads). An ideal op-amp should reject this completely; a real op-amp should reject it as much as possible.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation

**Given:** $A_d = 4 \times 10^4$, $A_{cm} = 0.2$, $v_1 = 2.48$ V, $v_2 = 2.52$ V.

**(i) CMRR Calculation:**

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = \frac{4 \times 10^4}{0.2} = 2 \times 10^5$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) = 20[\log_{10}(2) + 5] = 20[0.301 + 5] = 106.02 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Output Voltage:**

First, compute $v_d$ and $v_c$:

$$v_d = v_2 - v_1 = 2.52 - 2.48 = 0.04 \text{ V} = 40 \text{ mV}$$

$$v_c = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} = \frac{2.48 + 2.52}{2} = \frac{5.00}{2} = 2.50 \text{ V}$$

Now apply the output equation:

$$v_o = A_d \cdot v_d + A_{cm} \cdot v_c$$

$$v_o = (4 \times 10^4)(0.04) + (0.2)(2.50)$$

$$v_o = 1600 + 0.5 = 1600.5 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Common-Mode Output Component:**

$$v_{o,cm} = A_{cm} \cdot v_c = 0.2 \times 2.50 = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Percentage of CM Leakage:**

$$\% \text{ CM leakage} = \frac{v_{o,cm}}{v_o} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{1600.5} \times 100 \approx 0.0312\%$$

This is an excellent result, confirming that even with a significant common-mode input (2.5 V vs. 40 mV differential), the op-amp amplifies the differential signal cleanly with negligible noise contamination.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an op-amp in differential and common mode configurations. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of $A_d$, $A_{cm}$, $v_1$, and $v_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a particular op-amp, the differential gain is $1.5 \times 10^5$ and the common mode gain is $0.5$. If $v_1 = 5.005$ V and $v_2 = 5.015$ V, determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The CMRR in dB.
- (ii) The differential and common mode input voltages.
- (iii) The output voltage.
- (iv) Comment on the result if the op-amp is replaced by an ideal op-amp.

### Question B ŌĆö Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Block Diagram and Derivation

**Block Diagram:**

```
   v_1 (inverting)   ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
                           Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║  ╬Ż  ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [A_d block] ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
   v_2 (non-inverting) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś                          Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ ╬Ż ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ v_o
                                                       Ōöé
                          ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [A_cm block] ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

**Formal Derivation:**

The op-amp output can be modeled as the superposition of two effects:

1. **Effect of differential input:** When $v_d = v_2 - v_1$ is applied with $v_c = 0$, the output is $A_d \cdot v_d$.

2. **Effect of common-mode input:** When $v_c = (v_1 + v_2)/2$ is applied with $v_d = 0$, the output is $A_{cm} \cdot v_c$.

By superposition:

$$v_o = A_d \cdot v_d + A_{cm} \cdot v_c$$

Substituting the definitions:

$$v_o = A_d(v_2 - v_1) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right)$$

This is the **generalized output equation** of a real op-amp.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation

**Given:** $A_d = 1.5 \times 10^5$, $A_{cm} = 0.5$, $v_1 = 5.005$ V, $v_2 = 5.015$ V.

**(i) CMRR in dB:**

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{0.5} = 3 \times 10^5$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(3 \times 10^5) = 20[\log_{10}(3) + 5] = 20[0.477 + 5] = 109.54 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Differential and Common Mode Input Voltages:**

$$v_d = v_2 - v_1 = 5.015 - 5.005 = 0.010 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ mV}$$

$$v_c = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} = \frac{5.005 + 5.015}{2} = \frac{10.020}{2} = 5.010 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Output Voltage:**

$$v_o = A_d \cdot v_d + A_{cm} \cdot v_c$$

$$v_o = (1.5 \times 10^5)(0.010) + (0.5)(5.010)$$

$$v_o = 1500 + 2.505 = 1502.505 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Ideal Op-Amp Comparison:**

For an **ideal op-amp**, $A_{cm} = 0$, so:

$$v_{o,\text{ideal}} = A_d \cdot v_d + 0 \cdot v_c = 1500 \text{ V}$$

The **error** introduced by the real op-amp is:

$$\text{Error} = v_o - v_{o,\text{ideal}} = 1502.505 - 1500 = 2.505 \text{ V}$$

This error (2.505 V) is due to common-mode leakage. As a percentage:

$$\% \text{ error} = \frac{2.505}{1502.505} \times 100 \approx 0.167\%$$

Even though the common-mode input (5.01 V) is **501 times larger** than the differential input (10 mV), the differential signal dominates the output by a factor of $\sim 3 \times 10^5$ (the CMRR), demonstrating the op-amp's excellent noise rejection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $v_c$:** Many students write $v_c = v_1 + v_2$ instead of $v_c = (v_1 + v_2)/2$. This is a **3-mark deduction** in typical KTU valuation. Always remember: common mode is the **average**, not the sum.
> 
> 2. **Using $10 \log_{10}$ instead of $20 \log_{10}$ for CMRR:** CMRR involves **voltage gains**, not power gains. Voltage ratios use $20 \log_{10}$, power ratios use $10 \log_{10}$. Using the wrong formula loses 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Not specifying units in the final answer:** "CMRR = 106" is **incomplete** ŌĆö always write "CMRR = 106 dB" or "CMRR = $2 \times 10^5$ (linear)". Missing units = 1 mark lost.
> 
> 4. **Confusing differential gain with closed-loop gain:** The op-amp's $A_d$ in this context is the **open-loop differential gain** (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$). If the problem mentions feedback, you must distinguish between $A_d$ (open-loop) and the closed-loop gain set by the feedback network.
> 
> 5. **Sign errors in $v_d$:** Always verify: $v_d = v_2 - v_1$ (non-inverting minus inverting), **not** the reverse. Getting the sign wrong propagates through the entire problem.
> 
> 6. **Not verifying decomposition:** After calculating $v_d$ and $v_c$, always substitute back to verify $v_1 = v_c - v_d/2$ and $v_2 = v_c + v_d/2$. This is a **2-mark insurance** that catches algebra errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Differential and Common Mode Operation of Op-Amps**
> 
> **Core Definitions:**
> - Ō£ģ $v_d = v_2 - v_1$ (differential input voltage)
> - Ō£ģ $v_c = (v_1 + v_2)/2$ (common-mode input voltage)
> - Ō£ģ $A_d$ = differential-mode (open-loop) gain, typically $10^4$ to $10^6$
> - Ō£ģ $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain, typically $0.1$ to $10$
> 
> **Master Equation:**
> - Ō£ģ $v_o = A_d \cdot v_d + A_{cm} \cdot v_c$ ŌĆö the **single most important equation** for this topic
> - Ō£ģ Equivalent form: $v_o = A_d(v_2 - v_1) + A_{cm}[(v_1 + v_2)/2]$
> 
> **CMRR:**
> - Ō£ģ $\text{CMRR} = A_d / A_{cm}$ (linear, dimensionless)
> - Ō£ģ $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$ (in decibels)
> - Ō£ģ Ideal op-amp: CMRR = $\infty$ dB
> - Ō£ģ Practical op-amp (741): ~90ŌĆō100 dB
> - Ō£ģ Precision op-amp (OP-07): ~120ŌĆō140 dB
> 
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - Ō£ģ Differential mode = **useful signal** (amplify it)
> - Ō£ģ Common mode = **noise/interference** (reject it)
> - Ō£ģ Higher CMRR = better noise rejection
> 
> **Decomposition Verification:**
> - Ō£ģ $v_1 = v_c - v_d/2$
> - Ō£ģ $v_2 = v_c + v_d/2$
> 
> **Common Exam Pitfalls:**
> - ŌØī Using $v_c = v_1 + v_2$ (wrong ŌĆö must be average)
> - ŌØī Using $10 \log$ instead of $20 \log$ for voltage CMRR
> - ŌØī Forgetting units (dB vs. linear)
> - ŌØī Sign error in $v_d = v_2 - v_1$
> 
> **Real-World Applications to Remember:**
> - ­¤ö¦ Instrumentation amplifiers (ECG, EEG)
> - ­¤ö¦ Balanced audio transmission (XLR cables)
> - ­¤ö¦ RS-485 industrial communication
> - ­¤ö¦ Bridge sensor readout (strain gauges, load cells)
> - ­¤ö¦ Active filter design (CM noise suppression)
> 
> **Key Insight for Exams:**
> - ­¤ÆĪ When $v_1$ and $v_2$ are very close in value (small $v_d$, large $v_c$), the problem is testing your understanding of CMRR ŌĆö show that even with huge common-mode signals, the differential output dominates when CMRR is high.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ö¼ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inverting Amplifier ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An **Inverting Amplifier** is a closed-loop operational amplifier configuration in which the input signal is applied to the **inverting terminal (ŌłÆ)** through an input resistor $R_1$, and a feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting terminal. The non-inverting terminal (+) is grounded. The output voltage is an **amplified, phase-inverted (180┬░)** replica of the input signal.

**Circuit Topology (Canonical Form):**
The op-amp is operated in the **negative feedback** region using two external precision resistors ($R_1$ and $R_f$). Under ideal op-amp assumptions, the closed-loop voltage gain is determined *exclusively* by the ratio of these two passive components.

**Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants):**

| Parameter | Ideal Value | Practical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$) | $\infty$ | $10^5$ to $10^6$ |
| Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$) | $R_1$ | $\approx R_1$ |
| Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) | **0 ╬®** | $< 1 \text{ ╬®}$ |
| Bandwidth | Infinite | Limited by Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) |

---

## 1.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A **Non-Inverting Amplifier** is a closed-loop op-amp configuration in which the input signal is applied directly to the **non-inverting terminal (+)**, while the inverting terminal (ŌłÆ) is connected to a voltage divider ($R_1$, $R_f$) formed between the output node and ground. The output voltage is an **amplified, in-phase (0┬░)** version of the input.

**Special Case ŌĆö Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer):**
When $R_f = 0 \, \Omega$ and $R_1 = \infty \, \Omega$ (open), the gain collapses to exactly **unity (1)**, producing a high-impedance input / low-impedance output buffer.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition ­¤Ä»

**Imagine a Public Address (PA) System Microphone Setup:**

­¤Äż **The Inverting Amplifier** is like shouting into a **tube that flips the sound upside-down** before it reaches the speaker. Push the diaphragm *in*, and the air comes out *pulled back*. The tube has a *resistance* (friction) and a *feedback spring* that controls how much amplification happens. More spring tension ($R_f$) relative to friction ($R_1$) = louder (but still inverted) sound.

­¤ÄÖ’ĖÅ **The Non-Inverting Amplifier** is like a **megaphone**. Sound goes in one end and comes out the same direction ŌĆö *louder* but **not flipped**. The megaphone is naturally "non-inverting" because the wavefront simply gets compressed and amplified in its original orientation.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** 
> **I**nverting = **I**nput goes to the **I**nverting pin ŌåÆ Output is **I**nverted (negative sign)
> **N**on-Inverting = **N**o flip ŌåÆ Output stays **N**ormal (positive sign, no minus)

---

## 1.4 Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (KTU-Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 derivations, the following **FIVE** ideal op-amp conditions are mandatory:
> 1. **Infinite Open-Loop Gain:** $A_{OL} = \infty$
> 2. **Infinite Input Impedance:** $Z_{in} = \infty$ ŌåÆ Hence $I_+ = I_- = 0$
> 3. **Zero Output Impedance:** $Z_{out} = 0 \, \Omega$
> 4. **Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** CMRR = $\infty$
> 5. **Virtual Short Concept:** $V_+ = V_-$ (differential input is forced to zero)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōÉ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Virtual Ground Concept (Inverting Amplifier)

In the inverting configuration, the non-inverting terminal is tied directly to ground, so $V_+ = 0$. By the **virtual short principle**, $V_- = V_+ = 0$. This node is **not physically grounded**, but behaves as if it were ŌĆö hence the term *"virtual ground"*.

## 2.2 The Virtual Short Concept (Non-Inverting Amplifier)

In the non-inverting configuration, no current flows into the op-amp inputs. The voltage at the inverting terminal is set entirely by the resistor divider ($R_1, R_f$) connected to the output. The op-amp forces $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$ through negative feedback.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### ­¤ö╣ Inverting Amplifier Logic Flow:
- **Step 1:** Input $V_{in}$ applied to node $V_-$ through $R_1$.
- **Step 2:** Since $V_+ = 0$ (grounded) ŌåÆ Virtual Ground forces $V_- = 0$.
- **Step 3:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node: current through $R_1$ = current through $R_f$.
- **Step 4:** Output $V_{out}$ is forced by feedback to satisfy the KCL equation ŌåÆ $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$.
- **Step 5:** Negative sign indicates **180┬░ phase inversion**.

### ­¤ö╣ Non-Inverting Amplifier Logic Flow:
- **Step 1:** Input $V_{in}$ applied directly to $V_+$ terminal.
- **Step 2:** Virtual short: $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$.
- **Step 3:** The resistor pair $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (to output) form a **voltage divider** at node $V_-$.
- **Step 4:** Equate divider voltage to $V_{in}$: $V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$.
- **Step 5:** Solve for $V_{out}$: $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table completely ŌĆö these formulas carry 70% of KTU marks for this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closed-Loop Gain ($A_v$) | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ |
| 2 | Phase Shift ($\phi$) | $-180┬░$ | $0┬░$ (in phase) |
| 3 | Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$) | $R_1$ | $\infty$ (ideally) |
| 4 | Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ |
| 5 | Inverting Node Voltage | $V_- = 0$ (Virtual Ground) | $V_- = V_{in}$ (Virtual Short) |
| 6 | Bias Compensation Resistor | $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$ | $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$ |
| 7 | 3-dB Bandwidth ($f_H$) | $\dfrac{\text{GBP}}{\vert A_v \vert}$ | $\dfrac{\text{GBP}}{1 + R_f/R_1}$ |
| 8 | Voltage Follower Special Case | Not applicable | $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty \Rightarrow A_v = 1$ |
| 9 | Common-Mode Voltage | $V_{CM} = 0$ | $V_{CM} = V_{in}$ (susceptible) |
| 10 | KCL Equation at $V_-$ | $\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$ | $\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ In the table above, all absolute-value expressions use the keyword **`vert`** to preserve LaTeX rendering ŌĆö **never use the raw pipe `|` symbol** as it breaks markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Configuration Used | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Audio Mixing Consoles** | Inverting Summer | Multiple input summation with phase inversion |
| **Active Filters (LPF/HPF)** | Inverting Topology | Easy pole placement via $R_f / R_1$ ratio |
| **Sensor Signal Conditioning** | Non-Inverting | High input impedance prevents sensor loading |
| **Impedance Matching Buffers** | Voltage Follower | $Z_{in} \to \infty$, $Z_{out} \to 0$ |
| **Precision Rectifiers** | Inverting | Overcomes diode forward voltage drop |
| **ECG / Biopotential Amplifiers** | Non-Inverting (Instrumentation) | High CMRR, no signal inversion required |
| **Function Generators (Sine)** | Inverting | Wien Bridge + non-inverting amp is common |
| **Current-to-Voltage Converters (TIA)** | Inverting | Photodiode transimpedance amplification |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤¦« SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

**Given Circuit:** Op-amp with $V_{in}$ connected to inverting terminal via $R_1$, feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting terminal, and non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Assumptions (Ideal Op-Amp):**
- $A_{OL} = \infty$
- $I_+ = I_- = 0$ (infinite input impedance)
- $V_+ = V_-$ (virtual short)

**Step 1:** Identify the non-inverting input voltage.
$$V_+ = 0 \text{ V (tied to ground)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the virtual short principle to find the inverting node voltage.
$$V_- = V_+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

This is the **virtual ground** ŌĆö a node at 0 V that is not physically connected to ground.

**Step 3:** Apply KCL at the inverting node. Sum of currents entering the node = 0.

$$I_{R_1} + I_{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Express each current using Ohm's Law ($I = \frac{V}{R}$). Current through $R_1$ flows from $V_{in}$ to $V_-$:
$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

Current through $R_f$ flows from $V_{out}$ to $V_-$:
$$I_{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = \frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the KCL equation.
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Isolate $V_{out}$ on one side.
$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply both sides by $R_f$.
$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 8:** Define the closed-loop voltage gain as $A_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$.
$$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The **negative sign** confirms the 180┬░ phase inversion characteristic of the inverting amplifier.

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Non-Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

**Given Circuit:** Op-amp with $V_{in}$ connected to non-inverting terminal, $R_1$ from inverting terminal to ground, and $R_f$ from inverting terminal to output.

**Assumptions (Ideal Op-Amp):** Same as above.

**Step 1:** Identify the non-inverting input voltage.
$$V_+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the virtual short principle.
$$V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 3:** Observe that the inverting node is the midpoint of a voltage divider formed by $R_1$ and $R_f$ between $V_{out}$ and ground. Since **no current flows into the op-amp input**, the divider is unloaded.

$$V_- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $V_-$ (virtual short and divider).
$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the ratio $V_{out}/V_{in}$.
$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1}$$

**Step 6:** Split the fraction.
$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_1}{R_1} + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Step 7:** Define the closed-loop gain.
$$\boxed{A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The **positive sign** (and the constant offset of 1) confirms the non-inverting, in-phase output characteristic.

---

## 3.3 Special Case: Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer)

**Condition:** For the non-inverting amplifier, let $R_f \to 0$ (short circuit) and $R_1 \to \infty$ (open circuit).

**Substitute into the gain equation:**
$$A_v = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1 + 0 = 1$$

**Therefore:**
$$V_{out} = V_{in}$$

> [!TIP]
> The voltage follower provides **infinite input impedance** and **zero output impedance**, making it the **ideal buffer** for connecting a high-impedance source to a low-impedance load without signal loss.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** Design an inverting amplifier with a gain of $-25$ using a feedback resistor of $100 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate the input resistor, output voltage for $V_{in} = 0.4 \text{ V}$, and bandwidth if GBP = $1 \text{ MHz}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** From the gain formula $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$, solve for $R_1$:
$$R_1 = -\frac{R_f}{A_v} = -\frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{-25} = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Calculate output voltage:
$$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = -25 \times 0.4 \text{ V} = -10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the 3-dB bandwidth:
$$f_H = \frac{\text{GBP}}{\vert A_v \vert} = \frac{1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{25} = 40 \text{ kHz}$$

**Final Answer:** $R_1 = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{out} = -10 \text{ V}$, Bandwidth $= 40 \text{ kHz}$.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Feature | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier |
|---|---|---|
| Input at terminal | Inverting (ŌłÆ) | Non-Inverting (+) |
| Gain expression | $-\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ |
| Can produce gain $< 1$? | Yes (when $R_f < R_1$) | No (always $\geq 1$) |
| Phase relationship | $180┬░$ inverted | $0┬░$ in-phase |
| Input impedance | $R_1$ (finite) | $\infty$ (ideally) |
| Common-mode rejection | Excellent (no CM voltage) | Limited ($V_{CM} = V_{in}$) |
| Virtual ground present? | Yes | No (virtual short only) |
| Used as integrator? | Yes (replace $R_f$ with capacitor) | No |
| Used as comparator precursor? | Yes | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤¢╝’ĖÅ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inverting Amplifier ŌĆö Circuit Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["VIN SOURCE"] --> B1["R1 INPUT RESISTOR"]
    B1 --> C1["NODE V- INVERTING INPUT"]
    C1 --> D1["RF FEEDBACK RESISTOR"]
    D1 --> E1["VOUT OUTPUT NODE"]
    C1 --> F1["OP-AMP TRIANGLE"]
    F1 --> E1
    G1["NON-INVERTING V+ TERMINAL"] --> F1
    H1["GROUND 0V"] --> G1
    I1["VIRTUAL GROUND POINT"] -.-> C1
    E1 --> J1["LOAD RL"]
    J1 --> H1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed line `-.->` between the **Virtual Ground Point** and the inverting node represents the *abstract concept* of virtual ground ŌĆö not a physical connection.

---

## 4.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier ŌĆö Circuit Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2["VIN SOURCE"] --> B2["NON-INVERTING V+ INPUT"]
    B2 --> C2["OP-AMP TRIANGLE"]
    C2 --> D2["VOUT OUTPUT NODE"]
    E2["GROUND 0V"] --> F2["R1 TO GROUND"]
    F2 --> G2["NODE V- INVERTING INPUT"]
    G2 --> C2
    H2["RF FEEDBACK"] --> G2
    H2 --> D2
    D2 --> I2["LOAD RL"]
    I2 --> E2
    J2["VIRTUAL SHORT V- = V+"] -.-> G2
    J2 -.-> B2
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Closed-Loop Feedback)

```mermaid
graph TD
    K1["INPUT SIGNAL VS"] --> K2["INPUT NETWORK R1 OR DIRECT"]
    K2 --> K3["SUMMING JUNCTION SIGMA"]
    K3 --> K4["ERROR AMPLIFIER OP-AMP A_OL"]
    K4 --> K5["OUTPUT STAGE VOUT"]
    K5 --> K6["FEEDBACK NETWORK BETA = R1 OR R1+RF DIVIDER"]
    K6 --> K3
    K3 -.-> K7["CL SUMMATION SUBTRACTS FEEDBACK FROM INPUT"]
    K4 -.-> K8["GAIN CONTROLLED BY BETA"]
    K5 --> K9["LOAD"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the role of negative feedback," draw this block diagram and label the feedback factor $\beta$. The closed-loop gain is $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta}$.

---

## 4.4 Voltage Follower (Unity Buffer) ŌĆö Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1["VIN"] --> M2["NON-INVERTING V+ INPUT"]
    M2 --> M3["OP-AMP A_OL = INFINITE"]
    M3 --> M4["VOUT NODE"]
    M4 --> M5["DIRECT WIRE FEEDBACK TO V-"]
    M5 --> M6["INVERTING INPUT V-"]
    M6 --> M3
    M4 --> M7["LOW IMPEDANCE LOAD"]
    M1 -.-> M8["HIGH IMPEDANCE SOURCE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **direct wire from output to inverting input** (i.e., $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$) forces $V_{out} = V_{in}$ exactly. This is the **highest-fidelity buffer** in analog design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤ōØ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Inverting Amplifier Gain Derivation** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q:** Derive the closed-loop voltage gain of an **inverting amplifier** using an ideal op-amp. State the assumption(s) used.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Assumptions: $A_{OL} = \infty$, $I_+ = I_- = 0$, $V_+ = V_-$.
- **(1 Mark)** Since $V_+ = 0$, virtual ground gives $V_- = 0$. Apply KCL at $V_-$: $\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$.
- **(1 Mark)** Final expression: $\boxed{A_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}}$ (negative sign indicates 180┬░ phase shift).

---

### **Question 2: Non-Inverting Amplifier ŌĆö Voltage Follower** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q:** What is a **voltage follower**? Why is its gain equal to unity?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Definition: A voltage follower is a non-inverting amplifier with output directly connected to the inverting input ($R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$).
- **(1 Mark)** Gain formula: $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \dfrac{0}{\infty} = 1$.
- **(1 Mark)** Application: Used as a **buffer** to connect high-impedance sources to low-impedance loads without loading effect.

---

## ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Inverting Amplifier ŌĆö Complete Analysis** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** Draw the circuit of an inverting amplifier using an op-amp. Derive an expression for its **closed-loop voltage gain**, **input impedance**, and **output impedance** under ideal conditions.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** For an inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, calculate:
(i) The closed-loop voltage gain
(ii) The output voltage if $V_{in} = 0.5 \text{ V}$
(iii) The 3-dB bandwidth if the gain-bandwidth product (GBP) is $1 \text{ MHz}$

---

### **Model Solution A(a):**

**Circuit Description (1 Mark):**
The circuit consists of an op-amp with input signal $V_{in}$ applied through resistor $R_1$ to the inverting terminal (ŌłÆ). A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output to the inverting terminal. The non-inverting terminal (+) is grounded.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**
Apply ideal op-amp conditions: $V_+ = V_-$ and $I_+ = I_- = 0$.

Since $V_+ = 0$ ŌåÆ $V_- = 0$ (virtual ground).

KCL at inverting node:
$$\frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Substituting $V_- = 0$:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Rearranging:
$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Input Impedance (0.5 Mark):**
Looking from the source, $Z_{in} = R_1$ (since the op-amp input draws no current).

**Output Impedance (0.5 Mark):**
$Z_{out} = 0 \, \Omega$ (ideal op-amp assumption ŌĆö independent of load).

---

### **Model Solution A(b):**

**Given:** $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 0.5 \text{ V}$, GBP $= 1 \text{ MHz}$.

**(i) Closed-Loop Gain (2 Marks):**
$$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -\frac{50 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} = -5$$

**(ii) Output Voltage (2.5 Marks):**
$$V_{out} = A_v \times V_{in} = -5 \times 0.5 \text{ V} = -2.5 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) 3-dB Bandwidth (2.5 Marks):**
$$f_H = \frac{\text{GBP}}{\vert A_v \vert} = \frac{1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{5} = 200 \text{ kHz}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $A_v = -5$ (ii) $V_{out} = -2.5 \text{ V}$ (iii) $f_H = 200 \text{ kHz}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Non-Inverting Amplifier ŌĆö Complete Analysis** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** Draw the circuit of a non-inverting amplifier using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its **closed-loop voltage gain**. Mention **two advantages** over the inverting configuration.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** A non-inverting amplifier has $R_1 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 18 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate:
(i) The voltage gain
(ii) The output voltage for $V_{in} = 0.3 \text{ V}$
(iii) Comment on the input impedance and phase relationship with input.

---

### **Model Solution B(a):**

**Circuit Description (1 Mark):**
The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal (+). The inverting terminal is connected to a voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (to output).

**Derivation (4 Marks):**
Using virtual short: $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$.

The voltage at $V_-$ from the divider (no current flows into op-amp input):
$$V_- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

Equating the two:
$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

Solving:
$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Two Advantages Over Inverting (2 Marks):**
1. **Infinite (very high) input impedance** ŌĆö does not load the source.
2. **No phase inversion** ŌĆö output is in-phase with input.
3. (Bonus) Gain is always $\geq 1$ ŌĆö useful for impedance buffering with voltage gain.

---

### **Model Solution B(b):**

**Given:** $R_1 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 18 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 0.3 \text{ V}$.

**(i) Voltage Gain (2 Marks):**
$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{18 \text{ k}\Omega}{2 \text{ k}\Omega} = 1 + 9 = 10$$

**(ii) Output Voltage (2.5 Marks):**
$$V_{out} = A_v \times V_{in} = 10 \times 0.3 \text{ V} = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Input Impedance & Phase (2.5 Marks):**
- **Input Impedance:** Ideally **infinite** ($Z_{in} = \infty \, \Omega$), so practically it is limited only by the op-amp's input bias network (typically $10^{12} \, \Omega$ for CMOS op-amps).
- **Phase Relationship:** Output is **in-phase (0┬░ shift)** with the input signal ŌĆö this is the defining characteristic of the non-inverting configuration.

**Final Answers:** (i) $A_v = 10$ (ii) $V_{out} = 3.0 \text{ V}$ (iii) $Z_{in} \to \infty$, $0┬░$ phase shift.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the inverting amplifier gain formula (results in **ŌłÆ1 mark** deduction).
> 2. **Confusing $Z_{in}$** of inverting ($R_1$) vs non-inverting ($\infty$) ŌĆö examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 3. **Using $R_1$ alone for the bias-compensation resistor** ŌĆö the correct value is $R_1 \parallel R_f$ at the *non-inverting* input to cancel the offset due to input bias currents.
> 4. **Wrong phase shift statement** ŌĆö non-inverting is **0┬░**, not 360┬░ or 180┬░. Do not write "no phase shift" without specifying the angle.
> 5. **Arithmetic errors** in numericals ŌĆö especially in bandwidth calculations where students often use $A_v$ without the absolute value.
> 6. **Missing virtual ground / virtual short statement** ŌĆö KTU examiners allot **at least 1 dedicated mark** for naming the concept.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** before the derivation ŌĆö this costs **0.5 to 1 mark** consistently.

---

## ­¤Ä» TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam:**

Ō£ģ **Inverting Amplifier:**
- Input at **inverting (ŌłÆ)** terminal via $R_1$; non-inverting (+) grounded.
- **Gain:** $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (negative sign = 180┬░ phase inversion).
- **Virtual Ground** at inverting node ($V_- = 0$).
- **Input Impedance** = $R_1$ (finite).
- **Can produce gain < 1** (attenuation) when $R_f < R_1$.

Ō£ģ **Non-Inverting Amplifier:**
- Input at **non-inverting (+)** terminal directly.
- **Gain:** $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (always $\geq 1$).
- **Virtual Short** at inverting node ($V_- = V_+$).
- **Input Impedance** = $\infty$ (ideal).
- **Voltage Follower** is the special case ($A_v = 1$).

Ō£ģ **Universal Concepts (Both Topologies):**
- Negative feedback forces $V_+ = V_-$.
- No current flows into op-amp inputs ($I_+ = I_- = 0$).
- Bias compensation resistor: $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$.
- Bandwidth: $f_H = \dfrac{\text{GBP}}{\vert A_v \vert}$ (Gain-Bandwidth Tradeoff).
- Output impedance $\approx 0 \, \Omega$ in both cases.

Ō£ģ **Engineering Insight for KTU Viva:**
- *Inverting* = used in **integrators, differentiators, summers, active filters**.
- *Non-Inverting* = used in **buffers, instrumentation pre-amplifiers, sensor interfaces**.
- *Voltage Follower* = used for **impedance matching and ADC driver circuits**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oscillators: Condition for Oscillations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oscillators: Condition for Oscillations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Oscillator:** An electronic circuit that converts DC energy from the power supply into AC energy in the form of periodic waveforms (sine, square, triangular, etc.) **without any external AC input signal**. The output frequency is determined entirely by the passive components used inside the feedback network.

A **feedback oscillator** is essentially an amplifier that supplies its own input through a frequency-selective positive feedback loop. The mathematical condition that the loop must satisfy in order to produce and sustain sinusoidal oscillations is known as the **Barkhausen Criterion**, named after the German physicist **Heinrich Georg Barkhausen (1881ŌĆō1956)**.

The criterion states two simultaneous conditions:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain around the closed feedback loop must be exactly **unity**.
2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift introduced by the loop must be an integer multiple of **$2\pi$ radians (360┬░)**.

In compact mathematical form:

$$|A\beta| = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real engineering practice, $|A\beta|$ is kept **slightly greater than 1** (typically $1.01$ to $1.1$) at switch-on so that the oscillations build up from thermal noise. Automatic gain control (AGC) or amplitude stabilization then pulls the loop gain back to exactly unity for steady, undistorted output.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Child on a Swing

Imagine pushing a child on a playground swing.

- **Without your push (no feedback):** Air resistance slowly damps the swing's motion and it eventually comes to rest. This is analogous to a passive LC tank whose natural oscillations decay due to internal resistance.
- **With a perfectly timed push (positive feedback, correct phase):** Each push arrives exactly when the swing reaches its highest point and starts coming back. Even a small push adds energy and the swing rises higher.
- **If the push is too small (loop gain $< 1$):** Oscillations die out ŌĆö under-damped.
- **If the push is too large (loop gain $> 1$):** Oscillations grow indefinitely until something saturates ŌĆö over-driven.
- **If the push is timed correctly *and* has just the right strength (loop gain $= 1$, phase $= 0┬░$):** The swing oscillates forever at a constant amplitude ŌĆö **sustained oscillation**.

The Barkhausen Criterion is exactly this "perfectly timed, perfectly sized push" rule applied to electronic feedback loops.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Symbol | Typical Magnitude / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Loop Gain | $A\beta$ | Dimensionless (real, $\geq 1$) |
| Phase Margin | $\phi_m$ | **30┬░ to 60┬░** for safe oscillator design |
| Frequency Stability | $\Delta f / f_0$ | **1 ppm to 100 ppm** for crystal oscillators |
| Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | $THD$ | $< 1\%$ for high-quality sine oscillators |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Why can we not simply set $|A\beta| = 1$ exactly in theory?" ŌĆö the answer lies in temperature drift, component tolerances, and transistor parameter variation. Designers therefore use **non-linear amplitude stabilization** (thermistor, diode, lamp) or **AGC** circuits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Oscillator Architecture

Every sinusoidal oscillator in the KTU syllabus (RC phase shift, Wien bridge, Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal) is a special case of the same general block diagram. This single block diagram is the *mother* of all oscillator analysis.

**Components of the loop:**

1. **Amplifier block (A):** Provides voltage (or power) gain. Usually a transistor in CE configuration, op-amp, or FET stage.
2. **Feedback network ($\beta$):** A passive, frequency-selective network (RC ladder, LC tank, quartz crystal) that decides the oscillation frequency.
3. **Summing junction:** Adds the external input $V_{in}$ with the feedback voltage $V_f = \beta V_{out}$.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step by Step

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial excitation:**
The circuit is switched on. Thermal noise in resistors and transistors provides a broadband random signal containing all frequencies.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency selection:**
The feedback network $\beta(j\omega)$ is sharply selective. It allows only **one frequency** $f_0$ to pass through with the correct amplitude and phase. All other frequencies are attenuated.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Positive feedback multiplication:**
At $f_0$, the feedback voltage $V_f$ is exactly in phase with the original noise component at the input. After passing through the amplifier of gain $A$, the output at $f_0$ is larger than the original noise.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Build-up stage:**
This larger output produces an even larger feedback signal, which produces an even larger output. The amplitude grows exponentially until the amplifier begins to **saturate** (or amplitude-limiting kicks in).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Steady state:**
Non-linear amplitude stabilization reduces the effective loop gain from slightly greater than 1 back to exactly 1. The circuit now produces a clean sinusoid at $f_0$ indefinitely.

---

## 2.3 Why Two Conditions Are Necessary (Not Just One)

| Condition Failure | What Happens Physically |
|---|---|
| $\|A\beta\| < 1$ | Signal dies out ŌĆö damped oscillation (like a bell struck lightly) |
| $\|A\beta\| > 1$ | Signal grows until clipping/distortion ŌĆö saturated square-ish wave |
| $\|A\beta\| = 1$ but phase $\neq 2n\pi$ | Output is not a pure sinusoid ŌĆö produces growing/decaying beat pattern |
| Both satisfied simultaneously | **Sustained, pure sinusoidal oscillation at $f_0$** |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$ | Closed-loop gain of feedback amplifier | Derivation starting point |
| $1 - A\beta = 0$ | Barkhausen magnitude condition (denominator zero) | Sustained oscillation |
| $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Magnitude condition (lossless loop) | All KTU oscillator problems |
| $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ | Phase condition (in-phase feedback) | All KTU oscillator problems |
| $A_{crit} = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | Critical amplifier gain required at $f_0$ | Design of RC / Wien / Phase-shift oscillators |
| $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC oscillator frequency (Colpitts / Hartley) | RF domain ($1$ kHz to $1$ GHz) |
| $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | RC oscillator frequency (Wien bridge, single RC) | AF domain ($1$ Hz to $1$ MHz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students write $|A\beta| \geq 1$ in answers. The strict **equality** is for *sustained* (steady-state) oscillations. The *inequality* $|A\beta| \geq 1$ describes the *transient build-up* phase. Examiners award full marks only for distinguishing between these two cases.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of Oscillator Principle |
|---|---|
| Telecommunications | Carrier generation in AM/FM transmitters, local oscillators in superheterodyne receivers |
| Digital Clocks | Quartz crystal oscillator (32.768 kHz for watches, MHz range for CPUs) |
| Instrumentation | Function generators, audio sine-wave testing, biomedical stimulators |
| Radar / Sonar | Pulsed microwave generation, Doppler shift detection |
| Power Electronics | Resonant converters, induction heating |
| Music Synthesis | Voltage-Controlled Oscillators (VCOs) in analog synthesizers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivation of the Barkhausen Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is **exam-critical**. Every KTU university paper on oscillators has either a direct "Derive the Barkhausen criterion" question (7ŌĆō10 marks) or expects it as the introductory step in a problem on RC / LC oscillator design.

---

## 3.1 Starting Point ŌĆö General Feedback Amplifier

Consider an amplifier with open-loop voltage gain $A$ and a feedback network with transfer function $\beta$. The external input is $V_{in}$ and the output is $V_{out}$.

```
        V_in   (+)    +----------+    V_out
        ------>(+)---->          |------------>
                 ^    |   AMP    |
                 |    | (Gain A) |
                 |    +----------+
                 |         |
                 |         v
                 |       [Beta] <-- feedback network
                 |         |
                 +----<----+
```

Let the voltage sampled from the output and fed back through the network be:

$$V_f = \beta V_{out}$$

The voltage appearing at the input of the amplifier itself (call it $V_{id}$, the *difference input*) is:

$$V_{id} = V_{in} + V_f = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$$

(The "+" sign indicates **positive feedback** ŌĆö required for oscillation, as opposed to the "$-$" sign used in ordinary negative feedback amplifiers.)

---

## 3.2 Write the Output Equation

By definition of the amplifier gain $A$:

$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{id}$$

Substituting $V_{id}$:

$$V_{out} = A \left( V_{in} + \beta V_{out} \right)$$

---

## 3.3 Algebraic Isolation of $V_{out}$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$V_{out} = A V_{in} + A \beta V_{out}$$

Move the second term on the right over to the left-hand side:

$$V_{out} - A \beta V_{out} = A V_{in}$$

Factor $V_{out}$ on the left:

$$V_{out} \left( 1 - A\beta \right) = A V_{in}$$

Therefore, the **closed-loop gain** $A_f$ is:

$$A_f = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

---

## 3.4 Apply the Oscillator Condition

An oscillator has **no external input**; the output sustains itself purely from the feedback signal. Mathematically, this corresponds to demanding a finite, non-zero $V_{out}$ even when $V_{in} = 0$.

Setting $V_{in} = 0$ in the closed-loop equation gives:

$$V_{out} \left( 1 - A\beta \right) = 0$$

For a *non-trivial* solution ($V_{out} \neq 0$), the bracket must vanish:

$$1 - A\beta = 0$$

This is the **Barkhausen magnitude condition**, i.e.,

$$A\beta = 1$$

Taking the magnitude and phase separately:

$$|A\beta| = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle(A\beta) = 0┬░ + n \cdot 360┬░$$

Written more generally:

$$|A\beta| = 1, \quad \angle A\beta = 2n\pi \text{ rad}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

---

## 3.5 Physical Interpretation of the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase condition** $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ ensures the feedback voltage $V_f$ is in **exact phase** with the original input disturbance. If there is any phase error, successive cycles partially cancel each other and oscillations cannot build.
>
> **Magnitude condition** $|A\beta| = 1$ ensures each round-trip around the loop neither amplifies nor attenuates the signal. Together, the loop replicates its own input exactly ŌĆö self-sustaining oscillation.

---

## 3.6 Starting-Up vs Sustained Condition ŌĆö The Engineering Subtlety

In practical design:

| Phase | Condition | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Switch-on (build-up)** | $\|A\beta\| > 1$ | Amplitude must grow from microscopic thermal noise to usable level |
| **Steady state** | $\|A\beta\| = 1$ | Amplitude-limiting element (lamp, diode, thermistor) reduces effective gain to exactly unity |
| **Design guideline** | $\|A\beta\|$ around $1.01$ to $1.05$ | Compromise between fast start-up and low distortion |

This is why KTU problems often give you a slight margin in component values ŌĆö the strict equality is the *idealized* result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Feedback Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Thermal Noise Source] --> sumA((Summing Junction))
    sumA --> ampA[Amplifier Block - Gain A]
    ampA --> outNodeA[Oscillator Output Vout]
    outNodeA --> fbA[Feedback Network - Transfer Function Beta]
    fbA --> sumA
    outNodeA -.feedback sample.-> fbA
    fbA -.returns Vf.-> sumA
    classDef amp fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef fb fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#00688B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef sum fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef src fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class ampA amp
    class fbA fb
    class sumA sum
    class srcA src
    class outNodeA out
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **pink summing node** represents the algebraic addition $V_{id} = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$.
- The **amber amplifier block** multiplies $V_{id}$ by the complex gain $A(j\omega)$.
- The **cyan feedback block** is the frequency-selective network whose transfer function $\beta(j\omega)$ peaks at the desired $f_0$.
- The **green output node** delivers the sustained sinusoid to the external load.

---

## 4.2 Loop Gain Decision Flow ŌĆö When Will It Oscillate?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Circuit Switched ON]) --> noiseA{Thermal Noise Excitation}
    noiseA --> freqA[Feedback Network Selects Frequency f0]
    freqA --> phaseA{Phase Condition angle AB equals 0 deg or 360 deg}
    phaseA -- No --> dampA[Oscillations Damp Out]
    phaseA -- Yes --> magA{Magnitude Condition absolute AB greater than 1}
    magA -- No --> dampA
    magA -- Yes --> buildA[Amplitude Builds Exponentially]
    buildA --> satA{Amplitude Limiting Active}
    satA -- No --> clipA[Output Clips - Distorted]
    satA -- Yes --> ssA[Sustained Oscillation at f0]
    classDef ok fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bad fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class startA start
    class dampA,clipA bad
    class ssA ok
    class buildA,satA,freqA,noiseA,phaseA,magA proc
```

This decision tree captures exactly the dual condition $|A\beta| = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$. Both must be satisfied; satisfying only one is insufficient.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

**State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer:**

The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained sinusoidal oscillations in a feedback oscillator, the following two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

**(i) Magnitude condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be exactly unity.

$$|A\beta| = 1$$

**(ii) Phase condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians (i.e., $0┬░$ or $360┬░$).

$$\angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

The first condition ensures that the signal magnitude is preserved on each round trip of the feedback loop. The second condition ensures that the feedback voltage is exactly in phase with the input disturbance, enabling positive reinforcement. **3 marks**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Why is $|A\beta|$ kept slightly greater than 1 at switch-on in practical oscillators?**

**Model Answer:**

At switch-on, the only signal available to start the oscillation is thermal noise ŌĆö a very weak, random fluctuation. If $|A\beta|$ were set to exactly 1, the noise would circulate around the loop without any growth and the oscillations would fail to reach a usable amplitude.

By designing the amplifier so that $|A\beta|$ is **slightly greater than 1** (typically $1.01$ to $1.05$), each cycle of feedback multiplies the noise by a factor greater than 1, leading to an **exponential build-up** of amplitude. Once the amplitude reaches the desired operating level, a non-linear amplitude-limiting element (diode, thermistor, incandescent lamp, or AGC circuit) reduces the effective loop gain back to exactly 1, ensuring sustained, low-distortion sinusoidal output. **3 marks**

---

## Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

### Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024, Model Paper]

#### (a) Derive the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations starting from the basic feedback amplifier equation. State clearly the magnitude and phase conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the block diagram:**
Consider an amplifier of gain $A$ with a feedback network of transfer function $\beta$. The output $V_{out}$ is sampled, fed back, and added to the external input $V_{in}$ at the summing junction.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the feedback voltage:**
The feedback voltage reaching the input is $V_f = \beta V_{out}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the difference input:**
For positive feedback (essential for oscillation), the input to the amplifier is:
$$V_{id} = V_{in} + V_f = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Write the amplifier output:**
By definition of gain $A$:
$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{id} = A \left( V_{in} + \beta V_{out} \right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Expand and collect terms:**
$$V_{out} = A V_{in} + A\beta V_{out}$$
$$V_{out} - A\beta V_{out} = A V_{in}$$
$$V_{out} \left( 1 - A\beta \right) = A V_{in}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the oscillator requirement:**
Since an oscillator has no external input, set $V_{in} = 0$:
$$V_{out} \left( 1 - A\beta \right) = 0$$

For a non-trivial (non-zero) output, the bracket must vanish:
$$1 - A\beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A\beta = 1$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Split into magnitude and phase:**
$$|A\beta| = 1, \quad \angle A\beta = 0┬░ + n \cdot 360┬░ \quad (n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots)$$

This is the **Barkhausen criterion**. The first equation is the *magnitude condition* (loop lossless). The second is the *phase condition* (in-phase feedback). **7 marks**

---

#### (b) Explain why both magnitude and phase conditions must be simultaneously satisfied. What happens if only one is satisfied? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Role of the phase condition:**
The phase condition $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ ensures that the feedback voltage $V_f$ arrives **in phase** with the original input disturbance. If this condition is violated, even a very large feedback signal would partially cancel the original signal in subsequent cycles. The net energy injection would be zero or negative, and oscillations would damp out.

*If only the phase condition holds but $|A\beta| < 1$*: The signal circulates with the correct phase but loses amplitude on each cycle. Result ŌĆö **damped oscillation**, analogous to a freely oscillating pendulum in air.

*If only the phase condition holds but $|A\beta| > 1$*: The signal circulates in phase but grows unbounded. The amplifier saturates, and the output becomes a clipped (square-ish) waveform with high harmonic distortion.

**(ii) Role of the magnitude condition:**
The magnitude condition $|A\beta| = 1$ ensures that the loop neither amplifies nor attenuates the circulating signal. Combined with the phase condition, the loop exactly reproduces its own input ŌĆö the definition of a **self-sustaining** oscillation.

*If only the magnitude condition holds but the phase is wrong*: The signal magnitude is preserved but successive samples arrive out of phase, causing destructive interference. Result ŌĆö **decaying or beat-pattern oscillation**, never a clean sinusoid.

**Conclusion:**
Only the **simultaneous satisfaction** of both conditions guarantees a sustained, undistorted sinusoid at the desired frequency $f_0$. The phase condition selects *which* frequency will oscillate; the magnitude condition ensures *sustainable* amplitude at that frequency. **7 marks**

---

### Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]

#### (a) With the help of a block diagram, derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a feedback amplifier and show that the condition for oscillation is $A\beta = 1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider the closed-loop system with an amplifier of gain $A$ in the forward path and a feedback network of transfer function $\beta$ in the return path.

The summing junction produces the error (difference) input:
$$V_{id} = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$$

The amplifier multiplies this by $A$ to give:
$$V_{out} = A V_{id} = A \left( V_{in} + \beta V_{out} \right)$$

Rearranging:
$$V_{out} - A\beta V_{out} = A V_{in}$$
$$V_{out} \left( 1 - A\beta \right) = A V_{in}$$

Therefore the **closed-loop gain** is:
$$A_f = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

For an oscillator, no external input exists ($V_{in} = 0$), yet we require a finite, non-zero $V_{out}$. From the equation $V_{out}(1 - A\beta) = 0$, the only way to obtain $V_{out} \neq 0$ is:
$$1 - A\beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A\beta = 1$$

This is the algebraic basis of the Barkhausen criterion. **7 marks**

---

#### (b) Discuss the practical design considerations for ensuring that $|A\beta| = 1$ is maintained despite temperature variations and component aging. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In real circuits, the gain $A$ of the active device (transistor / op-amp) drifts with **temperature**, **supply voltage**, and **device aging**. Without compensation, the loop gain may drift away from unity, either causing oscillations to die out ($|A\beta| < 1$) or causing distortion ($|A\beta| > 1$).

**Common amplitude-stabilization techniques:**

1. **Thermistor stabilization:** A temperature-dependent resistor in the feedback loop. When amplitude rises, current heats the thermistor, its resistance changes, and $\beta$ is adjusted to bring $|A\beta|$ back to 1.
2. **Incandescent lamp (Wien bridge classic):** A small tungsten lamp has a positive temperature coefficient. Higher amplitude $\Rightarrow$ higher current $\Rightarrow$ higher filament temperature $\Rightarrow$ higher resistance $\Rightarrow$ reduced gain.
3. **Diode / Zener limiter:** Back-to-back diodes across the gain-setting resistor clip the signal mildly, providing non-linear gain reduction at high amplitudes.
4. **FET as voltage-controlled resistor:** A JFET in the feedback path acts as a non-linear element whose resistance changes with the peak signal level, providing automatic gain control.
5. **AGC with op-amp envelope detector:** A precision electronic circuit that senses the output amplitude and adjusts the bias of the gain element accordingly.

**Design rule of thumb:** Design for a small loop gain margin (typically $|A\beta| \approx 1.05$ at switch-on) so that build-up is rapid, but ensure the amplitude-limiting element kicks in well before saturation. This yields low THD (typically $< 0.1\%$) and good frequency stability. **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing $|A\beta| \geq 1$ without distinguishing start-up vs steady-state:** Examiner deducts 1 mark. Always clarify that the strict equality is for *sustained* oscillation; build-up needs $> 1$.
> 2. **Forgetting the phase condition:** If you only write the magnitude condition, you lose half the marks for the criterion.
> 3. **Confusing negative and positive feedback:** Oscillators use **positive** feedback (note the "+" sign in $V_{id} = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$). Ordinary amplifiers use negative feedback.
> 4. **Missing the units on loop gain:** $|A\beta|$ is dimensionless, but examiners sometimes ask for it explicitly.
> 5. **Wrong starting assumption:** Do not start from $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ ŌĆö that is the **negative** feedback formula. Use the **positive** feedback version $A_f = A/(1-A\beta)$.
> 6. **Skipping the $V_{in} = 0$ step:** Many students write $1 - A\beta = 0$ without justifying *why* ŌĆö always explain that an oscillator has no external AC input.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Barkhausen Criterion** is the **necessary and sufficient** condition for sustained sinusoidal oscillation in any linear feedback oscillator.
- **Magnitude condition:** $|A\beta| = 1$ ŌĆö the loop loss is exactly compensated by the amplifier gain.
- **Phase condition:** $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ ŌĆö feedback must be in phase with the original disturbance.
- **Closed-loop gain formula:** $A_f = A / (1 - A\beta)$ ŌĆö derived from $V_{out} = A(V_{in} + \beta V_{out})$ with **positive** feedback sign.
- **Start-up vs steady-state:** Practical oscillators are designed with $|A\beta| > 1$ initially, then amplitude-limiting reduces it to exactly 1.
- **No external input:** Setting $V_{in} = 0$ in the closed-loop equation is the key step that produces the criterion.
- **All KTU oscillators** (Wien bridge, RC phase shift, Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal, Clapp) are special cases of the same loop diagram.
- **Frequency selection** is done by the feedback network $\beta(j\omega)$, not the amplifier.
- **Amplitude stabilization** is mandatory for low-distortion sinusoidal output ŌĆö common methods: thermistor, lamp, diode limiter, AGC.
- **High-Yield KTU phrases to memorize:** "Positive feedback", "Loop gain unity", "Zero phase shift around the loop", "Frequency-determining network".
- **For numerical problems:** Substitute the $\beta$ expression of the specific oscillator (RC ladder, LC tank, crystal equivalent) into $A\beta = 1$, separate real and imaginary parts, solve for both the required amplifier gain $A$ and the oscillation frequency $f_0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
